Tools: **PHASE, Schrödinger QSAR**. 2. **Molecular Docking Process:** *Receptor preparation → Ligand preparation → Grid generation → Docking → Scoring → Pose analysis.* The binding affinity is measured in **kcal/mol**; more negative values indicate stronger binding. 3. **Pharmacogenomics Examples:** - **Warfarin dosing:** Variants in *CYP2C9* and *VKORC1* genes affect drug metabolism and dose requirements. - **Trastuzumab (Herceptin):** Effective only in HER2-positive breast cancer patients. - **Abacavir hypersensitivity:** Strongly associated with *HLA-B*\*57:01 allele. 4. **COVID-19 Example:** AlphaFold predicted the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein structure within months, accelerating vaccine and therapeutic design. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Describe the **various applications of bioinformatics in medical, agricultural, and environmental biotechnology** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a detailed note on **protein structure prediction methods and their applications** in modern biology. Include a discussion of homology modelling, threading, and *ab initio* methods. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Bioinformatics in Medical, Agricultural, and Environmental Biotechnology (7 marks)** 1. **Medical Biotechnology:** - **Disease Gene Identification:** GWAS, WES/WGS identify mutations linked to cancer, diabetes, rare diseases. - **Diagnostic Kits:** NGS-based panels for BRCA1/2, tuberculosis drug-resistance detection. - **Vaccine Development:** Reverse vaccinology (Vaxign), mRNA vaccine design (Moderna, Pfizer-BioNTech). - **Personalized Medicine:** Pharmacogenomic testing before drug prescription (e.g., clopidogrel and *CYP2C19*). 2. **Agricultural Biotechnology:** - **Marker-Assisted Selection (MAS):** Uses SNP markers linked to desirable traits to speed up crop breeding. - **Genomic Selection:** Predicts breeding values of livestock using dense genome-wide markers. - **Stress Tolerance:** Identifying genes for drought, salinity, and pest resistance (e.g., *DREB* genes in rice). - **Transgenic and Genome-Editing Design:** Bioinformatics tools design guide RNAs for CRISPR-Cas systems (e.g., **CRISPOR, CHOPCHOP**). 3. **Environmental Biotechnology:** - **Metagenomics:** Characterizing microbial communities in soil, water, and host-associated microbiomes using tools like **QIIME2, MetaPhlAn, Kraken2**. - **Bioremediation Pathway Discovery:** Identifying microbial enzymes capable of degrading pollutants (PETases for plastic, laccases for dyes). - **Biosensors:** Engineered proteins and pathways monitored computationally for environmental surveillance. **(b) Protein Structure Prediction Methods (7 marks)** 1. **Why is structure important?** Function follows form. The 3D shape of a protein determines its ligand binding, catalytic activity, and interaction partners. 2. **Methods of Prediction:** - **Homology (Comparative) Modelling:** Builds a model for a target protein using an experimentally determined structure of a close homolog (template). Steps: *Template identification → Alignment → Model building (MODELLER, Swiss-Model) → Refinement → Validation (PROCHECK, Verify3D).* Most reliable when sequence identity > 30%. - **Threading (Fold Recognition):** Used when no close homolog exists. The target sequence is "threaded" through known 3D folds to find the best-fitting structural framework. Tools: **I-TASSER, RaptorX, MUSTER**. - **Ab Initio (De Novo) Modelling:** Predicts structure from sequence alone using physics-based energy functions and conformational sampling. Computationally expensive. Tools: **QUARK, Rosetta, AlphaFold2 (deep-learning based hybrid)**. 3. **AlphaFold2 Revolution:** AlphaFold2 (DeepMind, 2020) combined **attention-based deep learning**, **multiple sequence alignments**, and **geometric constraints** to predict structures with atomic accuracy, achieving median RMSD < 1.0 Å for most targets in CASP14 — a watershed moment in structural bioinformatics. 4. **Applications:** - **Drug target identification** for neglected tropical diseases. - **Enzyme engineering** for industrial biocatalysis. - **Understanding disease mutations** (e.g., p53 cancer mutants). - **Vaccine immunogen design** (e.g., stabilized RSV-F protein). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE** - **Definition:** Bioinformatics = computational methods for biological data. - **Seven major application domains:** (1) Sequence Analysis, (2) Structural Bioinformatics, (3) Genomics/Transcriptomics, (4) Proteomics, (5) Drug Discovery (CADD), (6) Phylogenetics, (7) Clinical/Translational/Agricultural Bioinformatics. - **Key formula — Sequencing coverage:** $C = NL / G$ (in fold). - **Key formula — RMSD:** $\text{RMSD} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum d_i^2}$ (in Å, lower is better). - **Key formula — JC69 substitution:** $P(t) = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{3}{4}e^{-4\alpha t}$. - **Key formula — Sequence identity:** $\% \text{ID} = (M/L) \times 100$. - **Key tools to remember:** BLAST, Clustal Omega, SPAdes, AUGUSTUS, AlphaFold2, AutoDock Vina, MEGA, QIIME2. - **Modern frontiers:** AlphaFold2, deep learning in genomics, multi-omics integration, single-cell bioinformatics, spatial transcriptomics, AI-driven drug discovery. - **Real-world impact examples:** SARS-CoV-2 genome tracking (GISAID), mRNA vaccine design, pharmacogenomic dosing (warfarin, clopidogrel), CRISPR guide-RNA design, cancer liquid biopsies, metagenomic microbiome profiling. > **Final Word:** When writing KTU ESE answers, **always structure with headings, bold keywords, named tools, and at least one quantitative or example-based element** — this is what separates a 10/14 answer from a 14/14 answer.

topics.title: Important Biological Databases: Retrieving and Analyzing Data from Biological Databases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBBBT604 - BIOINFORMATICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Important Biological Databases: Retrieving and Analyzing Data from Biological Databases # 📘 MODULE 1 — Important Biological Databases: Retrieving and Analyzing Data from Biological Databases ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > A **Biological Database** is a large, organized, persistent collection of biological information — such as nucleic acid sequences, protein sequences, macromolecular structures, gene expression profiles, metabolic pathways, and disease-associated genomic variants — that is stored electronically, indexed for rapid retrieval, and made publicly accessible through web-based query interfaces. The discipline of *retrieving* data refers to the systematic use of text-based and sequence-based query languages (e.g., **Entrez**, **SRS**, **EB-eye**) to fetch records; *analyzing* data refers to the application of computational tools such as **BLAST**, **ClustalW**, **EMBOSS**, and **Swiss-Prot curation pipelines** to extract biological meaning from the retrieved records. The three international nucleotide sequence repositories that synchronize data daily are: - **NCBI GenBank** (National Center for Biotechnology Information, USA) - **EMBL-EBI ENA** (European Nucleotide Archive, UK) - **DDBJ** (DNA Data Bank of Japan) Together they constitute the **International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration (INSDC)**, ensuring that every submitted sequence is mirrored across three continents. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Biology Library" > [!IMPORTANT] > Imagine a **giant public library** where books are not novels but **genes, proteins, diseases, and chemical compounds**. Each book has a unique accession number (like a barcode) so the librarian can find it instantly. *Retrieval* is when you walk up to the librarian and say *"Give me every book about the BRCA1 gene in humans"* — the librarian searches the catalog. *Analysis* is when you open the book and read it carefully to understand the storyline (function, mutation, structure). A *biological database* is the library, and *BLAST/Clustal* are like expert assistants who help you compare many books side-by-side to find hidden similarities. The data stored in these repositories grow exponentially — as of 2024, GenBank alone holds more than **240 trillion base pairs** of sequence data, doubling approximately every **18 months**. ### Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme The KTU 2024 syllabus (PBBBT604 Module 1) explicitly tests the learner's ability to: | Course Outcome (CO) | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | What the Student Must Demonstrate | |---|---|---| | CO1 — *Recall database types* | Remember | Identify primary, secondary, and specialized databases | | CO2 — *Apply retrieval tools* | Apply | Use Entrez/SRS to fetch specific records | | CO3 — *Analyze retrieved data* | Analyze | Interpret BLAST output, flatfile annotations | --- ## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Biological Data Storage ### Primary Databases (Archival) Primary databases accept **direct experimental submissions** from researchers. They are the *authoritative source* of raw biological data. - **Examples:** GenBank, EMBL (now ENA), DDBJ, Protein Data Bank (PDB), ArrayExpress - **Key Feature:** Once deposited, an accession number (e.g., `NM_007294`) is permanently assigned and never reused. ### Secondary Databases (Curated / Derived) Secondary databases are built by **computationally processing** primary data, adding layers of annotation, cross-references, and quality control. - **Examples:** UniProt (Swiss-Prot), RefSeq, Ensembl, Pfam, PROSITE, InterPro - **Key Feature:** Higher annotation density, expert curation, and reduced redundancy. ### Specialized Databases (Tertiary / Domain-Specific) These focus on a **specific organism, molecule, or disease**. - **Examples:** OMIM (human genetic disorders), KEGG (metabolic pathways), FlyBase, WormBase, dbSNP, COSMIC (cancer mutations), MGD (mouse genome) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked *"Classify the following databases"*, the student must explicitly state the *criterion of classification* — by **data type** (nucleotide/protein/structure), by **origin** (primary/secondary), or by **biological scope** (generalist/specialized). Examiners award marks only when the criterion is named. # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Architecture of Biological Information Retrieval ## 2.1 The INSDC Triad — How Global Sequence Data is Synchronized Every night, the three INSDC members (GenBank, ENA, DDBJ) exchange new and updated submissions. This guarantees that **a single accession number resolves to the same biological record anywhere in the world**. ### Logical Workflow of a Sequence Submission 1. **Author submits** raw sequence + annotation via **BankIt** (web), **Sequin** (standalone), or **SPAdes/Annotation pipelines**. 2. The submission receives a **temporary ID (TPA / accession.status)**. 3. **Validation checks** run — vector contamination (VecScreen), reading frame verification, taxonomy lookup. 4. On acceptance, a **permanent accession** is issued (e.g., `BK000964`). 5. The record becomes **public** after the author's release date or upon publication. 6. **Daily synchronization** with ENA and DDBJ. > [!NOTE] > **Accession Number Conventions (FASTA-style):** > - **1 letter + 5 digits** — e.g., `M12345` (older GenBank entries) > - **2 letters + 6 digits** — e.g., `AF123456` (GenBank, 1999+) > - **NC_** — RefSeq complete genomic molecule > - **NM_** / **NR_** — RefSeq mRNA / RefSeq RNA > - **NP_** — RefSeq protein > - **XP_** — RefSeq predicted protein > - **P0DTC2** — UniProtKB accession (SARS-CoV-2 spike) ## 2.2 NCBI Entrez — The Federated Search Engine **Entrez** is NCBI's cross-database search engine. It allows a single query string to simultaneously retrieve records from **over 40 linked databases** (PubMed, GenBank, Protein, Structure, Taxonomy, OMIM, etc.). ### Boolean Query Operators Used in Entrez | Operator | Function | Example | |---|---|---| | `AND` | Both terms must appear | `BRCA1 AND breast cancer` | | `OR` | Either term | `kinase OR phosphatase` | | `NOT` | Exclude a term | `human NOT mouse` | | `[Field]` | Restrict to a metadata field | `BRCA1[Gene] AND 2024[Date]` | | `" "` | Exact phrase | `"apoptosis regulator"` | | `( )` | Group logic | `(BRCA1 OR BRCA2) AND therapy` | ### Linked Records — The "Entrez Neighbors" Each database record is **pre-computed** with similarity links to other databases. For example, a GenBank nucleotide record automatically links to: - The corresponding **Protein** record (translation) - The **PubMed** article describing it - The **Taxonomy** entry for its source organism - The **OMIM** phenotype if it is a disease gene - The **GEO** expression profile, if any ## 2.3 Protein Databases — UniProtKB in Detail **UniProtKB** (Universal Protein Knowledgebase) is the world's leading curated protein database, maintained by the **UniProt Consortium** (EBI + SIB + PIR). It consists of two sections: | Section | Curation Level | Size (approx.) | Redundancy | |---|---|---|---| | **Swiss-Prot** | Manually curated, expert-reviewed | ~570,000 entries | High quality, low redundancy | | **TrEMBL** | Automatically annotated (translation of EMBL nucleotide entries) | ~250 million entries | High redundancy, lower quality | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight:** The difference between *annotation score* and *evidence code* is frequently tested. UniProt uses **Experimental Evidence Codes** (ECO:0000269 = used in supporting experimental evidence) versus **Inferred Electronic Annotation** (ECO:0000256 = sequence similarity). An *Experimental* tag boosts an answer's credibility in Board exams. ## 2.4 PDB — The Macromolecular Structure Archive The **Protein Data Bank (PDB)** stores experimentally determined 3-D atomic coordinates of biological macromolecules, primarily determined by: - **X-ray crystallography** (highest precision, ~85% of entries) - **Cryo-Electron Microscopy (cryo-EM)** (rapidly growing, large complexes) - **NMR spectroscopy** (solution-state, smaller proteins) A PDB entry (e.g., `1A2B`) contains the coordinates in **PDB format** (legacy) or **mmCIF format** (modern). The associated **Structure Summary page** provides: - Sequence, secondary structure, ligand list - Validation reports (Ramachandran plot, clash scores) - Cross-links to **UniProt**, **GenBank**, **PubMed**, **CATH/SCOP** classification ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the formulas below — at least **two of these appear in every KTU Part B question** on this module. ### Table 2.1 — Essential Quantitative Relationships | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Sequence Identity % | $\%id = \dfrac{N_{id}}{L_{aln}} \times 100$ | $N_{id}$ = matched residues; $L_{aln}$ = alignment length | Percentage; 0–100 | | Sequence Similarity % | $\%sim = \dfrac{N_{pos}}{L_{aln}} \times 100$ | $N_{pos}$ = positive (conserved) substitutions | Percentage | | Molecular Weight (protein) | $MW = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i - 18(n-1)$ | $M_i$ = residue mass; $n$ = # residues; 18 = mass of water (Da) | Daltons (Da) | | E-value (BLAST) | $E = K m n e^{-\lambda S}$ | $K, \lambda$ = Karlin–Altschul params; $m$ = query length; $n$ = DB size; $S$ = raw score | Expected hits by chance | | Bit Score | $S' = \dfrac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$ | Independent of database size, comparable across runs | Bits, dimensionless | | Tm (Wallace rule) | $T_m = 2(A+T) + 4(G+C)$ | For short oligonucleotides (<14 nt) | Degrees Celsius (°C) | | GC Content | $\%GC = \dfrac{G+C}{A+T+G+C} \times 100$ | All four bases summed | Percentage | > **⚠️ LaTeX Isolation Note:** In every equation above, vertical bars were replaced with `\vert` where applicable, but since this table contains no pipe-delimited expressions, no `\vert` substitution was needed. Maintain this convention when typing your own answer sheets. ### Table 2.2 — Database Classification Quick-Reference (Board-Exam Favourite) | Database | Type | Data Stored | Submitter / Curator | |---|---|---|---| | GenBank | Primary, nucleotide | DNA/RNA sequences | NCBI (USA) | | ENA | Primary, nucleotide | DNA/RNA sequences | EMBL-EBI (UK) | | DDBJ | Primary, nucleotide | DNA/RNA sequences | NIG (Japan) | | RefSeq | Secondary, curated nucleotide | Reference sequences | NCBI | | UniProtKB-Swiss-Prot | Secondary, curated protein | Reviewed proteins | UniProt Consortium | | UniProtKB-TrEMBL | Secondary, automated protein | Unreviewed translations | EBI automation pipeline | | PDB | Primary, structure | 3-D atomic coordinates | RCSB, PDBe, PDBj | | OMIM | Specialized | Human genetic disorders | Johns Hopkins | | KEGG | Specialized | Metabolic / signalling pathways | Kyoto University | | Pfam | Secondary | Protein families (HMMs) | EBI | | dbSNP | Specialized | Single Nucleotide Polymorphisms | NCBI | # 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — The Karlin–Altschul E-value The **E-value** answers the question: *"Given a database of this size, how many alignments with this score or better do I expect by pure chance?"* ### Step 1 — Start with the score statistics For two random sequences compared with a substitution scoring matrix (e.g., **BLOSUM62**), the per-position score has: - mean $\mu < 0$ - standard deviation $\sigma$ When summing over an alignment of length $m$, by the central limit theorem: $$S \sim \mathcal{N}(m\mu,\ m\sigma^{2})$$ But this is only valid for ungapped local alignments. Karlin and Altschul (1990) showed that for a **local alignment with gaps allowed**, the score $S$ follows an **extreme-value distribution (Gumbel)** rather than a normal distribution. ### Step 2 — Define the Gumbel distribution The probability that a random alignment has score $\geq S$ is: $$P(S \geq x) = 1 - e^{-E}$$ where $E$ is the expected number of alignments reaching score $x$ or higher purely by chance. ### Step 3 — Derive the E-value formula By definition of the Gumbel distribution and the alignment scoring theory: $$E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$$ Where: - $K$ — a constant depending on the scoring matrix and gap penalties (Karlin-Altschul parameter) - $\lambda$ — the positive root of $\sum_{i,j} p_i p_j e^{\lambda \sigma_{ij}} = 1$ - $m$ — effective length of the query sequence - $n$ — effective length of the database - $S$ — raw alignment score > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Summary of Each Term:** > - $K$ accounts for the *reward* the scoring matrix gives for matching symbols. > - $\lambda$ converts the raw score into a natural-log scale. > - $m \cdot n$ represents the *search space* — larger search space = more chances of spurious matches. ### Step 4 — Convert to Bit Score for database-independent comparison Since raw scores depend on $K$, $\lambda$, and database size, NCBI defines the **Bit Score** $S'$: $$S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$$ This is independent of database size and query length, allowing direct comparison across different BLAST runs. ### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example Suppose a BLAST search yields: - Raw score $S = 120$ - $K = 0.041$ - $\lambda = 0.267$ - $m = 250$ (query length) - $n = 1{,}000{,}000$ (database size) **Calculate the E-value:** $$\begin{aligned} E &= K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S} \\ &= 0.041 \times 250 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \times e^{-0.267 \times 120} \\ &= 10{,}250{,}000 \times e^{-32.04} \\ &= 10{,}250{,}000 \times 1.27 \times 10^{-14} \\ &\approx 1.30 \times 10^{-7} \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** An E-value of $1.30 \times 10^{-7}$ means **less than one chance in 10 million** that this hit is random — a very strong, biologically meaningful match. **Now compute the Bit Score:** $$\begin{aligned} S' &= \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2} \\ &= \frac{0.267 \times 120 - \ln(0.041)}{\ln 2} \\ &= \frac{32.04 - (-3.194)}{0.693} \\ &= \frac{35.234}{0.693} \\ &\approx 50.84 \text{ bits} \end{aligned}$$ A Bit Score of **~51 bits** is considered a strong homology signal. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Sequence Identity Calculation Given a pairwise alignment of length **120 residues**: ``` Query : MKTLLLTLVVVTIVCLDLGYTFQPQNGQFICTTAG--NSSRLPIPSKQCV Subject : MKTLILTLVVITIVCLDLGYTFQPQNGQFICTTAGNSSSRLPIPSKQCV ``` - **Identical positions** $N_{id}$ = 110 - **Total aligned length** $L_{aln}$ = 120 - **Conservative substitutions (positive)** = 6 - **Mismatches (non-conservative)** = 4 **Calculate:** $$\%id = \frac{110}{120} \times 100 = 91.67\%$$ $$\%sim = \frac{110 + 6}{120} \times 100 = 96.67\%$$ **Interpretation:** A 91.67% identity over 120 residues almost certainly indicates **orthology** (same gene in different species), not a chance match. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Data Retrieval Tools | Tool | Primary Use | Database Backend | Search Mode | Strength | Limitation | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Entrez (NCBI)** | Cross-database text search | 40+ NCBI DBs | Boolean + field tags | Most integrated; PubMed links | Limited sequence similarity | | **SRS (EMBL-EBI legacy)** | Text + flatfile retrieval | ENA + secondary | Boolean | Fast text queries | Being phased out | | **EB-eye (EBI)** | Cross-DB text search | ~200 EBI DBs | Boolean | Largest integrated catalog | No native sequence search | | **UniProt Retrieve** | Protein-specific text | UniProtKB | Boolean + field tags | Highest-quality protein records | Only proteins | | **Ensembl BioMart** | Bulk gene/protein retrieval | Ensembl | Graphical + REST | Bulk export (TSV/CSV) | Only Ensembl-annotated genomes | | **UCSC Table Browser** | Genome-coordinate retrieval | UCSC Genome Browser | Coordinate-based | Pre-calculated tracks | Limited to UCSC assemblies | | **BLAST** | Sequence similarity | NCBI nr, RefSeq, PDB | Algorithm-based | Finds homologs | Slow for huge queries | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Database (Decision Tree Logic) **Step 1:** Identify your biological molecule → Nucleotide, Protein, or Structure? **Step 2:** If *nucleotide*: - Raw submitted → **GenBank / ENA / DDBJ** - Curated reference → **RefSeq** - mRNA expression atlas → **ENA + Expression Atlas** **Step 3:** If *protein*: - Curated (high confidence) → **Swiss-Prot** - High-throughput (all translations) → **TrEMBL** - Family classification → **Pfam / InterPro** **Step 4:** If *structure*: - Experimentally determined coordinates → **PDB** - Computed homology models → **SWISS-MODEL / AlphaFold DB** **Step 5:** If *disease / phenotype* → **OMIM / ClinVar / Orphanet** **Step 6:** If *pathway* → **KEGG / Reactome / BioCyc** > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often answer *"I would use GenBank"* for a *protein* query. GenBank stores nucleotide records; protein translations are stored in the linked `Protein` database or in **UniProtKB**. The Board examiner deducts **2 marks** for such category confusion. # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Biological Database Taxonomy ```mermaid graph TD BioDB["Biological Databases"] BioDB --> PrimaryDB["Primary Databases
