For KTU Board exams, when asked *"Explain the structure of a GenBank entry"*, you must list **at least eight sections** from the diagram above. # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define a primary database and a secondary database. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A **primary database** is one that accepts direct experimental submissions from researchers and stores raw biological data without further computational curation. Example: **GenBank** stores directly submitted nucleotide sequences. > > A **secondary database** is built by computationally processing primary data, adding curated annotations and reducing redundancy. Example: **Swiss-Prot** (part of UniProtKB) provides expert-reviewed protein sequences derived from TrEMBL and primary sources. > > *(1 mark — definition of primary; 1 mark — definition of secondary; 1 mark — correct examples)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is the significance of an accession number in biological databases?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > An **accession number** is a unique, stable, alphanumeric identifier assigned to a biological record (sequence, structure, or entry) upon acceptance into a database. Its significance includes: > 1. **Permanence** — Once issued, it is never changed or reused, ensuring traceability of scientific data. > 2. **Citation** — Researchers cite the accession number in publications to allow peers to retrieve the exact same record. > 3. **Cross-Database Linking** — The same accession prefix (e.g., `NM_` for RefSeq mRNA) uniquely identifies the type of record across NCBI. > > *(1 mark per significance point)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### 🔵 Question A — Comprehensive Question on NCBI & GenBank `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of NCBI's Entrez system**. Describe how cross-database links are established between GenBank, PubMed, Protein, and OMIM. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **structure of a GenBank flatfile entry** with the help of a labelled diagram. List the important fields in the FEATURES table and explain how the ORIGIN block stores the nucleotide sequence. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Entrez Architecture (7 marks):** > Entrez is NCBI's **federated search engine** that allows a single query to simultaneously search **40+ biological databases** through pre-computed cross-references. > > **Architecture components:** > 1. **Query Parser (1 mark):** Accepts Boolean operators (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`), field tags (`[Gene]`, `[Author]`, `[Organism]`), and date filters. Example: `BRCA1[Gene] AND "Homo sapiens"[Organism]`. > 2. **Indexed Database Layer (1 mark):** Each Entrez database maintains inverted indices on metadata fields for fast retrieval. > 3. **Cross-Link Computation (2 marks):** NCBI pre-computes links between records: > - **GenBank ↔ Protein:** translation link from the `CDS` feature > - **PubMed ↔ GenBank:** citation link via the `REFERENCE` block > - **GenBank ↔ OMIM:** gene-disease link via curated gene records > - **Taxonomy ↔ GenBank:** organism source link > 4. **Neighbour Mechanism (1 mark):** Each record automatically links to similar records (sequence neighbors, citation neighbors). > 5. **Result Display (1 mark):** Hits are ranked by relevance, with hyperlinks to the linked databases. > 6. **Block Diagram (1 mark):** As shown in **Section 4.2** above, the user query passes through the query parser → indexed records → all linked databases (PubMed, Nucl, Prot, OMIM, etc.) and external links. **Part (b) — GenBank Flatfile Structure (7 marks):** > A GenBank flatfile is a **human-readable ASCII text record** containing both annotation and sequence. Its major fields are: > > | Field | Purpose | Example | > |---|---|---| > | `LOCUS` | Identifier, length, type | `LOCUS NM_007294 7089 bp mRNA linear PRI 15-MAY-2024` | > | `DEFINITION` | Concise description | `Homo sapiens BRCA1 DNA repair associated, transcript variant 1, mRNA` | > | `ACCESSION` | Unique stable ID | `NM_007294` | > | `VERSION` | Accession + GI | `NM_007294.4` | > | `KEYWORDS` | Free-text tags | `DNA repair; tumor suppressor` | > | `SOURCE` / `ORGANISM` | Source organism + taxonomy | `Homo sapiens` → `Eukaryota; Metazoa; Chordata; Mammalia` | > | `REFERENCE` | Publication citation | Authors, title, journal, PubMed ID | > | `FEATURES` | Annotated regions | `gene`, `CDS`, `mRNA`, `exon`, `intron`, `misc_feature` | > | `ORIGIN` | Raw nucleotide sequence | Starts with `ORIGIN` line, followed by 60 bp per row, numbered `/1, /61` | > | `//` | Record terminator | Single line at file end | > > **FEATURES table explanation (2 marks):** The FEATURES table begins with the header `FEATURES Location/Qualifiers` and contains structured annotations. Example for the BRCA1 gene: > ``` > CDS 100..7092 > /gene="BRCA1" > /product="breast cancer type 1 susceptibility protein" > /protein_id="NP_009225.1" > ``` > **ORIGIN block (1 mark):** The `ORIGIN` line is followed by the nucleotide sequence arranged in blocks of 10 bases, with row counters in the format `/1`, `/61`, `/121`, etc., incrementing every 60 bases. The file terminates with `//`. > > **Labelled diagram (1 mark):** As shown in **Section 4.4** of these notes. --- ### 🟢 Question B — Alternative Comprehensive Question on UniProt, PDB & Data Analysis `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Describe the **organization of UniProtKB** into Swiss-Prot and TrEMBL. Discuss the annotation evidence codes used in UniProt entries and explain how a researcher should interpret them. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain how **PDB** stores macromolecular structures. Describe the main sections of a PDB Structure Summary page and discuss how a researcher can link a PDB structure to the corresponding UniProt protein and GenBank nucleotide record. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — UniProtKB Organization (7 marks):** > **UniProtKB** (Universal Protein Knowledgebase) is the world's leading curated protein database, jointly maintained by the **EBI (UK)**, **SIB (Switzerland)**, and **PIR (USA)**. > > **Two sections (2 marks):** > - **Swiss-Prot (Reviewed)** — Manually curated by expert biologists; ~570,000 entries; minimal redundancy; high annotation quality. Each entry is created, updated, and validated by a curator. > - **TrEMBL (Unreviewed)** — Automatically translated from EMBL nucleotide coding sequences (CDS); ~250 million entries; high redundancy; pending manual review. TrEMBL entries are eventually promoted to Swiss-Prot after expert curation. > > **Annotation Evidence Codes (3 marks):** > UniProt uses **Evidence Code Ontology (ECO)** tags to indicate the *source* of an annotation: > > | ECO Code | Meaning | Reliability | > |---|---|---| > | `ECO:0000269` | Experimental evidence used in manual assertion | Highest | > | `ECO:0000312` | Imported from another manually curated source | Very high | > | `ECO:0000255` | Assertion from sequence similarity (e.g., InterPro) | High | > | `ECO:0000256` | Inferred by electronic annotation only | Lower (auto-computed) | > | `ECO:0000303` | No biological data available; inferred by author | Lowest | > > **Interpretation (2 marks):** A researcher should **prioritize annotations backed by ECO:0000269 (experimental)** over electronic-only annotations. For drug-target research, an annotation with experimental evidence is required; for exploratory genomics, electronic annotations are sufficient. **Part (b) — PDB and Cross-Database Linking (7 marks):** > The **Protein Data Bank (PDB)** is the single global archive of experimentally determined 3-D atomic coordinates of biological macromolecules, jointly managed by **RCSB (USA)**, **PDBe (Europe)**, and **PDBj (Japan)**. **(1 mark)** > > **What PDB stores (1 mark):** Atomic coordinates (`x, y, z`), B-factors, occupancies, secondary structure assignments, ligand identity, experimental method (X-ray, NMR, cryo-EM), resolution, R-factor, and the experimental data citation. > > **Structure Summary page sections (3 marks):** > 1. **Title and Authors** — e.g., *Structure of the SARS-CoV-2 spike receptor-binding domain bound to ACE2.* > 2. **Sequence and Macromolecule** — FASTA sequence of each chain, source organism, mutation list. > 3. **Ligands and Modifications** — Bound small molecules (e.g., `NAG`, `GDP`, drug compounds). > 4. **Experimental Data & Validation** — Resolution, R-free, Ramachandran outliers, MolProbity score. > 5. **Functional and Biological Context** — GO terms, enzyme classification, pathway membership. > > **Cross-Database Linking (2 marks):** > - Each PDB entry lists **UniProt IDs** in the *Macromolecule* section — e.g., `P0DTC2` for the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein. Clicking the UniProt link takes the researcher to the full Swiss-Prot record. > - The PDB entry also shows the **source organism's NCBI Taxonomy ID**, linking to the GenBank nucleotide record (e.g., the gene encoding the spike protein). > - Citations link to **PubMed**, allowing full-text retrieval. > - **CATH / SCOP** classifications link to the protein's structural fold family. > > Together, this integration transforms a PDB entry from a static coordinate file into a **hub of biological knowledge**. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:** > > 1. **Confusing primary vs. secondary databases** (–2 marks). Always state the criterion of classification explicitly: *“Swiss-Prot is a secondary, curated protein database because it is derived by expert review of TrEMBL translations.”* > 2. **Misusing accession prefixes** (–1 mark). `NM_` = RefSeq **mRNA**, not protein. Protein is `NP_`. UniProt accessions are **6-character alphanumeric** (e.g., `P0DTC2`), NOT `NM_`. > 3. **Ignoring evidence codes** (–2 marks). In UniProt-based answers, never write *"the protein is phosphorylated at Ser 128"* without citing the ECO evidence code or the supporting publication. > 4. **Skipping the diagram** (–2 marks). KTU Part B explicitly requires *"neat labelled diagram"* for full marks. Always include a block diagram for Entrez architecture, flatfile structure, or workflow. > 5. **Computing E-value incorrectly** (–3 marks). The formula $E = K m n e^{-\lambda S}$ requires you to clearly identify each variable; examiners deduct marks if $m$ and $n$ are mixed up (m = query length, n = database size). > 6. **Using GenBank for a protein query** (–2 marks). Use UniProtKB or NCBI Protein, not GenBank. GenBank is for nucleotide sequences only. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > ✅ **Primary databases** = archival, raw experimental submissions (GenBank, PDB, GEO). > > ✅ **Secondary databases** = curated, annotation-rich, derived from primary (Swiss-Prot, RefSeq, Pfam, CATH). > > ✅ **Specialized databases** = single-organism or single-disease focus (OMIM, FlyBase, COSMIC). > > ✅ **INSDC triad** = GenBank (USA) + ENA (UK) + DDBJ (Japan) — synchronized daily. > > ✅ **Accession number** is permanent, unique, never reused; format encodes the source database. > > ✅ **Entrez** supports Boolean operators + field tags (`[Gene]`, `[Author]`, `[Organism]`) across 40+ linked databases. > > ✅ **FASTA format** = single header line starting with `>` followed by raw sequence (used for all BLAST input). > > ✅ **GenBank flatfile** has 10+ sections; key block is `FEATURES` (annotation) + `ORIGIN` (sequence) + `//` (terminator). > > ✅ **UniProtKB = Swiss-Prot (curated) + TrEMBL (automated)**; trust ECO:0000269 (experimental) annotations most. > > ✅ **PDB** stores 3-D atomic coordinates; cross-linked to UniProt (chains), GenBank (source gene), PubMed (citation). > > ✅ **E-value** $E = K m n e^{-\lambda S}$: lower = more significant; $E < 10^{-5}$ is generally considered homologous. > > ✅ **Bit Score** $S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$: database-size-independent, comparable across runs. > > ✅ **% Identity** = (matched positions / aligned length) × 100; >30% over long alignments = probable homology. > > ✅ **Molecular Weight** of protein = $\sum M_i - 18(n-1)$ Da (water loss correction). > > ✅ **Tools**: Entrez (text), BLAST (sequence), ClustalW (alignment), EMBOSS (suite), BioMart (bulk export).

topics.title: GenBank
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT604 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** GenBank

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GenBank®** is the **National Institutes of Health (NIH)** genetic sequence database, an **annotated collection of publicly available DNA sequences**. It is maintained and distributed by the **National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI)**, a division of the **U.S. National Library of Medicine (NLM)**, located at the National Institutes of Health in Bethesda, Maryland. GenBank is part of the **International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration (INSDC)**, which also includes the **European Nucleotide Archive (ENA)** at the EMBL-EBI and the **DNA Data Bank of Japan (DDBJ)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Library Analogy:**
> Imagine a **massive, global library** where instead of books, the shelves hold **digital DNA sequences** (A, T, G, C letters). Each "book" is annotated with information such as the organism it came from, the gene name, the publication reference, and the biological function. GenBank is essentially this library — freely accessible, internationally synchronized (a sequence submitted to any one of the three INSDC partners — GenBank, ENA, DDBJ — appears in all three within 24 hours), and constantly growing.

**Key Quantitative Metrics of GenBank (2024 statistics):**
- **Total bases:** Over **4 trillion bases** (≈ 4 × 10¹² bp)
- **Total sequences:** More than **250 million** individual sequence records
- **Growth rate:** **Doubles approximately every 18 months**
- **Daily updates:** ~ 1–2 million new/exchanged sequences

### 1.3 Historical Context

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **1982** | GenBank established by **Walter Goad** at Los Alamos National Laboratory |
| **1988** | NCBI takes over responsibility for GenBank |
| **1990s** | INSDC collaboration formalized with EBI/ENA and DDBJ |
| **2000s** | GenBank flat-file format standardized across INSDC |
| **Present** | Continuously updated, free public access via NCBI website and Entrez |

### 1.4 Relationship to Other Major Biological Databases

> [!NOTE]
> **NCBI hosts the "Omics" Triad:**
> - **GenBank** → Nucleotide sequences (DNA/RNA)
> - **UniProt/SwissProt** → Protein sequences
> - **PDB (Protein Data Bank)** → 3D protein structures
> All are cross-linked via **Entrez**, NCBI's integrated search engine.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 Organizational Hierarchy of GenBank Data

GenBank is divided into **divisions** for efficient data management:

```
GenBank
├── PRI (Primate)
├── ROD (Rodent)
├── MAM (Other Mammals)
├── VRT (Other Vertebrates)
├── INV (Invertebrates)
├── PLN (Plants)
├── BCT (Bacteria)
├── VRL (Viruses)
├── PHG (Bacteriophages)
├── SYN (Synthetic sequences)
├── UNA (Unannotated)
└── EST, GSS, HTG, PAT, STS (specialized divisions)
```

### 2.2 GenBank Flat File Format — Anatomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **GenBank flat file** is a human-readable ASCII text file containing a single sequence record. The **3-letter code (LOCUS)** is the heart of the flat file. Each field begins with a 2-letter keyword at the left margin.

| Field (Keyword) | Description | Example |
|-----------------|-------------|---------|
| **LOCUS** | Unique identifier, length, molecule type, division, modification date | `LOCUS    NM_001234567            1245 bp    mRNA    linear   PRI 15-OCT-2024` |
| **DEFINITION** | Brief description of sequence | `DEFINITION  Homo sapiens BRCA1 mRNA, complete cds.` |
| **ACCESSION** | Unique stable identifier (UPI or string with version) | `ACCESSION   NM_001234567  REGION: 1..1245` |
| **VERSION** | Accession + version number | `VERSION     NM_001234567.1` |
| **KEYWORDS** | Free-text descriptors for Entrez indexing | `KEYWORDS    breast cancer; tumor suppressor.` |
| **SOURCE** | Organism (free-text) | `SOURCE      Homo sapiens (human)` |
| **ORGANISM** | Full taxonomic classification | `ORGANISM  Homo sapiens Eukaryota; Metazoa; Chordata ...` |
| **REFERENCE** | Bibliographic citation(s) | `REFERENCE   1 (bases 1 to 1245) AUTHORS Hall, J.M. et al.` |
| **FEATURES** | Annotated features (CDS, gene, mRNA, exon, etc.) | `FEATURES    Location/Qualifiers  source 1..1245 /organism="Homo sapiens"` |
| **ORIGIN** | Sequence header | `ORIGIN      1 atgcatgcta tgctacgtac gtacgtacgt acgtacgtat gcatgcatgc ...` |
| **`//`** | End-of-record delimiter | (terminator) |

### 2.3 Accession Number Conventions

> [!NOTE]
> An **Accession Number** is the unique, stable identifier assigned to a sequence record that never changes, even if the sequence itself is updated. The **Version** number increments on update, the **GI** number is the older NCBI internal identifier (now largely deprecated).

| Accession Prefix | Meaning |
|------------------|---------|
| `NM_` / `NR_` | RefSeq mRNA / RefSeq non-coding RNA |
| `XM_` / `XR_` | RefSeq predicted mRNA / RNA |
| `NP_` / `XP_` | RefSeq protein (predicted) |
| `M12345` | GenBank direct submission (historical) |
| `AF123456` | Direct submission (modern, 2 letters + 6 digits) |
| `BN000123` | Third-party annotation (TPA) |

### 2.4 KTU Concept Sheet — "Cheat Sheet"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Concepts for KTU Board Examination:**

| Concept | Key Point |
|---------|-----------|
| **GenBank** | NIH/NCBI public nucleotide sequence database |
| **INSDC Partners** | GenBank (NCBI), ENA (EBI), DDBJ — data shared within 24 hrs |
| **Format** | GenBank Flat File (`.gb` or `.genbank`) vs. FASTA (`.fasta`) |
| **Accession vs. Version vs. GI** | Accession = stable, Version = updated, GI = deprecated |
| **Divisions** | Organism-based + data-type based (EST, GSS, HTG) |
| **Annotation** | Provided by submitters + curated by NCBI RefSeq |
| **Submission Tools** | BankIt (web), tbl2asn (stand-alone), Sequin (legacy) |
| **Retrieval Tools** | Entrez Nucleotide, BLAST, batch Entrez |
| **Updates** | Every 2 months → `gbrelease.txt`; daily updates via FTP |
| **Growth Law** | Sequences double ~ every 18 months (empirical) |
| **FASTA format** | `>header\nSEQUENCE` — no annotation, just ID + sequence |
| **Cross-references** | Links to PubMed, Taxonomy, OMIM, Gene, Protein, Conserved Domains |

### 2.5 The INSDC Three-Way Data Exchange

```
     Submitter
        |
        v
   [ Any of the 3 ]
        |
        +--> NCBI (GenBank)  --+
        +--> EBI (ENA)   <--->|  Daily synchronization
        +--> DDBJ (Japan)     |
                              v
                Combined Public Release
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility in engineering:** GenBank underpins modern **genomic medicine**, **forensic DNA analysis**, **phylogenetics**, **molecular diagnostics (PCR primer design)**, and the development of **synthetic biology** and **CRISPR gene-editing** workflows. For B.Tech Bioinformatics students, mastering GenBank retrieval is foundational for **sequence alignment (BLAST, ClustalW)**, **phylogenetic tree construction**, and **primer/probe design**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Content Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since GenBank is a **database/information system** topic, the "derivations" here focus on the **logical data model**, **identifier schemes**, and **search algorithms** rather than continuous mathematical formulas. Each logical step is shown explicitly with reasoning.

### 3.1 Worked Example: Anatomy of a Real GenBank Record

**Step 1 — Identify the LOCUS line and decode it:**

```
LOCUS       NM_007294              5592 bp    mRNA    linear   PRI 15-OCT-2024
```

**Reasoning:** 
- `NM_007294` is a **RefSeq accession** (`NM_` = non-predicted mRNA).
- `5592 bp` is the **length** of the sequence in base pairs.
- `mRNA` is the **molecule type** (could be `DNA`, `rRNA`, `tRNA`, `misc_RNA`).
- `linear` is the **topology** (could be `circular` for plasmids, mitochondria, chloroplasts).
- `PRI` is the **division** code (Primate).
- `15-OCT-2024` is the **last modification date**.

**Step 2 — Identify the ACCESSION and VERSION:**

```
ACCESSION   NM_007294
VERSION     NM_007294.4
```

**Reasoning:** The **accession `NM_007294`** never changes. The **version `.4`** indicates this is the **4th release** of this record (i.e., the sequence has been updated 3 times).

**Step 3 — Interpret the FEATURES table (most exam-relevant block):**

```
FEATURES             Location/Qualifiers
     source          1..5592
                     /organism="Homo sapiens"
                     /mol_type="mRNA"
                     /db_xref="taxon:9606"
                     /chromosome="17"
     gene            1..5592
                     /gene="BRCA1"
     CDS             120..5592
                     /gene="BRCA1"
                     /product="breast cancer type 1 susceptibility protein"
                     /protein_id="NP_009225.1"
                     /translation="MDLSALRVEEVQNVINAMQKILECPICLELIKEPVSTKCDHIFCK
                                  FQALGPKTESDKQTLISGGKTF..."
```

**Reasoning:**
- `1..5592` is a **location operator** — a **range** from base 1 to base 5592.
- `CDS` = **CoDing Sequence** → the region translated into protein.
- `/translation` provides the **amino acid sequence** in single-letter code.
- `/protein_id` links this nucleotide record to the corresponding **protein record in RefSeq (`NP_009225.1`)**.
- `/db_xref` provides a **cross-reference to the NCBI Taxonomy database** (taxon:9606 = *Homo sapiens*).

**Step 4 — Compute coding sequence properties:**

$$
\text{Number of amino acids} = \frac{\text{Length of CDS} - \text{any stop codons included}}{3}
$$

$$
\text{Length of CDS} = 5592 - 120 + 1 = 5473 \text{ bases}
$$

$$
\text{Number of amino acids} = \frac{5473 - 3 \text{ (stop codon)}}{3} = \frac{5470}{3} \approx 1823 \text{ residues}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 3 bases corresponding to the stop codon (`TAA`, `TAG`, or `TGA`) are typically **not translated** but are still **part of the CDS location range** in GenBank annotation. Hence the actual protein length may be CDS/3 − 1.

**Step 5 — Converting GenBank → FASTA (a frequent exam task):**

A GenBank flat file contains one sequence and its annotation. To convert to FASTA, the student must:
1. Extract the **first word of the DEFINITION** line.
2. Prepend `>` followed by the **accession number**.
3. Append the **sequence (from ORIGIN block)** in 60-character lines.

**Resulting FASTA:**

```
>NM_007294.4 Homo sapiens BRCA1 mRNA, complete cds
ATGGATTTATCTGCTCTTCGCGTTGAAGAAGTACAAAATGTCATTAATGCTATGCAGAAA
ATCTTAGAGTGTCCCATCTGTCTGGAGTTGATCAAGGAACCTGTCTCCACAAAGTGTGAC
CACATATTTTGCAAATTTTGCATGCTGAAACTTCTCAACCAGAAGAAAGGGCCTTCACAG
TGTCCTTTATGTAAGAATGATATAACCAAAAGGAGCCTACAAGAAAGTACGAGATTTAGT
...
```

### 3.2 Worked Example: Entrez Search Algorithm (Boolean Logic)

NCBI's **Entrez** uses a **term-based Boolean search engine** across multiple databases.

**Given search expression:**  
`(BRCA1 AND human) NOT (mouse OR rat)`

**Step-by-step resolution:**

1. **Lexical analysis** → tokens: `BRCA1`, `AND`, `human`, `NOT`, `(`, `mouse`, `OR`, `rat`, `)`
2. **Operator precedence** applied: `NOT` > `AND` > `OR`
3. **Evaluation order:**
   - First evaluate `mouse OR rat` → set A ∪ B
   - Then evaluate `BRCA1 AND human` → set C ∩ D
   - Finally apply `NOT` → (C ∩ D) − (A ∪ B)
4. **Result**: Records that contain the keyword "BRCA1" **AND** are from "human", **AND** are **NOT** tagged as "mouse" or "rat".

This is exactly how Entrez retrieves records at NCBI: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nucleotide](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nucleotide)

### 3.3 Analytical Content Matrix

| Sub-Topic | Expected Depth | Common Board Question Type |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------------------|
| Flat file structure | High | "List the fields in a GenBank record" |
| INSDC partners | High | "Explain INSDC and its members" |
| Accession numbers | High | "Distinguish accession, version, GI" |
| FASTA vs GenBank | High | "Convert/differentiate" |
| Submission tools | Medium | "Briefly explain Sequin / BankIt" |
| Entrez search | Medium | "How does Entrez retrieve records?" |
| Growth & statistics | Low | "Mention the growth rate of GenBank" |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GenBank Flat File Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GenBank Flat File Record] --> B[LOCUS Header]
    A --> C[DEFINITION]
    A --> D[ACCESSION + VERSION]
    A --> E[KEYWORDS]
    A --> F[SOURCE / ORGANISM Taxonomy]
    A --> G[REFERENCE 1 to N citations]
    A --> H[FEATURES Table]
    A --> I[ORIGIN Sequence Block]
    A --> J[Slash // End Marker]
    
    B --> B1[Sequence Name + Length + Topology + Division + Date]
    D --> D1[Stable Accession: NM_007294]
    D --> D2[Version Increment: NM_007294.4]
    F --> F1[Full Taxonomic Lineage]
    H --> H1[CDS gene mRNA exon misc_feature]
    H --> H2[Qualifiers: /gene, /product, /protein_id, /translation]
    H --> H3[Location Operators: 1..5592, complement, join]
    I --> I1[60 bp per line, numbered left margin]
    
    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 INSDC Data Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Researcher / Submitter] -->|Submit via BankIt / Sequin / tbl2asn| Q1{Choose INSDC Partner}
    Q1 --> N[NCBI GenBank USA]
    Q1 --> E[ENA EMBL-EBI Europe]
    Q1 --> D[DDBJ Japan]
    N <-->|Daily Exchange| E
    E <-->|Daily Exchange| D
    D <-->|Daily Exchange| N
    N --> P[Public Database]
    E --> P
    D --> P
    P --> R[Users via Entrez / FTP]
    
    style S fill:#17becf
    style N fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff
    style R fill:#bcbd22
```

### 4.3 GenBank Divisions Tree (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G[GenBank] --> D1[Organism-based Divisions]
    G --> D2[Data-type Divisions]
    
    D1 --> O1[PRI Primate]
    D1 --> O2[ROD Rodent]
    D1 --> O3[MAM Mammals]
    D1 --> O4[VRT Vertebrates]
    D1 --> O5[INV Invertebrates]
    D1 --> O6[PLN Plants]
    D1 --> O7[BCT Bacteria]
    D1 --> O8[VRL Viruses]
    D1 --> O9[PHG Bacteriophages]
    
    D2 --> T1[EST Expressed Sequence Tags]
    D2 --> T2[GSS Genome Survey Sequences]
    D2 --> T3[HTG High Throughput Genomic unfinished]
    D2 --> T4[STS Sequence Tagged Sites]
    D2 --> T5[PAT Patent sequences]
    D2 --> T6[SYN Synthetic constructs]
    D2 --> T7[UNA Unannotated]
    
    style G fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style D1 fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style D2 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 GenBank Submission-Retrieval Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Lab as Researcher/Lab
    participant Tool as Submission Tool
    participant INSDC as NCBI GenBank
    participant RefSeq as RefSeq Curators
    participant User as End User
    
    Lab->>Tool: Prepare submission (BankIt / Sequin / tbl2asn)
    Tool->>INSDC: Submit annotated sequence + metadata
    INSDC->>INSDC: Validate, assign Accession Number
    INSDC->>RefSeq: Forward for RefSeq curation (if applicable)
    RefSeq->>INSDC: Return curated NM_/NP_ record
    INSDC->>INSDC: Daily exchange with ENA and DDBJ
    Lab->>User: Accession number released (24-48 hrs)
    User->>INSDC: Query via Entrez / BLAST
    INSDC-->>User: Return annotated flat file / FASTA
    User->>User: Perform alignment / phylogeny / primer design
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model 2024]**

**Q1. Define GenBank. Name its parent organization.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
GenBank is a comprehensive, publicly accessible nucleotide sequence database maintained by the **National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI)**, which is a division of the U.S. **National Library of Medicine (NLM)**, under the **National Institutes of Health (NIH), Bethesda, Maryland**. It contains annotated DNA sequences submitted by researchers worldwide and is part of the **International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration (INSDC)** with ENA and DDBJ. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. What is the difference between FASTA format and GenBank flat file format?**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | FASTA Format | GenBank Flat File |
|---------|--------------|-------------------|
| Header | Single line: `>id description` | Multiple labeled fields |
| Annotation | None (header only) | Rich feature table + qualifiers |
| Size | Compact | Larger (full annotation) |
| Extension | `.fasta`, `.fa`, `.fna` | `.gb`, `.genbank` |
| Use | Alignment, BLAST input | Detailed record inspection |
| Multiple sequences | Yes (concatenated) | One per file |

FASTA is **sequence-only**, while GenBank is **sequence + annotation**. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks** sub-parts, as per the KTU ESE pattern. **Internal choice** is provided between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **GenBank flat file** with a clearly labeled diagram and example. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A GenBank flat file is a structured ASCII text file containing a single annotated nucleotide sequence record. Its main components are:

1. **LOCUS line** — Contains the sequence name (e.g., `NM_007294`), the length in bp, the molecule type (DNA/RNA), the topology (linear/circular), the division code, and the modification date.

2. **DEFINITION** — A one-line description of the sequence (e.g., "Homo sapiens BRCA1 mRNA, complete cds").

3. **ACCESSION** — The unique stable identifier (e.g., `NM_007294`) that **never changes**.

4. **VERSION** — The accession plus a version number (e.g., `NM_007294.4`) that **increments with updates**.

5. **KEYWORDS** — Free-text descriptors used by Entrez for indexing.

6. **SOURCE / ORGANISM** — Free-text organism and full taxonomic lineage (e.g., *Homo sapiens; Eukaryota; Metazoa; Chordata; Craniata; Vertebrata; Mammalia; Primates*).

7. **REFERENCE** — One or more bibliographic citations linked to **PubMed** IDs.

8. **FEATURES Table** — A two-column table. The left column lists the **feature key** (e.g., `CDS`, `gene`, `mRNA`, `exon`, `intron`, `misc_feature`, `5'UTR`, `3'UTR`). The right column gives the **location** (e.g., `1..5592` or `complement(join(100..250,400..500))`) and **qualifiers** prefixed by `/` (e.g., `/gene="BRCA1"`, `/product="..."`, `/protein_id="NP_009225.1"`, `/translation="MDLS..."`, `/db_xref="taxon:9606"`).

9. **ORIGIN** — The actual nucleotide sequence, written 60 bases per line with a 9-character left-margin line counter.

10. **`//`** — The end-of-record delimiter.

**Example (truncated):**

```
LOCUS       NM_007294              5592 bp    mRNA    linear   PRI 15-OCT-2024
DEFINITION  Homo sapiens BRCA1 mRNA, complete cds.
ACCESSION   NM_007294
VERSION     NM_007294.4
KEYWORDS    breast cancer; tumor suppressor.
SOURCE      Homo sapiens (human)
  ORGANISM  Homo sapiens
            Eukaryota; Metazoa; Chordata; ... Primates; Hominidae; Homo.
REFERENCE   1  (bases 1 to 5592)
  AUTHORS   Hall JM, King MC.
  TITLE     "BRCA1..."
  JOURNAL   Science 250:1684-1689(1990)
FEATURES             Location/Qualifiers
     source          1..5592
                     /organism="Homo sapiens"
                     /mol_type="mRNA"
     gene            1..5592
                     /gene="BRCA1"
     CDS             120..5592
                     /gene="BRCA1"
                     /product="breast cancer type 1 susceptibility protein"
                     /protein_id="NP_009225.1"
                     /translation="MDLSALRVEEVQNVINAMQKILECPICLELIKEPVSTKCD..."
ORIGIN      
        1 atggatttat ctgctcttcg cgttgaagaa gtacaaaatg tcattaatgc tatgcagaaa
       61 atcttagagt gtcccatctg tctggagttg atcaaggaac ctgtctccac aaagtgtgac
//
```

*Examiner Tip:* Mention at least **8 fields** for full marks. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Describe the **International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration (INSDC)** and its three partner databases. How is data synchronized between them? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The **International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration (INSDC)** is a long-standing partnership among three major nucleotide sequence databases, established to provide a unified, global public repository of DNA/RNA sequences.

**The three partners are:**

1. **GenBank** — maintained by NCBI (USA, NIH/NLM).
2. **European Nucleotide Archive (ENA)** — maintained by EMBL-EBI (European Bioinformatics Institute, Hinxton, UK).
3. **DNA Data Bank of Japan (DDBJ)** — maintained by the National Institute of Genetics, Mishima, Japan.

**Data synchronization mechanism:**

- A researcher can submit a sequence to **any one** of the three databases.
- Each database performs validation and assigns its own unique accession prefix (e.g., GenBank uses 1–2 letter + 5–6 digit codes; ENA uses 2–4 letter codes; DDBJ uses 5-character codes).
- The records are then **exchanged daily** among the three centers. This means a sequence submitted to DDBJ in Tokyo will appear in GenBank (USA) and ENA (UK) within **24 hours**.
- A unified **feature table syntax** is enforced across all three, ensuring that any record can be read by all software regardless of source.
- The collaboration also mandates free, unrestricted public access to all submitted data.

**Significance:** Avoids redundancy, ensures comprehensive coverage, provides redundancy and backup, and gives the global scientific community free access to a single, unified data resource.

*Examiner Tip:* Don't forget to mention **daily exchange** and **free public access** as key features. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the **Accession Number** system in GenBank. Distinguish between **Accession**, **Version**, and **GI** numbers with examples. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The **Accession Number** is the unique, permanent, stable identifier assigned to a single record in GenBank (or any INSDC database). It is assigned at the time of submission and **never changes**, even if the underlying sequence is later updated or the record is merged.

**Types of identifiers:**

| Identifier | Example | Meaning | Stability |
|------------|---------|---------|-----------|
| **Accession** | `NM_007294` | A unique label for the record. Two letters + 5–6 digits (e.g., `NM_` for RefSeq mRNA, `NP_` for protein, `M12345` for historical). | **Permanent** |
| **Version** | `NM_007294.4` | Accession + dot + integer. Increments **only** when the sequence itself is updated. | **Updated with sequence** |
| **GI** | `455981234` | NCBI's older internal integer ID. Used internally by Entrez. | **Deprecated (2023+)** |

**Why three different IDs?**
- The **accession** is the **citable** ID used in publications.
- The **version** lets a reader know whether they are looking at the original or an updated sequence.
- The **GI** was historically used by NCBI's internal search index; it is now gradually being retired in favor of accession.version.

**Example:** A paper citing *"GenBank accession NM_007294"* refers to the record; *"NM_007294.4"* specifically means the **4th revision** of that record.

*Examiner Tip:* Always give a clear example. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Write a short note on the **different divisions of GenBank** and mention the **GenBank submission tools**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Divisions of GenBank:**

GenBank is partitioned into multiple **divisions** for faster retrieval and management. These are of two types:

**A. Organism-based divisions (each is a 3-letter code in the LOCUS line):**

| Code | Division | Code | Division |
|------|----------|------|----------|
| PRI | Primate | PLN | Plant |
| ROD | Rodent | BCT | Bacterial |
| MAM | Other Mammal | VRL | Viral |
| VRT | Other Vertebrate | PHG | Bacteriophage |
| INV | Invertebrate | SYN | Synthetic |

**B. Data-type divisions:**

- **EST** — Expressed Sequence Tags (single-pass cDNA reads)
- **GSS** — Genome Survey Sequences
- **HTG** — High-Throughput Genomic (unfinished)
- **STS** — Sequence Tagged Sites
- **PAT** — Patent sequences
- **UNA** — Unannotated sequences

**Submission Tools:**

1. **BankIt** — A web-based submission tool (https://submit.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/bankit/) suitable for small numbers of sequences.
2. **Sequin** — A stand-alone desktop application (legacy) for submitting multiple sequences with rich annotation.
3. **tbl2asn** — A command-line tool for preparing large-scale, high-throughput submissions (used for whole-genome annotation).

Each submission tool produces a **.sqn** file validated by NCBI's `valseq` validator before acceptance.

*Examiner Tip:* Always write at least **5 divisions** and mention all 3 submission tools. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Confusing accession and version numbers** — they are not the same. Marks are lost for this. Always show the **dot (.)** in the version.
> - **Forgetting INSDC members** — Examiners expect all **three partners** (GenBank, ENA, DDBJ) and the **daily exchange** clause.
> - **Mixing up GenBank and RefSeq** — GenBank is the user-submitted database; RefSeq is the **curated, non-redundant** derivative generated by NCBI. They are **not** the same, though RefSeq records use a similar accession scheme (`NM_`, `NP_`, `XM_`, `XP_`).
> - **Omitting the daily exchange clause** in INSDC questions — this is a high-frequency expected phrase in KTU answers.
> - **Confusing FASTA's `>` symbol with GenBank's `//` terminator** — these belong to different file formats.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Exam-Ready):**

- **GenBank** is the **NCBI/NIH** public nucleotide sequence database, part of the **INSDC** along with **ENA (EBI)** and **DDBJ**.
- Data is **synchronized daily** among the three INSDC partners; a submission to any one appears in all three.
- **Format options:** GenBank flat file (rich annotation) vs. FASTA (sequence only, `>` header).
- **Identifier hierarchy:** **Accession** (stable, e.g., `NM_007294`) ⊃ **Version** (e.g., `NM_007294.4`, increments on update); **GI** is the legacy numeric ID.
- **RefSeq accessions** (curated): `NM_` mRNA, `NR_` ncRNA, `NP_` protein, `XM_` predicted mRNA, `XP_` predicted protein.
- **GenBank divisions** are organism-based (PRI, ROD, MAM, VRT, INV, PLN, BCT, VRL, PHG, SYN) and data-type based (EST, GSS, HTG, STS, PAT, UNA).
- **Flat file structure:** LOCUS → DEFINITION → ACCESSION → VERSION → KEYWORDS → SOURCE → ORGANISM → REFERENCE → FEATURES → ORIGIN → `//`.
- **FEATURES table** contains feature keys (gene, CDS, mRNA, exon, intron) and qualifiers (`/gene`, `/product`, `/protein_id`, `/translation`, `/db_xref`).
- **Submission tools:** **BankIt** (web, small submissions), **Sequin** (desktop, legacy), **tbl2asn** (command-line, large-scale).
- **Retrieval tools:** **Entrez** (text search), **BLAST** (similarity search), **batch Entrez** (programmatic, multiple IDs).
- **Cross-references** link GenBank to **PubMed, Taxonomy, Gene, Protein, OMIM, Conserved Domains, PubChem**.
- **Growth metric:** GenBank has crossed **4 trillion bases**; sequences **double every ~18 months**.
- **Updates:** Every 2 months as a full release; daily incremental updates; full releases announced in the `gbrelease.txt` file.
- **BLAST** (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool) is a sequence similarity search program maintained by NCBI that uses GenBank as its primary database.
- **Entrez** is the cross-database retrieval system that allows simultaneous searching of GenBank, PubMed, Taxonomy, Protein, and other NCBI resources.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NCBI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT604 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** NCBI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NCBI (National Center for Biotechnology Information)** is a branch of the **United States National Library of Medicine (NLM)**, which itself is a constituent institute of the **National Institutes of Health (NIH)**. It was established on **November 4, 1988**, through the Public Law 100–607, with the legislative mandate to develop cutting-edge information systems dedicated to the fields of molecular biology, computational genetics, and biomedical data science.

NCBI serves as the world's preeminent public repository and analytical engine for **biomedical and genomic information**. Its core mandate, as per its congressional charter, is fourfold:

1. To create automated systems for storing and analysing knowledge related to molecular biology, biochemistry, and genetics.
2. To perform research into advanced computational methods for analysing the structure and function of biologically significant molecules.
3. To facilitate the global dissemination of biomedical information through robust public databases.
4. To develop and distribute software tools (such as **BLAST**, **BLAST+**, **SRA Toolkit**) for processing and interpreting genomic, proteomic, and bibliographic data.

The primary public portal is accessible at `https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov`, which acts as a unified gateway to over **40 interconnected databases** and **computational tools**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Founders & Leadership**: NCBI was founded by Senator **Claude Pepper** (Florida). Its first Director was **Dr. David J. Lipman**, who served from 1989 to 2017. The current Director is **Dr. James M. Ostell**, who has been instrumental in maintaining the **GenBank**, **BLAST**, and **PubMed** infrastructure that today serves **>5 million daily users** globally.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Library + Workshop + Factory" Analogy for NCBI**

Imagine the world of molecular biology as a vast, ever-expanding universe of **biological blueprints, recipes, and instruction manuals**:

| Real-World Entity | Biological Equivalent | NCBI Component |
|---|---|---|
| **A National Library** | The published biological literature | **PubMed / PubMed Central (PMC)** |
| **A Federal Archives / Record Vault** | Experimentally validated DNA and protein sequences | **GenBank**, **RefSeq**, **dbSNP** |
| **A City-Wide Search Engine (like Google)** | A federated cross-database search across biological repositories | **Entrez** |
| **A Master Locksmith / Translator** | Sequence alignment and pattern matching tools | **BLAST** / **BLAST+** |
| **An Engineering Workshop** | Software and computational pipelines for researchers | **E-utilities**, **SRA Toolkit**, **BLAST+ suite** |
| **A 3D Blueprint Viewer** | Macromolecular structural data of proteins and nucleic acids | **Molecular Modeling Database (MMDB) / NCBI Structure** |

So, in simple English: **NCBI is the world's largest free biological library + workshop combined.** It not only *stores* the blueprints (DNA, RNA, protein sequences and 3D structures) but also provides the *tools* (BLAST, Entrez, E-utilities) to look them up, compare them, and discover new biological insights.

---

## 1.3 Mission, Vision, and Strategic Goals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Under Outcome-Based Education (OBE), students are expected to articulate the *role of NCBI as a public-domain data clearinghouse* for molecular biology. The official NIH mission statement of NCBI is:

> *"To develop new information technologies to aid in the understanding of fundamental molecular and genetic processes that control health and disease."*

This mission is operationalised through:

- **FAIR Data Principles** — Findability, Accessibility, Interoperability, and Reusability of all hosted datasets.
- **Open Access Mandate** — All data submitted to NCBI is **publicly accessible** within the *Standard Release Delay* (typically **immediate for most records**, or held confidential until the associated manuscript is published).
- **International Collaboration** — NCBI participates in the **International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration (INSDC)** alongside **EMBL-EBI (UK)** and **DDBJ (Japan)**, ensuring a synchronized, three-mirror global archive.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Organizational Hierarchy of NCBI

The hierarchical structure of NCBI, traced from the U.S. federal government down to its individual databases, is given below:

1. **U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)**
   - The cabinet-level federal department.
2. **National Institutes of Health (NIH)** — *Bethesda, Maryland, USA*
   - The primary U.S. government agency responsible for biomedical and public-health research.
3. **National Library of Medicine (NLM)** — *Bethesda, Maryland, USA*
   - The world's largest biomedical library; the parent body of NCBI.
4. **National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI)**
   - A specialized research center within NLM.
5. **Eight Major Branches / Divisions of NCBI**:
   - **Information Resources Branch (IRB)**
   - **Computational Biology Branch (CBB)**
   - **Information Engineering Branch (IEB)**
   - **GenBank / Sequence Database Group**
   - **Literature Database Group (PubMed)**
   - **Scientific Publishing Group**
   - **Office of the Director**
   - **Outreach & Education Branch**

## 2.2 The Major NCBI Databases — A Structured Catalogue

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus-Aligned Categorisation** — For KTU examination purposes, the major NCBI databases can be classified into **six functional categories**:

### Category 1: Literature Databases

| Database | Full Form | Content | Typical Query |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PubMed** | Public Medicine | Biomedical literature abstracts (>36 million citations) | Author, keyword, MeSH term |
| **PMC** | PubMed Central | Full-text biomedical literature | Open-access journals |
| **Bookshelf** | NCBI Bookshelf | Free biomedical textbooks | Topic, chapter |

### Category 2: Nucleotide Sequence Databases

| Database | Content | Submission Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **GenBank** | Annotated DNA/RNA sequences (>250 billion bases) | BankIt, Sequin, tbl2asn |
| **RefSeq** | Reference Sequences (curated, non-redundant) | Direct submission + curation |
| **dbSNP** | Single Nucleotide Polymorphisms & small variations | Submission via dbSNP portal |
| **dbEST** | Expressed Sequence Tags | EST division of GenBank |
| **dbGSS** | Genome Survey Sequences | GSS division of GenBank |
| **SRA** | Sequence Read Archive (NGS raw reads) | SRA Toolkit |

### Category 3: Protein Databases

| Database | Content |
|---|---|
| **GenPept** | Translated GenBank coding regions |
| **RefSeq (NP_)** | Reference protein sequences |
| **UniProt (collaborative)** | Curated protein knowledgebase (EBI–SIB–PIR) |
| **Conserved Domains (CDD)** | Conserved domain alignments |
| **Protein Clusters** | Related protein sequence sets |

### Category 4: Structure Databases

| Database | Content |
|---|---|
| **MMDB** | Molecular Modeling Database (links to PDB) |
| **Cn3D** | 3D structure viewer (helper application) |

### Category 5: Genome & Gene Catalogs

| Database | Content |
|---|---|
| **Gene** | Gene-specific information across species |
| **Genome** | Whole-genome assemblies |
| **NCBI Map Viewer** | Chromosomal maps |

### Category 6: Expression & Epigenomics

| Database | Content |
|---|---|
| **GEO** | Gene Expression Omnibus |
| **GEO Profiles** | Expression profiles by gene |
| **Epigenomics** | Epigenomic data tracks |

---

## 2.3 The Entrez Cross-Database Search System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Entrez is the unified search and retrieval engine that federates all NCBI databases.** It uses **text indexing (e.g., inverted indices)** and **pre-computed links** between records to provide *cross-database* navigation.

The formula governing Entrez's neighbour/link computation is the **link frequency-weighted formula**:

$$
\text{Link Score}(A \rightarrow B) = \frac{N(A \cap B)}{\min(N(A), N(B))} \times \text{Boost Factor}
$$

where $N(X)$ is the number of documents in database $X$ linked from the Entrez document $A$. This formula produces the **"Related Sequences"** or **"Find related data"** links seen on every NCBI record page.

### The 9 Primary Entrez Databases (E-utilities accessible)

1. **PubMed** — Literature
2. **Nucleotide** — GenBank + RefSeq DNA/RNA
3. **Protein** — GenPept + RefSeq proteins
4. **Genome** — Whole genomes
5. **Structure** — MMDB / PDB
6. **Taxonomy** — Organism classification
7. **Gene** — Gene catalog
8. **OMIM** — Mendelian Inheritance in Man
9. **Books** — NCBI Bookshelf

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Identifier Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Accession Number Format Decoder** — A frequently tested KTU concept. Every record in GenBank/RefSeq/UniProt is identified by a unique, **stable accession number**:

| Prefix | Database | Example | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NM_** | RefSeq mRNA | `NM_007294.4` | BRCA1 mRNA, version 4 |
| **NR_** | RefSeq ncRNA | `NR_003051.4` | Non-coding RNA |
| **NP_** | RefSeq Protein | `NP_009225.1` | Protein product |
| **NG_** | RefSeq Genomic | `NG_005905.2` | Genomic region |
| **NC_** | RefSeq Chromosome | `NC_000001.11` | Chromosome 1 assembly |
| **XM_** | RefSeq predicted mRNA | `XM_005249261.1` | Predicted model |
| **XR_** | RefSeq predicted ncRNA | `XR_001743545.1` | Predicted ncRNA |
| **2 letters + 6 digits** | GenBank | `AF123456` | Original GenBank record |
| **1 letter + 5 digits** | GenBank | `U12345` | Older GenBank records |
| **WP_** | RefSeq WP | `WP_000000001.1` | Prokaryotic proteins |

The version suffix (`.4` in `NM_007294.4`) is incremented each time the **RefSeq curation is updated**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Biomedical Utility

- **Drug Discovery**: NCBI's BLAST and PubChem data are foundational for *in silico* drug target identification.
- **Personalized Medicine**: ClinVar (NCBI) and dbSNP are used by hospitals to interpret patient genomic variants.
- **Synthetic Biology**: GenBank provides the public sequence repository that synthetic biologists (e.g., the BioBricks Foundation) draw from.
- **Epidemiology**: NCBI's SRA and GenBank host the SARS-CoV-2 reference genome (`NC_045512.2`) and all globally submitted variants — a textbook case of open data enabling pandemic response.
- **Agricultural Biotechnology**: dbEST hosts crop ESTs that engineers use to identify agronomically important genes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Entrez Link Score for "Find Related Records"

The Entrez system produces a ranked list of related records in any given database. The derivation proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 — Define the document set in the linked database.**
Let $A$ be a query document (record) in database $\mathcal{D}_A$. The number of linked records in target database $\mathcal{D}_B$ is given by the set:

$$
L(A) = \{ b \in \mathcal{D}_B \mid (A, b) \in E \}
$$

where $E$ is the set of pre-computed Entrez links (edges in the link graph).

**Step 2 — Count raw links.**

$$
N_{\text{links}} = \vert L(A) \vert
$$

This is the total number of records in $\mathcal{D}_B$ linked from $A$. The vertical bar in $\vert L(A) \vert$ denotes the *cardinality* of the link set.

**Step 3 — Apply the Jaccard-style similarity weighting.**
To rank *which* of the linked records is most relevant, Entrez uses an inverse document-frequency (IDF) inspired weight:

$$
\text{Score}(A, b) = \sum_{t \in b} \frac{\text{freq}(t, b) \cdot \log\left(\frac{\vert \mathcal{D}_B \vert}{\text{df}(t) + 1}\right)}{\text{normaliser}(b)}
$$

where $\text{freq}(t, b)$ is the term frequency of token $t$ in record $b$, and $\text{df}(t)$ is the number of documents containing $t$.

**Step 4 — Final composite score.**
The score displayed on the NCBI web page ("See 3 related records" with a numerical score) combines the link weight with the text-relevance score:

$$
\text{FinalScore}(A, b) = \alpha \cdot \text{LinkScore}(A, b) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \text{TextScore}(A, b)
$$

where $\alpha$ is a tunable weighting constant (typically $\alpha \approx 0.4$ for most databases). Records with the highest $\text{FinalScore}$ are shown first under the "Related Records" list.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: NCBI Tools vs Database vs Use-Case

| NCBI Tool | Pairs With Database | Primary Use | Output Format |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BLAST (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool)** | GenBank, RefSeq, PDB | Find regions of local similarity between sequences | Ranked hit list + pairwise alignments |
| **BLAST+ (NCBI C++ Toolkit)** | All sequence DBs | Command-line BLAST | XML / tabular text |
| **Primer-BLAST** | RefSeq mRNA | Design PCR primers with specificity check | Primer sequences |
| **ORF Finder** | GenBank nucleotide | Find open reading frames | Translated ORFs |
| **VecScreen** | GenBank | Detect vector contamination | Annotated sequence |
| **SRA Toolkit** | SRA | Convert NGS raw data formats | FASTQ, SAM |
| **Entrez / E-utilities** | All Entrez DBs | Programmatic querying | XML, JSON, text |
| **CD-Search (RPS-BLAST)** | CDD (Conserved Domains) | Identify conserved domains in a protein | Domain architecture diagram |
| **COBALT** | Protein sequences | Multiple alignment tool | Phylogenetic-style alignment |
| **Splign / Spidey** | mRNA + Genomic | Align transcripts to genomic DNA | Alignment summary |
| **SNPedia / ClinVar** | dbSNP / OMIM | Clinical variant interpretation | Variant clinical significance |
| **PubChem BioAssay** | PubChem Substance | High-throughput screening data | Activity outcome table |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Interpreting a GenBank Accession

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample accession**: `NM_007294.4`

| Component | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Prefix | `NM_` | **RefSeq mRNA** (curated reference sequence) |
| Numeric ID | `007294` | Unique numeric identifier within RefSeq |
| Version | `.4` | This is the **4th curated version** of the record |

**Linked records (auto-generated by Entrez):**

- Corresponding **protein** → `NP_009225.1` (translated from the open reading frame).
- Corresponding **genomic DNA** → `NG_005905.2`.
- Associated **gene record** → `Gene ID: 672` (BRCA1 in humans).
- Associated **PubMed citations** → listed via Gene → Bibliography link.

---

## 3.4 Data Submission Workflow (GenBank via BankIt)

The canonical submission pathway uses the web-based tool **BankIt** (for sequences <200 kb) or **Sequin** (for larger / annotated submissions). The submission lifecycle proceeds through six discrete steps:

1. **Account Login** — Submitter registers at NCBI.
2. **Nucleotide FASTA Input** — Raw sequence in FASTA format is uploaded.
3. **Annotation** — The submitter adds source organism, gene names, coding-region coordinates, and protein translations.
4. **Reference / Publication Link** — Optional PubMed or DOI of associated manuscript.
5. **Review** — Submitter verifies the record before clicking *Submit*.
6. **Public Release** — Either immediate release or **"Hold-Until-Published"** (HUP) status until the linked paper appears in PubMed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note**: The "Hold-Until-Published" mechanism is what allows genome sequencing centres to submit large-scale data without losing priority for publication.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Organizational Architecture of NCBI

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HHS["U.S. Department of Health and Human Services HHS"]
    NIH["National Institutes of Health NIH"]
    NLM["National Library of Medicine NLM"]
    NCBI["National Center for Biotechnology Information NCBI"]
    DIR["Office of the Director"]
    IRB["Information Resources Branch IRB"]
    CBB["Computational Biology Branch CBB"]
    IEB["Information Engineering Branch IEB"]

    LIT["Literature Group PubMed"]
    PUB["Scientific Publishing Group"]

    DB1["PubMed"]
    DB2["GenBank"]
    DB3["RefSeq"]
    DB4["dbSNP"]
    DB5["SRA"]
    DB6["MMDB"]
    DB7["Gene"]
    DB8["GEO"]

    TOOL1["BLAST"]
    TOOL2["Entrez E-utilities"]
    TOOL3["Primer-BLAST"]
    TOOL4["CD-Search"]

    HHS --> NIH --> NLM --> NCBI
    NCBI --> DIR
    NCBI --> IRB
    NCBI --> CBB
    NCBI --> IEB
    NCBI --> GBK
    NCBI --> LIT
    NCBI --> PUB
    NCBI --> OUT

    LIT --> DB1

    GBK --> DB3
    GBK --> DB4

    CBB --> DB8

    IRB --> TOOL1
    IEB --> TOOL2
    IRB --> TOOL3
    CBB --> TOOL4

    DB1 --> TOOL2
    DB2 --> TOOL1
    DB2 --> TOOL2
    DB7 --> TOOL2

```

## 4.2 Entrez Cross-Database Linking Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    USER["Researcher / Browser"]
    ENT["Entrez Federated Search Engine"]
    DBPM["PubMed Literature"]
    DBNT["Nucleotide GenBank RefSeq"]
    DBPR["Protein GenPept RefSeq"]
    DBGN["Gene Catalog"]
    DBST["Structure MMDB PDB"]
    DBTX["Taxonomy"]
    DBOM["OMIM"]
    DBBO["Bookshelf"]
    DBGP["Genome"]

    USER --> ENT
    ENT --> DBPM
    ENT --> DBNT
    ENT --> DBPR
    ENT --> DBGN
    ENT --> DBST
    ENT --> DBTX
    ENT --> DBOM
    ENT --> DBBO
    ENT --> DBGP

    DBPM -.->|Links via PMIDs| DBNT
    DBNT -.->|Translates| DBPR
    DBNT -.->|Gene mentions| DBGN
    DBPR -.->|3D structure| DBST
    DBGN -.->|Organism| DBTX
    DBGN -.->|Disease| DBOM
    DBNT -.->|Chromosome| DBGP
```

## 4.3 Data Submission Lifecycle (GenBank via BankIt)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Researcher Sequences DNA in Lab"]
    S2["Prepare FASTA and Annotation"]
    S3["Login to NCBI Submission Portal"]
    S4["Choose BankIt or Sequin"]
    S5["Upload Sequence and Metadata"]
    S6["Validate via BankIt Preview"]
    S7{"Release Strategy"}
    S8["Immediate Public Release"]
    S9["Hold Until Published HUP"]
    S10["GenBank Curator Review"]
    S11["Accession Number Issued"]
    S12["Record Available in Entrez Nucleotide"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8 --> S10 --> S11 --> S12
    S7 --> S9 --> S10 --> S11 --> S12
```

## 4.4 RefSeq vs GenBank — Conceptual Distinction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INSDC["INSDC International Nucleotide Database Collaboration"]
        GB["GenBank NCBI USA"]
        EMBL["EMBL EBI UK"]
        DDBJ["DDBJ Japan"]
    end

    subgraph REFS["RefSeq Reference Sequences"]
        RF1["NM NR NP NG NC XM XR"]
        RF2["Curated, Non-redundant, Stable"]
    end

    INSDC -->|Original submissions| REFS
    REFS -->|Source data| ENT["Entrez Unified Search"]

    INSDC --> ENT

    GB -.->|Synchronized mirror| EMBL
    EMBL -.->|Synchronized mirror| DDBJ
    DDBJ -.->|Synchronized mirror| GB
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define NCBI. Mention its parent organizations. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

NCBI stands for **National Center for Biotechnology Information**. It is a branch of the **National Library of Medicine (NLM)**, which is itself a part of the **National Institutes of Health (NIH)**, under the **U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)**. It was established in **1988** and is headquartered in **Bethesda, Maryland, USA**. NCBI maintains public databases such as GenBank, PubMed, dbSNP, and RefSeq, and develops software tools like BLAST, Entrez, and Primer-BLAST.

### Question 2: What is an Accession Number? Give an example of a RefSeq accession and explain each component. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

An **Accession Number** is a unique, stable, alphanumeric identifier permanently assigned to each record in a public biological database (GenBank, RefSeq, UniProt, etc.). It does not change even when the underlying record is updated; instead a version suffix is appended.

**Example**: `NM_007294.4`

- `NM_` → indicates the record is a **RefSeq curated mRNA** sequence.
- `007294` → unique six-digit identifier within the RefSeq collection.
- `.4` → version number, incremented each time the record is updated.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Following the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, two independent alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) are provided. Each contains two 7-mark sub-parts. Students answer **any one** in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the organizational hierarchy of NCBI. Trace its position within the U.S. federal structure down to its eight major branches. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The hierarchical structure is as follows:

1. **U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)** — the federal cabinet-level ministry.
2. **National Institutes of Health (NIH)** — under HHS; primary agency for biomedical research.
3. **National Library of Medicine (NLM)** — the world's largest medical library; under NIH.
4. **NCBI** — established 1988 under NLM by Public Law 100–607.

NCBI operates through **eight divisions**:

1. Office of the Director
2. Information Resources Branch (IRB)
3. Computational Biology Branch (CBB)
4. Information Engineering Branch (IEB)
5. GenBank / Sequence Database Group
6. Literature Database Group (managing PubMed)
7. Scientific Publishing Group
8. Outreach & Education Branch

The diagram (see SECTION 4.1 above) depicts this hierarchy. NCBI's mission is to advance molecular biology and biomedical research through open data and computational tools.

#### (b) List and briefly describe any **six major NCBI databases** with their functional classification. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

| # | Database | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PubMed** | Literature | >36 million citations for biomedical literature; abstracts from MEDLINE. |
| 2 | **GenBank** | Nucleotide | Annotated collection of publicly available DNA sequences (>250 billion bases). |
| 3 | **RefSeq** | Reference Sequence | Curated, non-redundant, high-quality reference sequences (NM_, NR_, NP_, NC_). |
| 4 | **dbSNP** | Variation | Catalogue of single nucleotide polymorphisms and short genetic variations. |
| 5 | **SRA** | NGS Data | Sequence Read Archive — raw reads from next-generation sequencing platforms. |
| 6 | **Gene** | Catalog | Gene-specific information consolidated across organisms. |

Other supporting databases include **UniProt** (proteins, hosted jointly with EBI/SIB/PIR), **MMDB** (3D structures), **GEO** (expression data), **Taxonomy** (organism classification), and **OMIM** (Mendelian disorders).

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) What is Entrez? Explain its role as a cross-database federated search engine. List the nine primary Entrez databases. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Entrez** is NCBI's **federated, cross-database search engine** that enables simultaneous querying of multiple biological databases through a single interface (web or programmatic via E-utilities).

**Features of Entrez:**

- Provides **unified query syntax** across databases (e.g., `brca1 AND human[orgn]`).
- Computes **pre-computed links** ("neighbors") between records across databases using a similarity weighting formula (see SECTION 3.1).
- Returns results in **summary, XML, or JSON** formats for programmatic access.
- Supports **history** (saving queries to NCBI server for later use).

**The Nine Primary Entrez Databases:**

1. PubMed (literature)
2. Nucleotide (DNA/RNA)
3. Protein
4. Genome
5. Structure (MMDB)
6. Taxonomy
7. Gene
8. OMIM
9. Books (Bookshelf)

#### (b) Explain **BLAST** as the most widely used NCBI tool. Differentiate between its main variants (BLASTN, BLASTP, BLASTX, TBLASTN, TBLASTX). (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**BLAST (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool)** is NCBI's primary sequence-similarity search algorithm. It finds regions of local similarity between a query sequence and a database of sequences, returning statistically significant alignments.

**Algorithm in brief**: BLAST heuristically identifies "high-scoring segment pairs" (HSPs) using a seed-and-extend strategy, replacing the slower dynamic programming of Smith–Waterman.

**Main Variants:**

| Variant | Query | Database | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BLASTN** | Nucleotide | Nucleotide | DNA vs DNA similarity |
| **BLASTP** | Protein | Protein | Protein homology search |
| **BLASTX** | Nucleotide (translated in 6 frames) | Protein | Find protein homologs of an unknown DNA |
| **TBLASTN** | Protein | Nucleotide (translated in 6 frames) | Map a protein to the underlying DNA |
| **TBLASTX** | Nucleotide (translated 6 frames) | Nucleotide (translated 6 frames) | Compare two DNA sequences at the protein level (e.g., EST vs EST) |

Other specialized tools include **MegaBLAST** (fast nucleotide comparison), **PSI-BLAST** (position-specific iterated BLAST for distant homology), and **PHI-BLAST** (pattern-hit-initiated BLAST).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing GenBank with RefSeq.** GenBank is a *primary archive* (author-submitted, no curation), whereas RefSeq is *curated and non-redundant*. Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by writing both interchangeably.
> 2. **Mis-spelling "Entrez" as "Entre" or "Entrée"** — a frequent sub-question trap.
> 3. **Confusing PubMed with PubMed Central (PMC).** PubMed contains *abstracts*; PMC contains *full-text* papers. Many students write one for the other.
> 4. **Forgetting the parent body.** Students often write "NCBI is under NIH" — the *correct* hierarchy is **HHS → NIH → NLM → NCBI**. Examiners award full marks only when the full chain is presented.
> 5. **Incorrect accession prefixes.** Writing `NM_` for a *protein* (which should be `NP_`) costs a mark in any 7-mark sub-question.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NCBI** = National Center for Biotechnology Information, established **1988** in **Bethesda, MD**, USA.
- Parent chain: **HHS → NIH → NLM → NCBI**.
- **Mission**: develop information technologies to understand molecular and genetic processes in health and disease.
- **Major databases**: PubMed, GenBank, RefSeq, dbSNP, SRA, Gene, Protein, GEO, MMDB, Taxonomy, OMIM, Books.
- **Key tools**: BLAST (with variants BLASTN, BLASTP, BLASTX, TBLASTN, TBLASTX), Entrez, E-utilities, Primer-BLAST, ORF Finder, CD-Search, SRA Toolkit.
- **RefSeq prefixes**: `NM_` (mRNA), `NR_` (ncRNA), `NP_` (protein), `NG_` (genomic), `NC_` (chromosome), `XM_/XR_` (predicted).
- **GenBank prefixes**: 2-letter + 6 digits (e.g., `AF123456`), maintained as part of **INSDC** with **EMBL-EBI** and **DDBJ**.
- **Entrez** is the **federated cross-database search** engine using **pre-computed links** for the "Find related records" feature.
- **Submission tools**: BankIt (web) and Sequin (desktop) for sequence submission.
- **BLAST variants** operate across query and database types (nucleotide vs protein) in **5 main flavours**.
- **Open data policy**: All NCBI data is **publicly accessible**, ensuring global equity in biomedical research.
- The **SARS-CoV-2 reference genome** (`NC_045512.2`) is the canonical real-world example of NCBI's global role in public health.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DDBJ
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT604 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** DDBJ

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**DDBJ (DNA Data Bank of Japan)** is the sole public-domain nucleotide sequence data repository in Asia, officially recognized as a primary member of the **International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration (INSDC)**. Operated by the **National Institute of Genetics (NIG)**, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT), Japan, DDBJ was established in **1986** as a foundational pillar of international bioinformatics infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **INSDC Tripartite Alliance** (Exam Favorite):
> DDBJ, **GenBank (NCBI, USA)**, and **ENA (EBI, UK)** are the three pillars of the INSDC. They exchange data on a **daily basis** to maintain global synchronization of all public nucleotide records.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the world of biological sequences as a massive global postal service. Every time a scientist in Tokyo, Mumbai, or Berlin discovers a new DNA sequence, they need a "mailbox" to deposit it permanently, get a unique tracking number, and allow anyone in the world to retrieve a copy.

- **DDBJ** = The official Japanese branch of this global postal service.
- **GenBank** = The American branch.
- **ENA** = The European branch.

All three are simply different **regional windows** into the **same single underlying database**. When you submit a sequence to DDBJ, it automatically becomes visible on GenBank and ENA the very next day.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Year of Establishment:** **1986**
> - **Host Institute:** **National Institute of Genetics (NIG), Mishima, Japan**
> - **Governing Body:** MEXT, Japan
> - **Primary Collaborators:** NCBI (USA) and EBI (UK)
> - **Daily Data Synchronization:** Yes (every 24 hours)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of DDBJ

DDBJ is not a single monolithic database. It is an integrated **Bioinformatics Center** comprising several interconnected sub-databases:

1. **DDBJ (Traditional Sequence Database):** Stores annotated nucleotide sequences (similar to GenBank's traditional records).
2. **DRA (DDBJ Sequence Read Archive):** Archives raw next-generation sequencing (NGS) reads.
3. **GEA (Genotype and phenotype Entity Archive):** Stores genotype/phenotype association data.
4. **BioProject & BioSample:** Project-level and sample-level metadata containers.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Data Submission Workflow

**Step 1 — Author Initiates Submission:** The researcher prepares a sequence file (FASTA + annotation table) and gathers metadata (source organism, reference paper, gene function).

**Step 2 — Submission Tool Selection:** DDBJ provides a web-based portal called **NSSS (Nucleotide Sequence Submission System)**. For high-throughput submissions, **MSS (Mass Submission System)** is used via command-line.

**Step 3 — Validation & Annotation:** The submission is processed through automated validation checks (vector contamination screening, ORF prediction, taxonomy validation).

**Step 4 — Accession Number Assignment:** Upon successful validation, DDBJ issues a **unique permanent accession number** (the "tracking ID" analogy from earlier).

**Step 5 — Daily Exchange with INSDC Partners:** The newly accepted record is propagated to GenBank and ENA within 24 hours, becoming part of the global pool.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Table

| Parameter | DDBJ Specification | Engineering/Bioinformatics Utility |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Established** | 1986 | Asia's first public DNA repository |
| **Host Institute** | NIG, Mishima | Japan's flagship genetics research center |
| **Parent Organization** | MEXT (Japan) | Government-funded research mandate |
| **INSDC Partners** | GenBank (NCBI), ENA (EBI) | Daily mirror synchronization |
| **Submission Portal** | NSSS / MSS | Web-based & CLI-based pipelines |
| **NGS Repository** | DRA (DDBJ Sequence Read Archive) | Stores raw short reads (BAM/SRA format) |
| **Accession Prefix** | **Single-letter prefix** (e.g., `AB`, `LC`, `AP`) | 2–6 alphanumeric characters |
| **Example Accession** | **AB123456** | Permanent, immutable identifier |
| **Data Format** | Flatfile (INSDC standard) | Structured multi-key annotation blocks |
| **Update Frequency** | Daily exchange | Real-time global synchronization |

### 2.4 Accession Number Format (Decoding the ID)

DDBJ accession numbers follow a precise structural pattern governed by INSDC:

$$
\text{Accession ID} = \underbrace{1 \text{ or } 2 \text{ letters}}_{\text{Prefix}} + \underbrace{5 \text{ or } 6 \text{ digits}}_{\text{Sequential ID}}
$$

For example: **AB123456** → Prefix `AB` (assigned to a specific submission type/dataset) + 6-digit unique number `123456`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The accession number is **permanent and immutable**. Even if the sequence annotation is later updated, the accession number never changes. This is critical for citation in research papers.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical R&D:** Japanese pharma companies use DDBJ to store proprietary pathogen sequences.
- **Agricultural Genomics:** Rice genome submissions (Japan leads rice genomics) are archived here.
- **Population Genomics:** Asian population genetic studies rely on DDBJ for reference haplotypes.
- **NGS Data Repositories:** DRA serves as Asia's equivalent of NCBI's SRA for high-throughput sequencing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: INSDC Data Exchange Protocol

The synchronization between DDBJ, GenBank, and ENA is not arbitrary — it follows a deterministic **daily exchange protocol**. Let us formally derive the synchronization cycle:

**Given:**
- $T_{\text{submission}}$ = Time of sequence submission to DDBJ
- $T_{\text{validation}}$ = Time taken for validation (typically 1–2 working days)
- $T_{\text{propagation}}$ = Time taken for daily exchange (24-hour window)

**Step 1 — Submission Time:**
The author submits the sequence to DDBJ at $T_{\text{submission}}$.

**Step 2 — Validation Window:**
The sequence undergoes QC and annotation review. A typical validated record gets an accession number at:

$$
T_{\text{accession}} = T_{\text{submission}} + T_{\text{validation}}
$$

**Step 3 — INSDC Propagation:**
Once accepted, the record is queued for the next daily exchange. The time to global availability is:

$$
T_{\text{global}} = T_{\text{accession}} + T_{\text{propagation}}
$$

**Step 4 — Worst-Case Availability:**
For submissions made just after a daily exchange window, the maximum wait is:

$$
T_{\text{global, max}} = T_{\text{submission}} + 24 \text{ hours} + 24 \text{ hours} = T_{\text{submission}} + 48 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5 — Best-Case Availability:**
If a submission is made just before the daily exchange window:

$$
T_{\text{global, min}} = T_{\text{submission}} + T_{\text{validation}} + \epsilon \quad (\epsilon \to 0)
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: DDBJ vs. GenBank vs. ENA Comparison

| Feature | **DDBJ** | **GenBank (NCBI)** | **ENA (EBI)** |
|---------|----------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Country** | Japan | USA | UK (Europe) |
| **Host Institute** | NIG, Mishima | NCBI, Bethesda | EBI, Hinxton |
| **Year Established** | **1986** | **1982** | **1980** |
| **NGS Archive** | DRA | SRA | ENA-Reads |
| **Submission Tool** | NSSS, MSS | BankIt, tbl2asn | Webin, Command-line |
| **Accession Style** | Letter+digits | Letter+digits | Letter+digits |
| **Daily Exchange** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Data Format** | INSDC Flatfile | INSDC Flatfile | INSDC Flatfile |

### 3.3 Exhaustive Submission Flow Derivation

Let us trace the complete lifecycle of a nucleotide sequence from laboratory bench to global repository:

**Step 1 — Data Generation:** Researcher sequences a gene using Sanger or NGS method. The output is a raw FASTA file (raw nucleotide string + quality scores if NGS).

**Step 2 — Pre-processing:** The researcher cleans the sequence, removes vector contamination, and verifies the open reading frame (ORF).

**Step 3 — Annotation:** Each feature (CDS, gene, mRNA, exon, intron, promoter, 5'UTR, 3'UTR) is annotated with structured descriptors.

**Step 4 — Submission Packet:** The complete submission consists of:
- FASTA sequence block
- Feature table (5-column tabular annotation)
- Source organism metadata
- Publication reference (if any)

**Step 5 — NSSS Upload:** The packet is uploaded to NSSS. The system performs automated checks.

**Step 6 — Accession Grant:** Post-validation, DDBJ issues a **unique accession number**.

**Step 7 — Daily Sync:** Within 24 hours, the record is mirrored to NCBI's GenBank and EBI's ENA.

**Step 8 — Citation in Literature:** The researcher cites the accession number in their paper, enabling others to retrieve the exact sequence.

### 3.4 Numerical Example: Accession Number Interpretation

Consider the accession **LC064801.1**.

- **LC** → DDBJ prefix assigned to a particular project/submission batch.
- **064801** → 6-digit unique serial number.
- **.1** → Version number (incremented only if the sequence itself is updated, not the annotation).

$$
\text{Accession} = \underbrace{\text{LC}}_{\text{Prefix}} + \underbrace{064801}_{\text{Serial}} + \underbrace{.1}_{\text{Version}}
$$

This structural decoding is a common 3-mark KTU question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DDBJ Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Researchers["Global Researchers"]
        R1[Submitter in Asia]
        R2[Submitter in Americas]
        R3[Submitter in Europe]
    end

    subgraph DDBJ_Center["DDBJ Bioinformatics Center Japan"]

    end

    subgraph INSDC["INSDC Global Mirror Network"]
        GB[GenBank NCBI USA]
        ENA[ENA EBI UK]
    end

    subgraph Users["Global End Users"]
        U1[Scientists]
        U2[Pharma Companies]
        U3[Agricultural Researchers]
    end

    R1 --> NSSS
    R2 --> GB
    R3 --> ENA
    NSSS --> VAL
    MSS --> VAL
    VAL --> ACC
    ACC --> TRAD
    ACC --> DRA
    TRAD -->|Daily Sync 24hr| GB
    TRAD -->|Daily Sync 24hr| ENA
    DRA -->|Daily Sync 24hr| GB
    DRA -->|Daily Sync 24hr| ENA
    GB --> U1
    ENA --> U2
    TRAD --> U3
```

### 4.2 Submission-to-Publication Lifecycle Diagram

```mermaid

    A[Lab Sequence Generation] --> B[FASTA and Feature Table Preparation]
    B --> C[NSSS Portal Upload]
    C --> D[Automated Validation]
    D --> E{Validation Passed?}
    E -->|No| F[Resubmission Required]
    F --> B
    E -->|Yes| G[Accession Number Issued]
    G --> H[Daily INSDC Mirror Sync]
    H --> I[Global Public Availability]
    I --> J[Citation in Research Papers]
    J --> K[Long-term Archival and Updates]
```

### 4.3 INSDC Tripartite Collaboration Model

```mermaid
