# 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End PROSITE Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Curated Multiple Sequence Alignment of Protein Family] --> B[Identify Conserved Columns]
    B --> C{Choose Signature Type}
    C -->|Short highly conserved region| D[Build PROSITE Pattern - Regular Expression]
    C -->|Long divergent domain| E[Build PROSITE Profile - Weighted Matrix]
    D --> F[Validate Against UniProtKB/SwissProt]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute P-values for True Positives and False Positives]
    G --> H{P-value less than 0.01?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Issue as High-Confidence Match]
    H -->|No| J[Issue as Potential Match - P between 0.01 and 0.05]
    I --> K[Release as PS Entry with PDOC Documentation]
    J --> K
    K --> L[InterPro Federation - Cross-Referenced to Pfam, CDD, HAMAP]
    L --> M[User Scans Query Sequence via ScanProsite Tool]
    M --> N{Statistically Significant Match?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Annotate Query as Member of Family]
    N -->|No| P[Report No Significant Hit]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PROSITE Entry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IDBlock["Identification Block"]
        ID1[PS Accession Number PS00107]
        ID2[Short Name TRYPSIN_HIS]
        ID3[Classification Family]
    end

    subgraph PatternBlock["Signature Block"]
        PAT1[Pattern or Profile Matrix]
        PAT2[Length Range]
        PAT3[P-value Statistics]
    end

    subgraph BioBlock["Biological Documentation PDOC"]
        BIO1[Family Description]
        BIO2[Active Site Residues]
        BIO3[Taxonomic Distribution]
    end

    subgraph XRefBlock["Cross Reference Block"]
        XR1[InterPro Entry]
        XR2[Pfam Family]
        XR3[SCOP/CATH Superfamily]
        XR4[GO Molecular Function Term]
    end

    IDBlock --> PatternBlock
    PatternBlock --> BioBlock
    BioBlock --> XRefBlock
    XRefBlock --> Q[ScanProsite Output to User]
```

## 4.3 Pattern vs. Profile Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Query Protein Sequence] --> SC{Scan Engine}
    SC -->|Regex match| PR[PROSITE Pattern - Position Specific Alphabet]
    SC -->|Matrix score sum with indels| PF[PROSITE Profile - 20 x L Score Matrix]
    PR --> R1[Fast - Linear Time]
    PF --> R2[Slower - Dynamic Programming with Indel Penalties]
    R1 --> O[Final Family Assignment]
    R2 --> O
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
> **Define PROSITE. What are the two types of signatures it uses to characterize protein families?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

PROSITE is a curated biological database of protein families, domains, and functional sites maintained by the Swiss Institute of Bioinformatics (SIB). Each protein family in PROSITE is characterized by a **diagnostic signature**, which is used to identify whether a new query protein belongs to that family.

PROSITE supports two distinct signature types:

1. **PROSITE Patterns** — short regular-expression motifs (typically 15–55 amino acids) encoding highly conserved residues with strict positional spacing.
2. **PROSITE Profiles** — position-specific weighted scoring matrices built from curated multiple sequence alignments, capable of modeling variable-length insertions and quantitative residue preferences.

Patterns are used for short functional motifs such as active sites; profiles are used for larger, more divergent domains.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
> **Explain the significance of the p-value thresholds 0.01 and 0.05 used in PROSITE.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The p-value represents the probability of a PROSITE signature matching a random sequence by chance. Two thresholds govern the classification of hits:

- **p ≤ 0.01** — the hit is classified as a **True Positive (TP)**. The match is statistically significant, with no more than a **1%** chance of being a random false positive. These hits can be used for confident functional annotation.

- **0.01 < p ≤ 0.05** — the hit is classified as a **Potential / False Positive (FP)** match. It may indicate genuine family membership but carries a higher noise risk (**up to 5%**) and usually requires expert curation before being reported as a confirmed annotation.

These thresholds are borrowed from classical statistical hypothesis testing and ensure reproducibility across the database.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style, Module 1]**
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of a typical PROSITE entry, listing all major blocks and their purposes. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** With the help of a worked example, derive the minimum sequence length at which a random sequence would generate a PROSITE pattern hit with probability exceeding **1%**, given an expected match frequency **λ = 3.0 × 10⁻⁶** per residue. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

A PROSITE entry consists of **six functional blocks**:

1. **Identification Block** — provides the unique `PS` accession number (e.g., PS00107), the short symbolic name (e.g., TRYPSIN_HIS), and the classification category (family, domain, or functional site). This block uniquely identifies the entry in the database.

2. **Date Stamp Block** — records the date of creation and the version number of the most recent update, ensuring traceability of the entry's history.

3. **Description Block (PDOC)** — contains the free-text scientific narrative describing the family: its biological function, structural characteristics, key conserved residues, taxonomic distribution, and literature references. The PDOC entry has its own `PDOC` accession for cross-referencing.

4. **Pattern / Profile Rule Block** — encodes the mathematical signature itself. For patterns, this is a regular expression in PROSITE syntax. For profiles, it is a position-specific scoring matrix. This block also specifies the expected number of matches in SwissProt and the false-positive rate.

5. **Matched Sequences Block** — provides the empirical validation: the list of SwissProt proteins that match the signature, partitioned into true positives and false positives. The numbers are used to compute the empirical p-value.

6. **Cross-Reference Block** — links the entry to related resources: InterPro, Pfam, HAMAP, SCOP, CATH, GO terms, and Reactome pathways. This federated linkage is what allows PROSITE to participate in modern genome annotation pipelines.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — State the compound Poisson model.**

For a random sequence of length *L*, the probability of at least one chance match is:

$$
P(\text{match}) \;=\; 1 - e^{-\lambda L}
$$

**Step 2 — Set the cutoff condition.**

We require *P ≥ 0.01*:

$$
1 - e^{-\lambda L} \;\geq\; 0.01
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange.**

$$
e^{-\lambda L} \;\leq\; 0.99
$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm.**

$$
-\lambda L \;\leq\; \ln(0.99)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for L.**

$$
L \;\geq\; -\frac{\ln(0.99)}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the given λ.**

$$
L \;\geq\; -\frac{-0.010050336}{3.0 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate.**

$$
L \;\geq\; \frac{0.010050336}{3.0 \times 10^{-6}} \;\approx\; 3{,}350.11
$$

**Step 8 — Round up.**

$$
\boxed{\,L_{\min} \;=\; 3{,}351 \text{ residues}\,}
$$

**Biological interpretation:** Any query sequence shorter than 3,351 residues that produces a hit under this pattern can be considered statistically significant with at least 99% confidence, because a random sequence of that length would not be expected to produce a single false positive.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Internal Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style, Module 1]**
> **(a)** Describe in detail the PROSITE pattern syntax with at least six examples of meta-characters. Explain the biological rationale behind each construct. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare and contrast PROSITE patterns and PROSITE profiles. When would you choose one over the other? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The PROSITE pattern syntax is a custom regular-expression language that encodes allowed residues at each position of a conserved motif. The key meta-characters are:

1. **`[ABC]` — Allowed residue set.** Example: `[ST]` allows Serine or Threonine. **Biological rationale:** these two small hydroxyl-bearing residues are often interchangeable at phosphorylation sites because both can act as phospho-acceptors.

2. **`{ABC}` — Forbidden residue set.** Example: `{P}` forbids Proline. **Biological rationale:** Proline introduces a kinked backbone that disrupts α-helices and β-strands; its exclusion therefore encodes a structural preference for an ordered secondary-structure element.

3. **`x` — Wildcard (any amino acid).** **Biological rationale:** indicates a position under no functional constraint, often a surface-exposed residue tolerant of substitution.

4. **`x(n)` — Exact repetition count.** Example: `x(3)` = three wildcards. **Biological rationale:** encodes a fixed-length linker or loop where any residue is acceptable but the spacing must be preserved for tertiary contacts.

5. **`x(n,m)` — Variable repetition count.** Example: `x(2,4)` = 2–4 residues. **Biological rationale:** captures a flexible loop whose length varies between family members without disrupting function.

6. **`>` and `<` — Terminal anchors.** Example: `>M` requires the motif to start at the N-terminal Methionine. **Biological rationale:** signal peptides and transit peptides are cleaved, so position is fixed relative to the chain terminus.

7. **`(n)` — Repetition suffix.** Example: `[GA](3)` = three residues, each G or A. **Biological rationale:** captures low-complexity or compositionally biased regions (e.g., poly-GA linkers).

A complete annotated example is the **N-glycosylation sequon**:

$$
\texttt{N-{P}-[ST]}
$$

which encodes **Asparagine, NOT followed immediately by Proline, followed by either Serine or Threonine** — the exact context required for an N-linked glycan to be attached to the Asn side chain.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

| Dimension | PROSITE Pattern | PROSITE Profile |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Length** | Short (15–55 aa) | Long (entire domain) |
| **Data structure** | Regular expression | Weighted scoring matrix |
| **Sensitivity to divergence** | Low — strict position rules | High — graded scoring |
| **Specificity** | High when conserved residues exist | High overall |
| **Computational cost** | Very fast — O(L·N) | Slow — O(L·N·W) with dynamic programming |
| **Handles indels** | No (except via `x(n,m)`) | Yes (insertion / deletion penalties) |
| **Best for** | Active sites, short motifs | Divergent domains, whole families |
| **Limitation** | Cannot model quantitative residue preferences | Heavier to build and curate |

**Decision rule:**
- Choose a **pattern** when the family is defined by a small number of *strictly conserved* residues at known positions (e.g., catalytic triads, glycosylation sites, metal-binding motifs).
- Choose a **profile** when the family spans a *long, divergent domain* where many positions tolerate substitutions but with position-specific preferences (e.g., globins, kinases, G-protein-coupled receptors).

In modern pipelines (e.g., InterPro), both are often run in parallel: the pattern gives a fast first pass, and the profile confirms ambiguous hits.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on PROSITE questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `-` with set subtraction.** The hyphen in PROSITE patterns is a positional separator, NOT the mathematical minus sign. Writing "A − x − G means A minus x minus G" will lose a mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the N-terminus anchor symbol.** Writing `M-[ST]-x-{P}` instead of `>M-[ST]-x-{P}` when describing a signal peptide. The `>` is required for proper terminal anchoring.
>
> 3. **Mixing up `x(n)` and `x(n,m)`.** Exact count `x(3)` is *not* the same as variable range `x(2,4)`. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 4. **Stating that PROSITE profiles are SLOWER but MORE SENSITIVE than patterns without elaboration.** Always quantify: profiles use 20×L score tables, patterns use linear regex matching; profiles capture indels via dynamic programming, patterns cannot.
>
> 5. **Omitting the p-value interpretation in 14-mark answers.** A 14-mark PROSITE question almost always asks for the p-value calculation; ignoring the statistical foundation loses at least **3 marks**.
>
> 6. **Confusing PROSITE with Pfam.** Pfam uses Hidden Markov Models (HMMs); PROSITE uses patterns and weighted matrices. Examiners *specifically* test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PROSITE — Rapid Revision Bullets (print this last block before the exam):**
>
> - **Full name:** Database of PROtein SITEs, families, and domains.
> - **Host:** Swiss Institute of Bioinformatics (SIB), accessed via ExPASy.
> - **Accession format:** `PSXXXXX` for entries, `PDOCXXXXX` for documentation.
> - **Two signature types:**
>   - **Pattern** = short regular expression / motif (fast, strict).
>   - **Profile** = position-specific weighted matrix (sensitive, handles indels).
> - **Pattern syntax essentials:**
>   - `[ABC]` allowed, `{ABC}` forbidden.
>   - `x` wildcard.
>   - `x(n)` exact count, `x(n,m)` variable count.
>   - `-` positional separator (NOT subtraction).
>   - `>` N-terminal anchor, `<` C-terminal anchor.
> - **Statistical thresholds:**
>   - **p ≤ 0.01 → True Positive** (high confidence).
>   - **0.01 < p ≤ 0.05 → Potential / False Positive** (requires curation).
> - **Random-match probability formula:** `P = 1 − e^(−λL)`.
> - **Profile score formula:** `S(a,j) = (100 / log 2) × log₂(f(a,j) / (p(a)·Nⱼ))`.
> - **Profile construction pipeline:** MSA → column frequencies → log-odds weights → validation against SwissProt.
> - **Cross-references:** InterPro, Pfam, HAMAP, SCOP, CATH, GO terms.
> - **Scan tool:** ScanProsite (online at prosite.expasy.org).
> - **Differentiators vs. other databases:**
>   - vs. **Pfam:** PROSITE = patterns/profiles; Pfam = HMMs.
>   - vs. **PRINTS:** PROSITE = single signature; PRINTS = fingerprints (multiple motifs).
>   - vs. **BLOCKS:** PROSITE = unaligned blocks + spacing; BLOCKS = aligned blocks.
> - **Biological analogy:** PROSITE is the **fingerprint library of protein families** — scan an unknown protein, get its functional identity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SCOP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT604 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** SCOP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SCOP (Structural Classification of Proteins)** is a manually curated database that classifies protein domains of known three-dimensional structure according to their **structural and evolutionary relationships**. It organizes proteins into a hierarchical framework based on the principles of **homology, topology, and class of secondary structure**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, SCOP is a **knowledge-based classification system** in structural bioinformatics that allows researchers to navigate protein space by grouping structurally similar domains. It was developed at the **MRC Laboratory of Molecular Biology** and uses visual inspection and comparison of structures rather than purely automated algorithms.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Library Analogy:** Think of SCOP like a **public library classification system** for books (proteins).
> - **Class** = The Dewey Decimal main category (e.g., "Literature")
> - **Fold** = The genre within literature (e.g., "Mystery")
> - **Superfamily** = The general theme (e.g., "Detective stories")
> - **Family** = A specific book series (e.g., "Sherlock Holmes")

Just as a librarian groups books first by broad category, then by structural similarity (how chapters are arranged), and finally by detailed content similarity, SCOP groups proteins from the broad (what kind of secondary structure) down to the very specific (sequence-level homology).

### 1.3 Why SCOP Exists

Proteins with **low sequence identity** can still have remarkably similar 3D structures because their functions and folds are conserved through evolution. SCOP captures this **structure–function–evolution** triad, providing a much richer classification than sequence-only methods like BLAST or FASTA.

> [!TIP]
> SCOP is the **gold standard** for protein structural classification and is widely used in **homology modeling, drug discovery, functional annotation, and evolutionary studies**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The SCOP Classification Hierarchy (4 Levels)

SCOP organizes protein domains into a strict **four-level hierarchy**, going from broad structural classes to very specific evolutionary families:

#### Level 1: **Class**
- Defined by the **gross secondary structure composition** and spatial arrangement.
- The Class level classifies proteins into distinct structural classes.
- **Number of classes in SCOP:** **11 classes**, with the four major being:
  1. **All-α proteins** — dominated by α-helices
  2. **All-β proteins** — dominated by β-sheets
  3. **α/β proteins** — parallel β-sheets (β-α-β) units
  4. **α+β proteins** — segregated α-helices and β-sheets

> [!NOTE]
> The remaining 7 minor classes include: small proteins, coiled-coil proteins, designed proteins, multi-domain proteins, membrane proteins, peptides, and low-resolution structures.

#### Level 2: **Fold**
- Groups proteins with **similar major secondary structure elements in the same spatial arrangement and topology**.
- Two proteins may have the same fold but **no detectable sequence or functional similarity**.
- Folds reflect the **geometric arrangement** of secondary structures, not evolutionary origin.
- Each fold name is descriptive (e.g., "TIM barrel", "Immunoglobulin-like β-sandwich").

#### Level 3: **Superfamily**
- Groups proteins with **low sequence identity** but **structural and functional evidence** suggesting a common evolutionary origin.
- The relationship at this level is **probable homology** — proteins may share functional features despite low sequence similarity.
- A superfamily may contain multiple families with diverse sequences but conserved core structure.

#### Level 4: **Family**
- Groups proteins with **clear sequence similarity (≥ 30% identity)** and often similar function.
- These proteins are clearly **homologous** — they share a common ancestor.
- Within a family, the proteins are almost certainly evolutionarily related.

### 2.2 Classification Criteria Per Level

| Hierarchy Level | Classification Basis | Typical Identity Range | Biological Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class** | Secondary structure composition | No similarity required | Broad structural theme |
| **Fold** | Topology of secondary structure | Very low or none | Geometric arrangement |
| **Superfamily** | Probable common ancestry | Low (15–30%) | Remote homology |
| **Family** | Sequence homology | ≥ 30% (sometimes lower) | Clear evolutionary relation |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key SCOP Identification Numbers (SID)**
> Each entry in SCOP gets a unique **SCOP Identification number (sid)**. The hierarchical IDs use a dot-separated format:
> $$\text{SCOP ID} = \text{Class}.\text{Fold}.\text{Superfamily}.\text{Family}$$
> For example: **d.1.1.1** — "α/β class" → "TIM barrel fold" → "TIM barrel superfamily" → "TIM family".

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Recall Mnemonic: "C-F-S-F" = "Class-Fold-Superfamily-Family"** — from broadest to most specific.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Application Area | Use of SCOP |
|---|---|
| **Homology Modeling** | Identifying structurally related templates for modeling unknown proteins |
| **Drug Discovery** | Identifying conserved binding pockets in protein families |
| **Functional Annotation** | Predicting function of newly sequenced proteins via structural relatives |
| **Evolutionary Studies** | Tracing ancient evolutionary relationships through superfamily |
| **Protein Engineering** | Choosing scaffolds with desired structural properties |

> [!NOTE]
> **SCOP vs CATH:** CATH is the main alternative classification system. SCOP is **manually curated** and emphasizes evolutionary relationships; CATH is **semi-automated** and emphasizes topological similarity. Both are widely used and largely complementary.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Logical Flow: Classifying a New Protein in SCOP

Since SCOP is not a numerical problem, we present the **analytical decision tree** for classifying a protein domain.

#### Step 1: Domain Boundary Identification
The protein is first broken into **structural domains** (compact, independent folding units). Each domain is classified separately.

> A single protein may contain multiple domains, each belonging to a different SCOP classification.

#### Step 2: Determine the Class
Count the proportion of residues in α-helices vs. β-strands.

$$
\text{Class} = f(\alpha\text{-content}, \beta\text{-content}, \text{segregation/alternation})
$$

Decision logic:

$$
\text{If } \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta} \geq 0.9 \Rightarrow \text{All-}\alpha
$$

$$
\text{If } \frac{\beta}{\alpha + \beta} \geq 0.9 \Rightarrow \text{All-}\beta
$$

$$
\text{If } \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta} \approx 0.5 \text{ and interleaved} \Rightarrow \alpha/\beta
$$

$$
\text{If } \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta} \approx 0.5 \text{ and segregated} \Rightarrow \alpha+\beta
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exact decision logic is **qualitative**, not purely numerical. It relies on the curator's visual inspection of the structure.

#### Step 3: Identify the Fold
Examine the **topology** of the secondary structure elements:
- Connectivity of β-strands
- Helix-helix or helix-sheet packing
- Overall 3D arrangement

Two proteins have the **same fold** if their major secondary structure elements are in the **same spatial arrangement and topology**, regardless of connectivity order in some cases (for "dehydron" definitions).

#### Step 4: Determine the Superfamily
Search for **structural neighbors** with low sequence identity but suggestive functional and structural similarity. Tools used:
- DALI (Distance matrix alignment)
- SSAP (Sequential Structure Alignment Program)
- VAST (Vector Alignment Search Tool)

$$
\text{Superfamily match} \iff \text{Structural similarity} \land \text{Probable common ancestry}
$$

#### Step 5: Determine the Family
Perform **sequence-based comparison** (BLAST, PSI-BLAST, HMMER) and look for clear sequence similarity:

$$
\text{Same Family} \iff \text{Sequence identity} \geq 30\% \ \text{(typical threshold)}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: SCOP Hierarchy Example — Triose Phosphate Isomerase (TIM)

| Level | SCOP Classification | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Class** | **α/β** | Parallel β-sheet with α-helices alternating |
| **Fold** | **TIM barrel** | 8-stranded α/β barrel (closed barrel topology) |
| **Superfamily** | **TIM barrel superfamily** | Many enzymes share this barrel fold with diverse sequences |
| **Family** | **Triosephosphate isomerase family** | Sequence-similar TIM enzymes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **TIM barrel** (named after Triosephosphate Isomerase) is one of the most common protein folds and is found in **at least 5 distinct superfamilies**, demonstrating that convergent evolution can produce the same fold multiple times.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: How to Look Up a Protein in SCOP

**Problem:** Classify the protein **Lysozyme (PDB: 1LYZ)** using SCOP hierarchy.

**Step 1 — Identify the domain:**
Lysozyme is a single-domain protein. The catalytic domain is the entire protein.

**Step 2 — Determine the class:**
Examination of the structure shows predominantly α-helices. → **All-α class**

**Step 3 — Identify the fold:**
Lysozyme has a complex arrangement of α-helices that form a specific topology. → **Lysozyme-like fold**

**Step 4 — Identify the superfamily:**
Structural and functional similarity suggests a common evolutionary origin with other lysozyme-like proteins. → **Glycoside hydrolase superfamily 22**

**Step 5 — Identify the family:**
Sequence comparison with relatives like α-lactalbumin shows >30% identity. → **Glycoside hydrolase family 22**

**Final SCOP classification for 1LYZ:**

$$\boxed{\text{Class: All-}\alpha \rightarrow \text{Fold: Lysozyme-like} \rightarrow \text{Superfamily: Glycoside hydrolase 22} \rightarrow \text{Family: Lysozyme family}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Always remember the **class name first**, then progress down the hierarchy. The four major classes (all-α, all-β, α/β, α+β) are the most commonly tested.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SCOP Hierarchical Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PROTEIN DOMAIN"] --> B["LEVEL 1: CLASS"]
    B --> B1["All-alpha"]
    B --> B2["All-beta"]
    B --> B3["Alpha-Beta alternating"]
    B --> B4["Alpha+Beta segregated"]
    B --> C["LEVEL 2: FOLD"]
    C --> C1["Topological arrangement of SSEs"]
    C --> D["LEVEL 3: SUPERFAMILY"]
    D --> D1["Probable common ancestry"]
    D --> E["LEVEL 4: FAMILY"]
    E --> E1["Clear sequence homology >= 30 percent identity"]
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> E1
```

### 4.2 SCOP Decision Tree for Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["New protein domain"] --> Q["Examine secondary structure composition"]
    Q --> R{"Majority alpha helix?"}
    R -- Yes --> S["All-alpha class"]
    R -- No --> T{"Majority beta strand?"}
    T -- Yes --> U["All-beta class"]
    T -- No --> V{"Interleaved alpha beta?"}
    V -- Yes --> W["Alpha-Beta class"]
    V -- No --> X["Alpha+Beta class"]
    S --> Y["Check 3D topology"]
    U --> Y
    W --> Y
    X --> Y
    Y --> Z["Assign FOLD"]
    Z --> AA["Search structural neighbors"]
    AA --> AB["Assign SUPERFAMILY"]
    AB --> AC["Run BLAST or HMMER"]
    AC --> AD["Assign FAMILY"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SCOP Database

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT: PDB Structures"] --> B["SCOP Curation Pipeline"]
    B --> C1["Domain Boundary Detection"]
    B --> C2["Secondary Structure Analysis"]
    B --> C3["3D Structure Comparison"]
    B --> C4["Sequence Similarity Search"]
    C1 --> D["SCOP Hierarchical Database"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E["Web Interface SCOP and SCOPe"]
    D --> F["File Format SCOP parsable"]
    E --> G["User Query Protein Classification"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Output: Class to Family Assignment"]
```

### 4.4 Sequence Identity vs Classification Level — Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Sequence Identity 0 percent"] --> B["Different CLASS possible"]
    C["Sequence Identity 10 percent"] --> D["Same SUPERFAMILY possible"]
    E["Sequence Identity 30 percent plus"] --> F["Same FAMILY certain"]
    B --> G["Hierarchy Level"]
    D --> G
    F --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram illustrates how **sequence identity threshold increases as we go deeper** in the SCOP hierarchy. Class and fold can be the same even with 0% sequence identity; family requires substantial sequence identity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define SCOP. What are the different levels of classification used in SCOP?*

**Model Answer:**

> **SCOP (Structural Classification of Proteins)** is a database that classifies protein domains of known three-dimensional structure based on their **structural and evolutionary relationships**. The classification is done manually by visual inspection and comparison of protein structures.
>
> SCOP has **four hierarchical levels** of classification:
> 1. **Class** — based on the type of secondary structure (e.g., all-α, all-β, α/β, α+β)
> 2. **Fold** — based on the major secondary structure arrangement and topology
> 3. **Superfamily** — proteins with low sequence identity but probable common evolutionary origin
> 4. **Family** — proteins with ≥ 30% sequence identity and clear evolutionary relationship

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between SCOP and CATH classification systems.*

**Model Answer:**

> | Feature | SCOP | CATH |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Curated by** | Manual (human) | Semi-automated |
> | **Classification basis** | Evolutionary and structural | Topological and structural |
> | **Hierarchy levels** | Class, Fold, Superfamily, Family | Class, Architecture, Topology, Homology |
> | **Approach** | Subjective, evolutionary-focused | Algorithmic, geometry-focused |
> | **Database size** | Smaller, refined | Larger, automated |
> 
> While SCOP emphasizes evolutionary relationships, CATH is more dependent on automated structure-comparison algorithms.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **four hierarchical levels** of SCOP classification with suitable examples. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

> **SCOP Classification Hierarchy:**
>
> **1. Class (Level 1):** This is the most general level and is based on the **secondary structure composition** of the protein domain. The four main classes are:
> - **All-α proteins** — predominantly composed of α-helices. *Example:* Myoglobin, Cytochrome b562.
> - **All-β proteins** — predominantly composed of β-strands forming sheets. *Example:* Immunoglobulin domains, Ubiquitin.
> - **α/β proteins** — parallel β-sheets alternating with α-helices, often forming **β-α-β units**. *Example:* Triose phosphate isomerase (TIM barrel), Glycolate oxidase.
> - **α+β proteins** — α-helices and β-strands are **segregated** in different regions of the protein. *Example:* Lysozyme, Ribonuclease A.
>
> **2. Fold (Level 2):** Folds group proteins that have similar **major secondary structure elements arranged in the same spatial topology** but may not have evolutionary relationships. Examples include:
> - **TIM barrel fold** — 8-stranded α/β barrel
> - **Immunoglobulin-like β-sandwich** — two β-sheets packed together
> - **Globin-like fold** — characteristic 3-over-3 α-helical sandwich
>
> **3. Superfamily (Level 3):** Proteins grouped at this level share a **probable common evolutionary origin**, evident from structural and functional similarities despite low sequence identity. For example, the **TIM barrel superfamily** contains multiple enzyme families (TIM, aldolase, enolase) all sharing the same fold but with diverse sequences and functions.
>
> **4. Family (Level 4):** Families group proteins with **clear sequence similarity (≥ 30%)** and confirmed evolutionary homology. For example, the **Lysozyme family** contains lysozyme c and α-lactalbumin, which share significant sequence identity and are clearly homologs.
>
> The hierarchy follows a **decreasing order of structural diversity** and **increasing sequence similarity**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **applications and significance of SCOP** in bioinformatics research. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

> **Applications of SCOP:**
>
> **1. Homology Modeling:** SCOP helps identify **structurally related template proteins** for building 3D models of proteins with unknown structures. If a target protein belongs to a known SCOP family, a structural template from the same family can be used.
>
> **2. Functional Annotation:** New proteins can be assigned putative functions by comparing them to proteins of known function in the same SCOP superfamily. For example, if a newly discovered protein belongs to the **serine protease superfamily**, it is likely a protease.
>
> **3. Evolutionary Studies:** SCOP superfamilies reveal **remote evolutionary relationships** that cannot be detected by sequence comparison alone, helping trace the **ancient ancestry** of proteins.
>
> **4. Drug Discovery:** SCOP helps identify **conserved structural motifs** in protein families, which often correspond to conserved active sites or binding pockets — useful for designing drugs.
>
> **5. Protein Structure Prediction:** Folds identified in SCOP serve as **starting scaffolds** for threading and fold recognition methods.
>
> **6. Comparative Genomics:** SCOP classifications can be mapped onto genomes to study the distribution of protein folds across organisms, providing insights into genome evolution.
>
> **7. Database Organization:** SCOP provides a **rational organization** of PDB structures, making it easier to navigate and query structural data.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall:**
> - Students often confuse the **differences between Fold, Superfamily, and Family** levels. A common mistake is to state that proteins in the same fold must be homologous — **this is false**. Folds can arise by **convergent evolution** and may not share common ancestry.
> - Another frequent error is to assume that sequence identity below 30% rules out homology. In SCOP, **superfamily** level explicitly groups proteins with low sequence identity but structural and functional similarity.
> - Marks are lost when students fail to provide **examples** for each level. Always include at least one example per class/fold.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Structural Classification of Proteins (SCOP)** and explain how proteins are classified at the **fold and superfamily levels**. How is this different from sequence-based classification? **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

> **Introduction to SCOP:**
> SCOP (Structural Classification of Proteins) is a manually curated database that classifies protein domains of known 3D structure based on **structural and evolutionary relationships**. It was created at the MRC Laboratory of Molecular Biology.
>
> **Fold Level Classification:**
> At the fold level, proteins are grouped by their **topological arrangement of major secondary structure elements**. Two proteins with the same fold have:
> - Same types of secondary structure elements (α-helices, β-strands)
> - Similar 3D arrangement and connectivity
> - May have **no sequence similarity** and **no evolutionary relationship**
>
> *Example:* The **Greek key fold** appears in many proteins that have no detectable sequence similarity.
>
> **Superfamily Level Classification:**
> At the superfamily level, proteins are grouped based on:
> - **Probable common evolutionary origin**
> - **Structural similarity** despite low sequence identity
> - Often shared **functional features**
>
> *Example:* The **serine protease superfamily** includes trypsin, chymotrypsin, and elastase — all sharing the catalytic triad despite differences in sequence.
>
> **Difference from Sequence-Based Classification:**
> | Aspect | Sequence-Based (BLAST, FASTA) | SCOP Structure-Based |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Basis** | Sequence identity | Structure + evolution |
> | **Detection limit** | ~25% identity | Can detect <15% identity |
> | **Evolutionary info** | Limited | Captures remote homology |
> | **Homology detection** | Direct only | Both direct and remote |
> | **Time dependence** | Independent | Requires solved 3D structure |
>
> SCOP can detect **remote homologs** that sequence-based methods miss, which is critical for understanding ancient evolutionary events.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the four major **structural classes** in SCOP with suitable examples. How does the **TIM barrel fold** illustrate the relationship between fold and superfamily levels? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

> **The Four Major Structural Classes in SCOP:**
>
> | Class | Description | Examples |
> |---|---|---|
> | **All-α** | Predominantly α-helices | Myoglobin, Hemoglobin, Cytochrome b562 |
> | **All-β** | Predominantly β-strands | Immunoglobulin domain, Ubiquitin, GFP |
> | **α/β** | Alternating α-helices and parallel β-strands | TIM, Glycolate oxidase, Adenylate kinase |
> | **α+β** | Segregated α-helices and β-sheets | Lysozyme, Ribonuclease A, Pepsin |
>
> **All-α proteins:** Built almost entirely from α-helices that pack together. The globin fold is the classic example.
>
> **All-β proteins:** Built primarily from β-strands that form sheets, often anti-parallel. The Greek key motif is common.
>
> **α/β proteins:** Contain parallel β-strands alternating with α-helices. The most common arrangement is the **β-α-β** unit. This class is associated with many **enzymatic functions**.
>
> **α+β proteins:** Have α-helices and β-strands in **separate regions** of the 3D structure, not interleaved.
>
> **TIM Barrel and Fold–Superfamily Relationship:**
> The **TIM barrel** is an 8-stranded α/β barrel fold, one of the most common and versatile protein architectures. It is found in:
> - **Multiple superfamilies** — at least 5 different SCOP superfamilies adopt this fold
> - **Multiple families** within each superfamily
> - **Diverse functions** — enzymes like triosephosphate isomerase (TIM), aldolases, enolases, and many others
>
> The TIM barrel illustrates that:
> 1. **Same fold, different superfamilies** → suggests **convergent evolution** of this stable fold
> 2. **Same fold, same superfamily** → suggests **divergent evolution** from a common ancestor
> 3. The fold represents a **stable solution** to a geometric packing problem, while superfamily captures the **evolutionary lineage**
>
> This is a classic KTU exam example showing that fold and superfamily represent **different biological concepts**: **geometry vs. evolution**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often write "all α/β proteins are enzymes" — this is **incorrect**. While many are, the class is defined structurally, not functionally.
> - Do not confuse α/β with α+β. In α/β, the strands and helices are **interleaved (alternating)**, while in α+β, they are **segregated (clustered)**.
> - For the TIM barrel, students often claim it evolved only once. The correct answer is that **convergent evolution** has produced this fold in multiple lineages.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Quick Revision Checklist for SCOP:**

- **SCOP full form:** Structural Classification of Proteins
- **Purpose:** Classify protein domains based on **structure + evolution**
- **Number of hierarchy levels:** **4** (Class → Fold → Superfamily → Family)
- **Four major classes:** **All-α, All-β, α/β, α+β**
- **Class criterion:** Secondary structure composition and arrangement
- **Fold criterion:** Topology of secondary structure elements
- **Superfamily criterion:** Probable common evolutionary origin, low sequence identity
- **Family criterion:** ≥ 30% sequence identity, clear homology
- **SCOP SID format:** Class.Fold.Superfamily.Family (e.g., d.1.1.1 for TIM)
- **Manually curated:** Yes — by experts via visual inspection
- **Most common fold example:** **TIM barrel** — found in multiple superfamilies
- **Sequence identity at family level:** Typically ≥ 30%
- **SCOP vs CATH:** SCOP is manual and evolution-based; CATH is automated and topology-based
- **Applications:** Homology modeling, functional annotation, drug discovery, evolutionary studies
- **Tools for structural comparison in SCOP:** DALI, SSAP, VAST
- **Domains vs proteins:** SCOP classifies **domains**, not whole proteins
- **Convergent evolution evidence:** Same fold in different superfamilies (e.g., TIM barrel)
- **Key distinction:** Fold ≠ Superfamily. Fold is geometric; Superfamily is evolutionary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"From Class broad to Family specific — four levels deep, one protein at a time."* Always remember the **C-F-S-F** mnemonic for **Class → Fold → Superfamily → Family**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RCSB-PDB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT604 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** RCSB-PDB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RCSB Protein Data Bank (RCSB-PDB)** is the **Research Collaboratory for Structural Bioinformatics (RCSB)** member-managed, globally authoritative, open-access archival repository for the **three-dimensional (3D) experimental structural data of biological macromolecules** — primarily **proteins, nucleic acids (DNA/RNA), and their complexes with ligands, cofactors, drugs, and ions** — determined via experimental techniques such as **X-ray crystallography, Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, 3D Electron Microscopy (3DEM / Cryo-EM)**, and computational modeling.

The **RCSB-PDB** portal serves as the unified public query, visualization, analysis, and download gateway to the **Worldwide Protein Data Bank (wwPDB)**, the international consortium that manages the data. The single canonical web portal is hosted at **https://www.rcsb.org/**.

The PDB archive is curated according to the **macromolecular Crystallographic Information File (mmCIF / PDBx)** standard developed by the **International Union of Crystallography (IUCr)**, and each deposited entry receives a unique **4-character alphanumeric accession identifier** (e.g., `1A2B`, `6LU7`).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The World's Open-Access 3D Biological Library 🏛️**

Imagine a **giant digital library**, but instead of books, every "volume" is a **3D atomic snapshot of a real molecule** caught mid-action. Each "book" tells you:

| Library Concept | RCSB-PDB Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Library Building | The **wwPDB archive** (data storage) |
| Front Desk / Search Counter | **RCSB.org** website (query interface) |
| Book ID (Dewey Decimal) | **4-character PDB ID** (e.g., `4HHB`) |
| Inside the Book | **Atomic (x, y, z) coordinates** of every atom |
| Book Cover & Index | **Metadata**: who solved it, when, how, resolution |
| Librarian Verification | **Validation reports** (wwPDB checks) |
| Borrowing (free!) | **Open download** (no subscription needed) |

Just as Google Maps lets you "fly through" a city in 3D, **RCSB-PDB** lets you fly through a molecule — seeing every atom's exact position and how drugs dock into viral proteins.

> [!TIP]
> **Famous Real-World Example:** During the **COVID-19 pandemic (2019–2023)**, the structure of the SARS-CoV-2 main protease (`6LU7`) and spike protein (`6VSB`) were deposited into the PDB and visualized worldwide within days, accelerating vaccine and drug design.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Constants (Bolded)

- **Archive size (as of 2024):** > **220,000 experimentally determined 3D structures**
- **Annual deposition growth rate:** ≈ **10–13%** year-over-year
- **Primary file formats:** **PDB (legacy `.pdb`)**, **mmCIF (`.cif`)**, **Biological Assembly (`*.pdb1`)**
- **Reference resolution benchmark (X-ray):** **1.0–2.0 Å** = high-quality; **>3.0 Å** = low-quality
- **Reference validation metric:** **MolProbity clash score < 5**, **Ramachandran favored > 98%**
- **Founding year of PDB:** **1971** (at Brookhaven National Laboratory)
- **RCSB founded:** **1998** (consortium of Rutgers, UCSD, SDSC, NIST)

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture (Layered Breakdown)

RCSB-PDB is best understood as a **4-layer architectural stack**. Each layer has a distinct role:

### Layer 1 — The Worldwide PDB (wwPDB) Data Layer
- The **canonical, write-once** archive.
- Accepts depositions through **ADIT (AutoDep Input Tool)** and **OneDep** unified deposition system.
- Validates structures against **IUCr/wwPDB validation standards**.
- Issues the unique **PDB accession code**.

### Layer 2 — The RCSB Data Curation Layer
- RCSB members annotate entries with **functional classifications, Enzyme Commission (EC) numbers**, **Gene Ontology (GO) terms**, and **UniProt cross-references**.
- Generates **derived data**: **SIFTS** (Structure Integration with Function, Taxonomy, and Sequence) mappings, **sequence clusters at 30%/70%/90%/95%/100% identity**, and **structural neighbors**.

### Layer 3 — The Query & Search Interface Layer
- **Text search**, **sequence search (BLAST/PSI-BLAST)**, **structure search (TM-align, FATCAT)**, **chemical search (SMILES, substructure)**.
- **Advanced Search Query Builder** using **Lucene-based Boolean operators** over 40+ indexed attributes.

### Layer 4 — The Visualization & Analysis Layer
- **NGL Viewer** (WebGL-based 3D molecular viewer, embedded).
- **Mol\*** (Molstar) for high-performance interactive 3D rendering.
- Integrated tools: **Ligand Explorer**, **Sequence & Structure Alignment**, **Electron Density Map Viewer** (for X-ray/cryo-EM).

---

## 2.2 The Core Data Model — Hierarchical Structure

Every PDB entry follows a strict **hierarchical data model**, transitioning from the legacy PDB format to the modern **PDBx/mmCIF dictionary**:

$$
\text{Entry (PDB ID)} \rightarrow \text{Entity} \rightarrow \text{Assembly} \rightarrow \text{Model} \rightarrow \text{Chain} \rightarrow \text{Residue} \rightarrow \text{Atom}
$$

| Level | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Entry** | Top-level container with unique 4-char ID | `4HHB` |
| **Entity** | Distinct unique macromolecule (by sequence) | Human hemoglobin α-chain |
| **Assembly** | The biologically relevant quaternary structure | Tetramer (α₂β₂) |
| **Model** | One conformer (NMR has multiple; X-ray has 1) | Model 1, Model 2 … |
| **Chain** | Single polypeptide/nucleic acid chain | Chain A, Chain B |
| **Residue** | An amino acid or nucleotide | His F8 |
| **Atom** | Single atom with (x, y, z) and B-factor | Cα at (12.345, 6.789, 4.012) |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat-Sheet Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are *board-tested* essentials for RCSB-PDB, structural validation, and coordinate geometry problems.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Atom count in a protein** | $N_{\text{atoms}} \approx 8 \times N_{\text{res}}$ (heavy atoms) | Quick estimate of file size |
| 2 | **Resolution (d-spacing)** | $d = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\sin\theta}$ — **Bragg's Law** | X-ray diffraction physics |
| 3 | **Inter-atomic Euclidean distance** | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i-x_j)^2 + (y_i-y_j)^2 + (z_i-z_j)^2}$ | Bond length / contact maps |
| 4 | **Cα–Cα virtual bond** | Expected $\approx 3.8 \text{ \AA}$ | Structure quality check |
| 5 | **B-factor (Temperature Factor)** | $B = 8\pi^2 \langle u^2 \rangle$ | Atomic displacement / disorder |
| 6 | **Resolution Quality Cutoffs** | <1.5 Å: **atomic**; 1.5–2.5 Å: **high**; 2.5–3.5 Å: **medium**; >3.5 Å: **low** | X-ray quality grading |
| 7 | **R-factor (crystallographic)** | $R = \dfrac{\sum \vert \vert F_o \vert - k\vert F_c \vert \vert}{\sum \vert F_o \vert}$ | Model-to-data agreement |
| 8 | **R-free** | Same as R but on 5–10% omitted reflections | Cross-validation |
| 9 | **Ramachandran φ, ψ angles** | φ = N–Cα; ψ = Cα–C' dihedrals | Backbone validation |
| 10 | **Solvent Accessible Surface Area (SASA)** | Lee–Richards rolling-probe algorithm | Buried/exposed residues |
| 11 | **Radius of Gyration (R$_g$)** | $R_g = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_i m_i (\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_{\text{com}})^2}{\sum_i m_i}}$ | Globularity / folding state |
| 12 | **TM-score normalization** | $L_{\text{norm}} = L_{\text{target}}$ (not aligned length) | Structure similarity 0–1 |
| 13 | **RMSD calculation** | $\text{RMSD} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert \vec{r}_i^{\text{obs}} - \vec{r}_i^{\text{ref}} \vert^2}$ | Structural superposition |
| 14 | **Sequence identity %** | $\dfrac{\text{identical residues}}{\text{aligned length}} \times 100$ | Homology threshold |
| 15 | **Molecular weight from sequence** | $MW = 110 \times N_{aa} - 18\,(\text{water})$ Da (approx) | Quick mass estimate |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formula 7 & 8 (R-factor):** Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ rendered as `\vert` in LaTeX to avoid clashing with markdown table pipes. A *good* X-ray structure has $R < 0.25$ and $R_{\text{free}} < 0.30$.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Scope Note:** RCSB-PDB is a database resource, so the "derivation" here focuses on (a) **deriving structural coordinates from raw crystallographic data**, (b) **deriving validation metrics from coordinates**, and (c) **deriving similarity scores** between two PDB entries. These are KTU-favorite derivation problems.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inter-Atomic Distance Between Two Atoms in a PDB Entry

**Given:** A PDB file lists atom `N` of residue 5 of chain A at coordinates $(x_1, y_1, z_1)$ and atom `O` of residue 12 of chain A at $(x_2, y_2, z_2)$. Compute the inter-atomic distance.

### Step 1 — State the General Euclidean 3D Distance Formula

For two points $\vec{P}_1 = (x_1, y_1, z_1)$ and $\vec{P}_2 = (x_2, y_2, z_2)$ in 3D Cartesian space:

$$
d_{12} = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2 + (z_2 - z_1)^2}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the 3D extension of the Pythagorean theorem. The squared distance is the sum of squared differences along each Cartesian axis, and we take the square root to recover length in Ångströms (Å).

### Step 2 — Substitute Example Values

Let $P_1 = (12.345,\ 6.789,\ 4.012)$ Å and $P_2 = (15.678,\ 9.012,\ 7.345)$ Å (typical from a hemoglobin structure).

$$
\Delta x = x_2 - x_1 = 15.678 - 12.345 = 3.333 \text{ \AA}
$$

$$
\Delta y = y_2 - y_1 = 9.012 - 6.789 = 2.223 \text{ \AA}
$$

$$
\Delta z = z_2 - z_1 = 7.345 - 4.012 = 3.333 \text{ \AA}
$$

### Step 3 — Square Each Difference

$$
\Delta x^2 = (3.333)^2 = 11.109 \text{ \AA}^2
$$

$$
\Delta y^2 = (2.223)^2 = 4.942 \text{ \AA}^2
$$

$$
\Delta z^2 = (3.333)^2 = 11.109 \text{ \AA}^2
$$

### Step 4 — Sum and Take Square Root

$$
d_{12} = \sqrt{11.109 + 4.942 + 11.109} = \sqrt{27.160} \approx 5.21 \text{ \AA}
$$

**Result:** $d_{12} \approx 5.21 \text{ Å}$.

> **Sanity Check (KTU Examiner Trick):** A typical hydrogen bond is 2.7–3.3 Å; a covalent bond is 1.0–1.8 Å; a van der Waals contact is 3.0–4.0 Å. A distance of 5.21 Å means these two atoms are **not in direct contact** — likely separated by 2–3 residues along the chain or on different secondary-structure elements.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computing RMSD Between Two Superimposed Structures

**Given:** Two PDB structures `Model_A` and `Model_B`, each with $N = 50$ aligned Cα atoms. Compute RMSD.

### Step 1 — State the RMSD Formula

After optimal superposition (Kabsch algorithm), the **Root-Mean-Square Deviation** is:

$$
\text{RMSD} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert \vec{r}_i^{A} - \vec{r}_i^{B} \vert^2}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We (a) take the **squared Euclidean distance** for every paired atom, (b) **average** across all $N$ atoms, (c) take the **square root** to return to distance units. The squaring penalizes large deviations heavily; the square root returns to Å.

### Step 2 — Hypothetical Residue-Pair Distances (Å)

Assume we have computed $\vert \vec{r}_i^{A} - \vec{r}_i^{B} \vert$ for each of the 50 atoms:

| Pair Index | Distance (Å) | Squared (Å²) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.50 | 0.25 |
| 2 | 0.80 | 0.64 |
| 3 | 1.20 | 1.44 |
| ... | ... | ... |
| 50 | 0.90 | 0.81 |
| **Sum** | — | $\sum d_i^2 = 32.50$ |

### Step 3 — Compute the Mean

$$
\langle d^2 \rangle = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i^2 = \frac{32.50}{50} = 0.650 \text{ \AA}^2
$$

### Step 4 — Take the Square Root

$$
\text{RMSD} = \sqrt{0.650} \approx 0.806 \text{ \AA}
$$

**Result:** $\text{RMSD} \approx 0.81 \text{ Å}$.

> **Sanity Check:** RMSD < 1.0 Å = highly similar folds; 1.0–2.0 Å = similar with small variations; >3.0 Å = substantially different. Our result indicates **near-identical structures**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resolution and Bragg's Law

**Given:** X-ray wavelength $\lambda = 1.5418$ Å (Cu Kα source) and observed diffraction peak at $\theta = 15.0°$. Compute resolution.

### Step 1 — Bragg's Law

$$
n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = \frac{n\lambda}{2\sin\theta}
$$

For first-order diffraction ($n = 1$):

$$
d = \frac{\lambda}{2\sin\theta}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
d = \frac{1.5418}{2 \times \sin(15.0°)} = \frac{1.5418}{2 \times 0.2588} = \frac{1.5418}{0.5176}
$$

$$
d \approx 2.979 \text{ \AA}
$$

**Result:** Resolution $d \approx 2.98$ Å.

> **Interpretation:** This is **medium-resolution** — sufficient to see secondary structure (helices/sheets) but not always individual side-chain rotamers.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — B-factor and Atomic Displacement

**Given:** A residue's Cα atom has B-factor $B = 40.0$ Å². Compute its **mean atomic displacement** $\langle u^2 \rangle$.

### Step 1 — The B-factor Equation

$$
B = 8\pi^2 \langle u^2 \rangle
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $\langle u^2 \rangle$

$$
\langle u^2 \rangle = \frac{B}{8\pi^2}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$
\langle u^2 \rangle = \frac{40.0}{8 \times (3.1416)^2} = \frac{40.0}{78.957} \approx 0.507 \text{ \AA}^2
$$

### Step 4 — Root-Mean-Square Displacement

$$
u_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\langle u^2 \rangle} = \sqrt{0.507} \approx 0.712 \text{ \AA}
$$

**Result:** The atom vibrates on average by $\approx 0.71$ Å about its mean position — typical for a **flexible loop** residue.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for RCSB-PDB Queries

| If User Wants … | Use This RCSB-PDB Tool | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Sequence similar to mine | **Sequence Search** (BLAST) | List of PDB entries with E-value |
| 3D fold similar to mine | **Structure Search** (TM-align, FATCAT) | Aligned superpositions |
| Specific compound bound | **Chemical Search** (SMILES/substructure) | Ligand-bound entries |
| By experimental method | **Advanced Search → Experimental Method** | X-ray / NMR / EM entries |
| Specific organism | **Advanced Search → Source Organism** | Taxonomy-filtered hits |
| Resolution cutoff | **Advanced Search → Resolution** | High-quality subset |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

### Q1. Define RCSB-PDB. Mention any four experimental techniques whose structural data it archives. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
RCSB-PDB (Research Collaboratory for Structural Bioinformatics – Protein Data Bank) is the primary international open-access repository for experimentally determined 3D structures of biological macromolecules, managed by the wwPDB consortium. **(1 mark)**
The four experimental techniques are: **(½ mark each)**
1. **X-ray crystallography**
2. **NMR (Nuclear Magnetic Resonance) spectroscopy**
3. **3D Electron Microscopy (Cryo-EM)**
4. **Neutron diffraction / Integrative/hybrid methods**

---

### Q2. What is a PDB ID? Give one example and state the significance of the Biological Assembly file. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A **PDB ID** (or accession code) is a **4-character alphanumeric unique identifier** assigned by wwPDB to every deposited structure. Example: `6LU7` (SARS-CoV-2 main protease). **(1½ marks)**
The **Biological Assembly file (`*.pdb1`)** represents the **functional, physiologically relevant quaternary structure** of the macromolecule (e.g., a dimer, tetramer), as opposed to the asymmetric unit which may contain only a fraction of the biological form. **(1½ marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### 🅰️ QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Describe the hierarchical data model of an RCSB-PDB entry. Explain each level with a suitable example from a hemoglobin entry. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the validation pipeline used by wwPDB before releasing a structure to the public. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**The Hierarchical Data Model** organizes every PDB entry in a strict top-down hierarchy:

$$
\text{Entry} \rightarrow \text{Entity} \rightarrow \text{Assembly} \rightarrow \text{Model} \rightarrow \text{Chain} \rightarrow \text{Residue} \rightarrow \text{Atom}
$$

Using the example PDB ID **4HHB (Human Hemoglobin, deoxy form)**:

| Level | Definition | Example from 4HHB |
|---|---|---|
| **Entry** | Unique 4-char ID; top container | `4HHB` |
| **Entity** | A unique macromolecule (by sequence) | Entity 1 = α-chain (141 aa); Entity 2 = β-chain (146 aa); Entity 3 = Heme |
| **Assembly** | Biologically functional oligomer | α₂β₂ tetramer |
| **Model** | One 3D coordinate set | Model 1 (X-ray has 1 model) |
| **Chain** | A single polymer chain | Chain A (α), Chain B (α), Chain C (β), Chain D (β) |
| **Residue** | One amino acid or ligand | Val 1, His F8, HEM |
| **Atom** | Single atom with Cartesian coords | N of Val 1 at (x, y, z), B = 12.4 Å² |

**Marks Distribution:** Hierarchy statement (2), each level explained (½ × 6 = 3), example from 4HHB (2).

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

The **wwPDB validation pipeline** runs the following checks before public release:

1. **Format Validation (1 mark):** Ensures the file conforms to the **PDBx/mmCIF dictionary** — correct data types, categories, and mandatory fields. Tool: **OneDep validator**.

2. **Geometric Validation (2 marks):** Checks **bond lengths** (expected ~1.5 Å for C–C, ~1.0 Å for C–H), **bond angles**, **planarity of aromatic rings**, and **chirality** (L-amino acids, not D).

3. **Steric Clash Analysis (1.5 marks):** Uses **MolProbity** to detect atomic overlaps < sum of van der Waals radii. Clash score < 5 is desirable.

4. **Ramachandran Validation (1 mark):** Plots φ, ψ dihedral angles; expects > **98% residues in favored regions**, < 0.5% outliers.

5. **Sequence Cross-Check (1 mark):** Compares the SEQRES (declared) sequence with the ATOM (observed) sequence and cross-references **UniProt** canonical sequence.

6. **Electron Density / Restraint Fit (0.5 marks):** For X-ray and Cryo-EM, real-space correlation between map and model is computed.

If all checks pass → unique **PDB accession code** is issued → entry becomes publicly accessible at rcsb.org.

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the difference between **PDB** and **mmCIF** file formats. List the major sections of a legacy PDB file with one-line descriptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two Cα atoms of a protein are located at $(x_1,y_1,z_1) = (10.0,\ 5.0,\ 3.0)$ Å and $(x_2,y_2,z_2) = (13.0,\ 9.0,\ 7.0)$ Å. Compute (i) the inter-atomic distance, and (ii) the molecular weight of a protein with 250 amino acid residues. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**PDB vs mmCIF Comparison (3 marks):**

| Feature | Legacy PDB | PDBx/mmCIF |
|---|---|---|
| Year introduced | 1976 | 2014 (now mandatory) |
| Format | Fixed-width columns | Key-value dictionary |
| Capacity | 99999 atoms (limitation) | Unlimited |
| Data types | Only coordinates + metadata | Rich metadata, categories |
| Readability | Human-friendly columns | Machine-friendly tags |
| File extension | `.pdb` | `.cif` |
| Primary use | Legacy software | Modern pipelines |

**Major Sections of a Legacy PDB File (4 marks – ½ each):**

1. **HEADER** — Title, deposition date, PDB ID
2. **OBSLTE / TITLE / COMPND / SOURCE** — Bibliographic and biological source
3. **SEQRES** — Declared sequence of each chain
4. **HELIX / SHEET** — Secondary structure records
5. **ATOM / HETATM** — Atomic coordinates (x, y, z, occupancy, B-factor)
6. **CONECT / LINK / SSBOND** — Connectivity (bonds, disulfide)
7. **CRYST1** — Crystallographic cell parameters (a, b, c, α, β, γ, space group)
8. **REMARK** — Free-text annotations (resolution, R-factor, refinement details)
9. **TER / END / MASTER** — Chain termination and end-of-file markers

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**(i) Inter-atomic distance (4 marks):**

Using the Euclidean distance formula:

$$
d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}
$$

Step 1: Compute differences:

$$
\Delta x = 13.0 - 10.0 = 3.0 \text{ \AA}
$$

$$
\Delta y = 9.0 - 5.0 = 4.0 \text{ \AA}
$$

$$
\Delta z = 7.0 - 3.0 = 4.0 \text{ \AA}
$$

Step 2: Square each:

$$
\Delta x^2 = 9.0,\quad \Delta y^2 = 16.0,\quad \Delta z^2 = 16.0
$$

Step 3: Sum and square root:

$$
d = \sqrt{9.0 + 16.0 + 16.0} = \sqrt{41.0} \approx 6.403 \text{ \AA}
$$

**(ii) Molecular weight (3 marks):**

Using the approximation $MW \approx 110 \times N_{aa} - 18$ (subtracting one water for the linear chain):

$$
MW = 110 \times 250 - 18 = 27500 - 18 = 27482 \text{ Da} \approx 27.5 \text{ kDa}
$$

**Final Answers:** $d \approx 6.40$ Å; $MW \approx 27.48$ kDa.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Traps on RCSB-PDB Questions:**
> - **Confusing asymmetric unit with biological assembly** — this costs 1–2 marks routinely. Remember: asymmetric unit = crystallographic; biological assembly = *functional* form.
> - **Forgetting units** — always quote distances in **Ångströms (Å)** and molecular weights in **Da (or kDa)**.
> - **Writing "mmCIF" without expansion** — full form **macromolecular Crystallographic Information File** is mandatory for full marks.
> - **Skipping validation metrics** — examiners expect at least **R-factor, R-free, Ramachandran, MolProbity clash score** when discussing quality.
> - **Wrong number of decimal places in distance calculation** — at least **2 decimals** for Å-level distances.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these high-yield facts before the exam:**

- 🧬 **RCSB-PDB = global open-access 3D structure archive** of proteins, DNA, RNA, complexes.
- 🏛️ **Managed by wwPDB consortium** (RCSB + PDBe + PDBj + BMRB).
- 📅 **Founded 1971** at Brookhaven; **RCSB established 1998**.
- 🔑 **4-character PDB ID** uniquely identifies each entry (e.g., `4HHB` for deoxyhemoglobin).
- 📂 **Two main formats:** legacy `.pdb` (fixed-width, deprecated for deposition) and modern `.cif` (mmCIF/PDBx, **mandatory since 2014**).
- 🧪 **Experimental techniques:** **X-ray crystallography** (most common), **NMR** (multiple models), **Cryo-EM** (rapidly growing), integrative methods.
- 🌐 **Portal:** https://www.rcsb.org/ (also features PDB-101 educational portal).
- 🔍 **Search capabilities:** text, sequence (BLAST), structure (TM-align), chemical (SMILES).
- 👁️ **3D viewer:** **NGL Viewer** and **Mol\*** (Molstar).
- ✅ **Validation metrics:** **R-factor < 0.25**, **R-free < 0.30**, **Ramachandran favored > 98%**, **MolProbity clash < 5**, resolution < 2.5 Å.
- 📐 **Hierarchy:** Entry → Entity → Assembly → Model → Chain → Residue → Atom.
- 🧮 **Key formulas:** Euclidean distance $d = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2 + \Delta z^2}$, RMSD, R-factor, Bragg's law $d = \lambda/(2\sin\theta)$, B-factor $B = 8\pi^2 \langle u^2 \rangle$.
- 💊 **Real-world impact:** Drug design (Imatinib/Ableson kinase), COVID-19 vaccine acceleration, AlphaFold training set.
- 🔗 **Cross-references:** SIFTS maps every PDB chain to **UniProt**, **Pfam**, **GO**, **EC number**, **taxonomy**.
- 📊 **Archive scale (2024):** > **220,000 structures**, growing at ~10–13% annually.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — RCSB-PDB | KTU 2024 Scheme | PBBBT604 — Bioinformatics**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EMBL-EB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT604 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** EMBL-EB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**EMBL (European Molecular Biology Laboratory)** is an intergovernmental research organization founded in **1974**, supported by over **25 member states** including most European nations, Israel, and Australia. It functions as a basic research institute in molecular life sciences, operating across **six sites** in Europe (Heidelberg, Hinxton, Hamburg, Grenoble, Rome, and Barcelona).

**EBI (European Bioinformatics Institute)** is the bioinformatics arm of EMBL, established in **1994** and located on the **Wellcome Genome Campus in Hinxton, near Cambridge, UK**. It serves as Europe's primary public repository and computational service hub for biological big data, hosting open-access databases, analysis tools, and training resources free of charge to the global scientific community.

> [!NOTE]
> **Joint EMBL-EBI Identity:** The institute is jointly branded as "EMBL-EBI" because the bioinformatics operations are deeply integrated with EMBL's broader molecular biology research. The Hinxton campus also hosts the **Sanger Institute** (genome sequencing) and the **Wellcome Trust**, creating a unique life-sciences cluster.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of **EMBL-EBI as the "European Library of Alexandria" for biology**. Imagine a vast, free, public library where:

- **Books** = biological sequences, structures, and molecular data
- **The Catalogue System** = curated databases (ENA, UniProt, PDBe, Ensembl)
- **Reading Assistants** = bioinformatics tools (BLAST, ClustalW, InterProScan)
- **Card Catalogue** = cross-referenced accession numbers linking related data

Just as a librarian organizes, indexes, and lends books, EMBL-EBI **organizes, curates, indexes, and freely distributes** genomic, proteomic, and structural biological data to researchers worldwide. While the **NCBI (USA)** acts as the American equivalent and **DDBJ (Japan)** as the Asian counterpart, the three together form the **International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration (INSDC)** — a tripartite alliance ensuring global data parity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight:** EMBL-EBI is *not* a single database — it is an **institute** that *hosts and develops* dozens of specialized databases. Students must distinguish between EMBL (the parent lab) and EBI (the bioinformatics division).

### 1.3 Why EMBL-EBI Matters in Bioinformatics

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Open Access Policy** | All data is freely accessible without registration |
| **Data Volume** | Hosts **petabytes** of biomolecular data |
| **Tool Ecosystem** | Provides **100+ analysis tools** integrated with databases |
| **Training** | Offers courses via **Train Online** and **EMBL-EBI Hands-on** |
| **Standardization** | Champions open data formats (FASTA, EMBL flat-file, XML) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Organizational Architecture

EMBL-EBI operates under a layered architecture that mirrors the biological data hierarchy:

- **Layer 1 — Submission Layer:** Accepts raw sequencing reads, assembled sequences, and annotated records from researchers worldwide via standardized portals (e.g., **Webin**, **Europe PMC Plus**).
- **Layer 2 — Curation Layer:** Expert biocurators validate, annotate, and cross-reference submissions using controlled vocabularies (Gene Ontology, Sequence Ontology, ChEBI).
- **Layer 3 — Storage Layer:** Redundant, mirrored data centers (UK, plus mirror at **Geneva, Switzerland** and **Amazon Web Services cloud**).
- **Layer 4 — Retrieval Layer:** Public query interfaces (REST APIs, web portals, FTP servers, programmatic access via BioServices, Biopython).
- **Layer 5 — Analysis Layer:** Integrated tools for sequence similarity searching, multiple alignment, structural visualization, and pathway analysis.

### 2.2 Core Databases Hosted at EMBL-EBI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **"Big Five"** databases that KTU examiners frequently test. Memorize their full names, data types, and accession prefixes.

| Database | Full Name | Data Type | Accession Prefix Example | Mirror Partner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ENA** | European Nucleotide Archive | Raw reads, assembled sequences, annotations | **LR, ER, SR** (reads), **AL, BN** (assemblies) | NCBI GenBank, DDBJ |
| **UniProt** | Universal Protein Resource | Protein sequences + functional annotation | **P, Q** (UniProtKB), **I** (UniParc) | SIB (Swiss-Prot curation), PIR |
| **PDBe** | Protein Data Bank in Europe | 3D macromolecular structures | **PDB** (4-char code, e.g., 1A2B) | RCSB PDB (USA), PDBj (Japan) |
| **Ensembl** | Genome assemblies + annotation | Annotated genomes of vertebrates + model organisms | **ENSG** (gene), **ENST** (transcript) | UCSC Genome Browser, NCBI |
| **ArrayExpress** | Gene expression atlas | Microarray + RNA-seq expression data | **E-GEOD, E-MTAB** | NCBI GEO |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Accession Number Patterns

> [!NOTE]
> While EMBL-EBI is not mathematically intensive, **accession number conventions** follow logical patterns that examiners may ask you to interpret.

| Concept | Pattern / Rule | Example | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| ENA Read Accession | `[2-letter prefix] + [6-9 digits] + [version]` | ERR123456.1 | ERR = ENA Read, SRR = NCBI mirror |
| UniProtKB/Swiss-Prot | `[6-char alphanumeric] | O, P, Q prefix + 5 digits` | P53\_HUMAN = **P04637** | Reviewed, manually curated |
| UniProtKB/TrEMBL | `[6-char] | [OPQ][0-9][A-Z][0-9A-Z]{3}` | A0A123XYZ1 | Computationally annotated, unreviewed |
| PDB Code | `[digit][alphanumeric 3 chars]` | 1A2B, 4HHB | Always 4 characters |
| Ensembl Gene ID | `ENSG` + `[11 digits]` | ENSG00000141510 | Stable across releases |

### 2.4 EMBL Flat-File Format (Legacy Format)

The original EMBL nucleotide flat-file format (still used for ENA submissions) has a strict **two-letter line-type code** structure:

| Line Code | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `ID` | Identification line | ID   AF123456; SV 1; linear; mRNA; STD; PLN; 432 BP. |
| `AC` | Accession number | AC   AF123456; |
| `DT` | Date | DT   05-JAN-2024 (Rel. 130, Created) |
| `DE` | Description | DE   Arabidopsis thaliana actin mRNA |
| `KW` | Keywords | KW   cytoskeleton; actin. |
| `OS` | Organism species | OS   Arabidopsis thaliana (thale cress) |
| `OC` | Organism classification | OC   Eukaryota; Viridiplantae; Streptophyta; ... |
| `OG` | Organelle | OG   chloroplast (plastid) |
| `RN` | Reference number | RN   [1] |
| `RA` | Reference authors | RA   Smith J., Doe A.; |
| `RT` | Reference title | RT   "Characterization of plant actins"; |
| `RL` | Reference location | RL   J. Mol. Biol. 312(2):123-135(2001). |
| `DR` | Database cross-reference | DR   UniProtKB/Swiss-Prot; P0CJ46; ACT7\_ARATH. |
| `FH` | Feature header | FH   Key             Location/Qualifiers |
| `FT` | Feature table data | FT   CDS             34..402 |
| `SQ` | Sequence header | SQ   Sequence 432 BP; 119 A; 102 C; 98 G; 113 T; 0 other; |
| `//` | Termination | `//` |

### 2.5 International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration (INSDC)

EMBL-EBI operates under a unique data-sharing treaty with NCBI and DDBJ:

- Any sequence submitted to **any one** of the three is mirrored to the other two within **24 hours**.
- This ensures **no single point of failure** for global genomic data.
- The collaboration mandates a **24-hour data release lag** for patent-sensitive submissions (via the *pre-publication* mechanism).

### 2.6 ELIXIR — The European Infrastructure

**ELIXIR** (since **2014**) is a pan-European intergovernmental organization that coordinates bioinformatics resources across member states. EMBL-EBI serves as ELIXIR's **central coordinating node**, while each country operates a **national node** (e.g., ELIXIR-UK, ELIXIR-Italy, ELIXIR-India as observer).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU: **ELIXIR ≠ EMBL-EBI**, but they share the same campus and operational philosophy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Data Submission to Public Release at ENA

This step-by-step breakdown reflects the **operational pipeline** that examiners love to test as a "describe the workflow" question worth **7 marks**.

**Step 1 — Researcher Preparation**

The submitting lab assembles raw sequencing reads (FASTQ format) and verifies quality using tools such as **FastQC**. They generate an **MD5 checksum** to ensure data integrity during transfer.

$$\text{MD5}(\text{file}) \rightarrow \text{32-character hexadecimal hash}$$

**Step 2 — Webin Portal Upload**

The researcher logs into the **Webin** submission portal (https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/submit/sra/) and provides:

- **Sample metadata** (organism, tissue, isolation source)
- **Study registration** (project umbrella, accession prefix assigned)
- **Experiment description** (library strategy, platform, instrument model)

**Step 3 — File Transfer**

Data is uploaded via:
- **Web browser** (small files, <2 GB)
- **Aspera** (high-speed for large files)
- **FTP** (programmatic, scripted)

**Step 4 — Validation Pipeline**

ENA's automated pipeline performs:
- **Format validation** (FASTQ integrity, base quality encoding)
- **Read count consistency** between metadata and file
- **Taxonomic ID validation** against the NCBI taxonomy

**Step 5 — Accession Number Assignment**

ENA assigns accession numbers following a hierarchical pattern:

$$\text{Accession} = \underbrace{\text{2-letter prefix}}_{\text{database type}} + \underbrace{\text{6 alphanumeric}}_{\text{unique ID}} + \underbrace{\text{version}}_{\text{incremental}}$$

For example:
- `ERR` → ENA Raw Read
- `ERX` → ENA Experiment
- `ERS` → ENA Sample
- `ERP` → ENA Study (Project)

**Step 6 — Public Release**

After the **hold date** (default: immediate release; can be delayed up to 2 years for publication), the data flows into the INSDC mirror network and becomes searchable via:

- **ENA Browser** (web interface)
- **NCBI SRA Toolkit** (mirror access)
- **DDBJ SRA** (Japanese mirror)
- **Programmatic REST API**: `https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/portal/api/search`

### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: Cross-Referencing Between Databases

A key bioinformatics skill is **traversing the data graph** between EMBL-EBI resources.

**Starting Point:** A nucleotide sequence from ENA with accession `AF123456`.

**Step 1 — Identify the linked protein.**

The `FT` (feature table) contains a `CDS` (coding sequence) entry. The `translation` qualifier yields a protein sequence, and `db_xref="UniProtKB/Swiss-Prot:P04637"` provides a direct link.

**Step 2 — Navigate to UniProt.**

Open `https://www.uniprot.org/uniprot/P04637`. This reveals:
- Protein function
- Subcellular localization
- Disease associations
- Cross-references to PDBe, Ensembl, Reactome, Pfam

**Step 3 — Navigate to PDBe.**

From the UniProt page's "Structure" section, follow the PDB cross-reference to view the 3D coordinates (e.g., `1A1U` for p53 DNA-binding domain).

**Step 4 — Navigate to Reactome.**

From UniProt → "Pathway" → Reactome to visualize the protein's role in the **p53 signaling pathway**.

**Step 5 — Programmatic Retrieval (REST API).**

Modern retrieval uses **RESTful endpoints**:

$$\text{GET} \ \text{https://www.ebi.ac.uk/proteins/api/proteins/P04637}$$

This returns JSON/XML metadata, integrating seamlessly with downstream analysis pipelines.

### 3.3 Quantitative Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often asks: "Compare EMBL-EBI with NCBI." Use this table as your exam-ready answer scaffold.

| Feature | EMBL-EBI (Europe) | NCBI (USA) | DDBJ (Japan) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Founded** | 1994 (EBI) / 1974 (EMBL) | 1988 | 1986 |
| **Parent Org** | EMBL (intergovernmental) | NLM (US Government) | NIG (Japan) |
| **Primary Nucleotide DB** | ENA | GenBank | DDBJ |
| **Primary Protein DB** | UniProt (with SIB/PIR) | GenPept + RefSeq | — |
| **Structure DB** | PDBe | RCSB PDB | PDBj |
| **Search Engine** | EBI Search | Entrez | DDBJ ARSA |
| **Sequence Similarity** | EBI BLAST | NCBI BLAST | — |
| **Data Policy** | Open access (CC0/CC-BY) | Open access | Open access |
| **Cloud Mirror** | AWS + Google Cloud | AWS + Google Cloud | NIG supercomputer |

### 3.4 Tools Available at EMBL-EBI (Categorized)

| Category | Tool Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Sequence Search** | EBI BLAST, FASTA, SSEARCH | Similarity search vs ENA/UniProt |
| **Multiple Alignment** | Clustal Omega, MUSCLE, MAFFT | Align 3+ sequences |
| **Protein Analysis** | InterProScan, Pfam, PRINTS | Domain/motif identification |
| **Structure** | PDBeFold, PDBeMotif, Ligand Expo | Structural alignment & ligand info |
| **Genomics** | Ensembl REST, Ensembl VEP, Variant Effect Predictor | Variant annotation |
| **Pathways** | Reactome, IntAct, Complex Portal | Interaction & pathway data |
| **Chemogenomics** | ChEMBL, ChEBI | Bioactive molecules & ontology |
| **Literature** | Europe PMC, CiteXplore | Open-access publications |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
