Give one example of each. **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer (3 Marks distribution):** | Step | Marks | Content | |---|---|---| | Motif definition | 1 | A short, conserved biologically-functional sequence element (TATA box, zinc-finger) | | Pattern definition | 1 | Computational abstraction of a motif — consensus, regex, or profile | | Example each | 1 | TATA box (motif) → `[CT]ATA[AT]A[AG]` (regex pattern) | **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Retest Paper]** What is a **sequence logo**? Mention the two pieces of information it conveys at each position. **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Step | Marks | Content | |---|---|---| | Definition | 1 | A graphical representation of a motif showing conservation per position | | Information conveyed | 2 | (i) Height of stack = information content $R_j$ in bits; (ii) Relative letter heights = frequency of each nucleotide at that position | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks) #### ⭐ Question A — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Slot] **(a)** Explain the construction of a **Position Weight Matrix (PWM)** from a set of aligned sequences with an example. Show how the **pseudocount** is applied. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Define **Information Content** of a motif. For the aligned motif block below (5 sequences, length 4), calculate the information content at each position. **(7 Marks)** ``` Seq1: A C G T Seq2: A C G T Seq3: A A G C Seq4: A C G T Seq5: G C A T ``` **Solution (a) — Construction of PWM (7 Marks):** | Step | Marks | Content | |---|---|---| | 1. PFM from counts | 2 | Build $c_{j,a}$ at each position $j$ for letters $a \in \{A,C,G,T\}$ | | 2. Apply pseudocount $\alpha$ | 2 | $p_{j,a} = (c_{j,a}+\alpha)/(N+4\alpha)$ to avoid $\log 0$ | | 3. Background $q_a$ | 1 | Choose background (often uniform 0.25) | | 4. Log-odds conversion | 2 | $S_{j,a} = \log_2(p_{j,a}/q_a)$ | | Worked numerical example | included | Optional but earns full marks | **Worked PWM example for the motif block above:** *Step 1 — PFM counts (N = 5):* Position 1: A = 4, C = 0, G = 1, T = 0 Position 2: C = 3, A = 1, G = 0, T = 1 Position 3: G = 3, A = 2, C = 0, T = 0 Position 4: T = 3, C = 1, A = 0, G = 1 *Step 2 — Smoothed frequencies with $\alpha = 1$ ($N + 4\alpha = 9$):* Position 1: $p_A = 5/9,\; p_C = 1/9,\; p_G = 2/9,\; p_T = 1/9$ *Step 3 — PWM with $q = 0.25$:* $$S_{1,A} = \log_2\frac{5/9}{0.25} = \log_2 2.222 = 1.152$$ $$S_{1,C} = \log_2\frac{1/9}{0.25} = \log_2 0.444 = -1.170$$ $$S_{1,G} = \log_2\frac{2/9}{0.25} = \log_2 0.889 = -0.170$$ $$S_{1,T} = \log_2\frac{1/9}{0.25} = -1.170$$ (Repeat similarly for positions 2, 3, 4.) **Solution (b) — Information Content (7 Marks):** Position 1 raw frequencies (no pseudocount): $p_A = 0.8,\; p_G = 0.2,\; p_C = p_T = 0$. $$H_1 = -\!\left(0.8\log_2 0.8 + 0.2\log_2 0.2 + 0 + 0\right)$$ $$= -\!\left(0.8 \times (-0.3219) + 0.2 \times (-2.3219)\right)$$ $$= -\!\left(-0.2575 - 0.4644\right) = 0.7219\;\text{bits}$$ $$R_1 = 2 - 0.7219 = 1.2781\;\text{bits}$$ Position 2 raw frequencies: $p_C = 0.6,\; p_A = 0.2,\; p_T = 0.2$. $$H_2 = -\!\left(0.6\log_2 0.6 + 0.2\log_2 0.2 + 0.2\log_2 0.2\right)$$ $$= -\!\left(0.6 \times (-0.7370) + 2 \times 0.2 \times (-2.3219)\right)$$ $$= -\!\left(-0.4422 - 0.9288\right) = 1.3710\;\text{bits}$$ $$R_2 = 2 - 1.3710 = 0.6290\;\text{bits}$$ Position 3 raw frequencies: $p_G = 0.6,\; p_A = 0.4$. $$H_3 = -\!\left(0.6\log_2 0.6 + 0.4\log_2 0.4\right)$$ $$= -\!\left(-0.4422 - 0.5288\right) = 0.9710\;\text{bits}$$ $$R_3 = 2 - 0.9710 = 1.0290\;\text{bits}$$ Position 4 raw frequencies: $p_T = 0.6,\; p_C = 0.2,\; p_G = 0.2$. $$H_4 = -\!\left(0.6\log_2 0.6 + 2 \times 0.2 \times \log_2 0.2\right) = 1.3710\;\text{bits}$$ $$R_4 = 2 - 1.3710 = 0.6290\;\text{bits}$$ $$\boxed{R_{\text{total}} = 1.2781 + 0.6290 + 1.0290 + 0.6290 = 3.5651\;\text{bits}}$$ **Final Consensus (most frequent letter per position): A – C – G – T** Total motif length = 4 bp, max possible $R = 4 \times 2 = 8$ bits → our motif retains **3.57 / 8 = 44.6 %** of the maximum information. --- #### ⭐ Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary] **(a)** What are **regular expressions** in the PROSITE sense? Write the PROSITE pattern for the **TATA box** and explain each symbol. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A PWM has the following two columns (assume uniform background $q = 0.25$). A 2-bp window "AT" receives what total log-odds score? Comment on its significance. **(7 Marks)** | Position | A | C | G | T | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1.15 | -1.17 | -0.17 | -1.17 | | 2 | -1.00 | 0.45 | -0.40 | 1.20 | **Solution (a) — PROSITE Pattern (7 Marks):** | Symbol | Marks | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Definition of PROSITE regex | 1 | Compact character-based pattern for motif matching | | TATA box pattern | 1 | `[CT]A{1,2}TATA[AT]A[AG]` | | Symbol-by-symbol explanation | 4 | `[CT]` = C or T; `A{1,2}` = 1 or 2 As; `TATA` = exact; `[AT]A` = A or T then A; `[AG]` = A or G | | Use case | 1 | Scans UniProt/SwissProt for putative TATA-like promoters | **Solution (b) — PWM Score Calculation (7 Marks):** *Step 1 — Look up scores:* For window "AT" of length 2: $w_1 = A$, $w_2 = T$. $$S_1 = S_{1,A} = +1.15\;\text{bits}$$ $$S_2 = S_{2,T} = +1.20\;\text{bits}$$ *Step 2 — Sum:* $$S(W) = 1.15 + 1.20 = 2.35\;\text{bits}$$ *Step 3 — Compare to maximum:* Maximum possible score = $1.15 + 1.20 = 2.35$ bits (since AT are the two highest-scoring letters), so this window is a **perfect PWM match**. *Step 4 — Significance interpretation (Z-score approximation):* If calibration gives $\mu_S = -0.5$, $\sigma_S = 0.8$: $$Z = \frac{2.35 - (-0.5)}{0.8} = \frac{2.85}{0.8} = 3.56$$ $Z = 3.56$ ⇒ one-tailed $p \approx 1.9 \times 10^{-4}$ ⇒ **statistically significant** motif hit. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing motif vs. pattern (–1 mark):** Always write the *biological* element first (motif), then the *computational* form (pattern). > 2. **Forgetting the pseudocount (–1 to –2 marks):** PWM cells with raw $c/N$ become $-\infty$ whenever $c = 0$. Examiners deduct heavily for omitting $\alpha$. > 3. **Sign error in $R_j$:** The formula is $R_j = 2 + \sum p_{j,a}\log_2 p_{j,a}$, **not** $2 - \sum p\log p$. Many students flip the sign and lose 2 marks. > 4. **Units missing (–0.5 each):** Always write "bits" after every information-content number. > 5. **No E-value discussion (–2 marks on long answers):** Just giving a score is incomplete; a complete answer must include the E-value or p-value interpretation. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] **Motif = biology (functional element); Pattern = computational abstraction (consensus/regex/profile).** - [ ] **PFM → PWM conversion is the heart of motif identification** — must include pseudocount. - [ ] **Schneider's formula** $R_j = \log_2(\vert\Sigma\vert) + \sum_a p_{j,a}\log_2 p_{j,a}$ — sign convention is *plus*, not minus. - [ ] **Bits units** — nucleic acids max **2 bits/position**, proteins max **≈ 4.32 bits/position**. - [ ] **Z-score** $Z = (S-\mu)/\sigma$; significance threshold typically $Z \geq 3$. - [ ] **E-value** $E = Kmn e^{-\lambda S}$; hits are significant if $E \leq 10^{-4}$. - [ ] **PROSITE pattern syntax:** `[AC]`, `{AC}`, `x`, `(x){n}`, `x(m,n)`. - [ ] **Sequence logo** displays $R_j$ (stack height) and letter frequency (relative heights). - [ ] **Common motif-finding tools:** MEME (EM), Gibbs Sampler (stochastic), HMMER (HMM), TRANSFAC/MATCH (PWM scanning). - [ ] **Always state biological validation** — motif hits must be confirmed against literature, ChIP-seq, or conservation across orthologues. - [ ] **Threshold score** for declaring a hit: $T = 0.7 \times S_{\max}$ is a common heuristic. - [ ] **Laplace pseudocount** $\alpha$: pick $\alpha = 0.5$ (small samples) or $\alpha = \sqrt{N}/4$ (large samples, Schneider's recipe). ---

topics.title: Hidden Markov Model; Phylogenetic Analysis: Elements of Phylogenetic Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT604 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hidden Markov Model; Phylogenetic Analysis: Elements of Phylogenetic Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hidden Markov Model (HMM) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hidden Markov Model (HMM):** A *Hidden Markov Model* is a statistical Markov chain in which the system being modeled is assumed to be a **Markov process with unobserved (hidden) states**, and the observations (emissions) are probabilistic functions of these hidden states. Formally, an HMM is defined by the 5-tuple $\lambda = (S, V, A, B, \pi)$.

| Symbol | Name | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $S$ | Hidden state set | $S = \{S_1, S_2, \dots, S_N\}$ with $N$ states |
| $V$ | Observation alphabet | $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_M\}$ with $M$ symbols |
| $A$ | Transition matrix | $A = \{a_{ij}\}$ where $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1}=S_j \mid q_t=S_i)$ |
| $B$ | Emission matrix | $B = \{b_j(k)\}$ where $b_j(k) = P(v_k \text{ at } t \mid q_t=S_j)$ |
| $\pi$ | Initial distribution | $\pi_i = P(q_1 = S_i)$ |

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Tunnel Cave"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a blindfolded friend (the *observer*) inside a pitch-dark cave with multiple tunnels. The friend cannot see *which* tunnel he is in (this is the **hidden state**), but he can feel the temperature, hear the dripping water, and smell the air at each spot (these are the **observations**). Knowing only the sequence of sensory readings, can we guess which tunnel he walked through? Yes — this is exactly what an HMM does in bioinformatics: from an *observed sequence* (DNA/Protein), we infer the *hidden biological state* (gene, exon, intron, promoter, etc.).

### Two Key Assumptions of an HMM

1. **Markov Assumption (Limited Horizon):** The probability of being in state $S_j$ at time $t+1$ depends *only* on the state at time $t$.
$$P(q_{t+1}=S_j \mid q_1, q_2, \dots, q_t) = P(q_{t+1}=S_j \mid q_t)$$
2. **Output Independence Assumption:** The observation at time $t$ depends *only* on the hidden state at time $t$, and not on any other observations or states.
$$P(O_t \mid q_1, q_2, \dots, q_T, O_1, \dots, O_{T-1}) = P(O_t \mid q_t)$$

---

## 1.2 Phylogenetic Models — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phylogenetic Model:** A *phylogenetic model* is a mathematical framework that describes the **evolutionary substitution process** by which one biological character (nucleotide or amino acid) replaces another along a branch of a phylogenetic tree, governed by a *rate matrix* $Q$ and an *evolutionary distance* (branch length) $t$.

### Core Components of a Phylogenetic Model

| Component | Symbol | Biological Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Tree topology | $\tau$ | Branching pattern showing ancestral relationships |
| Branch lengths | $t$ | Expected number of substitutions per site |
| Substitution model | $Q$ | Rate matrix defining probabilities of character change |
| Alignment | $\mathcal{D}$ | Multiple sequence alignment input to the model |
| Substitution rate | $r$ | Per-site per-unit-time probability of change |

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Great-Grandparent Language Drift"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine 5 grandchildren speaking modern Indian languages (Hindi, Tamil, Malayalam, Bengali, Marathi). All these languages descended from a common *Proto-language* spoken by their great-grandparent. The languages have *diverged* (substitutions), but some words are still recognizable (conserved characters). A phylogenetic model is like a *linguistic diffusion equation*: given the modern "words" (sequences) and time elapsed (branch length), it estimates how the ancestral language (root sequence) might have sounded, and *how fast* words changed (substitution rate). In biology, the "language" is DNA, the "words" are codons, and the "grandparent" is the Last Universal Common Ancestor (LUCA).

---

## 1.3 Why HMMs and Phylogenetic Models Coexist in Bioinformatics

| Tool | Typical Biological Application |
|---|---|
| HMM | Gene finding (GENSCAN), Protein family modelling (Pfam HMMs), Sequence alignment (profile HMMs), CpG island detection |
| Phylogenetic Models | Reconstructing evolutionary trees, Measuring divergence times, Detecting positive selection (dN/dS), Molecular clock dating |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Problems of HMMs

Rabiner (1989) formalized three core computational problems that any HMM must solve:

### Problem 1 — The Evaluation Problem (Scoring)
> [!NOTE]
> **Given** $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ and an observation sequence $O = O_1 O_2 \dots O_T$, **find** $P(O \mid \lambda)$.
- **Algorithm Used:** Forward Algorithm
- **Bioinformatics Use:** "How likely is this DNA sequence given my model of a gene?"

### Problem 2 — The Decoding Problem (Path Prediction)
> [!NOTE]
> **Given** $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ and $O$, **find** the optimal hidden state sequence $Q^* = q_1 q_2 \dots q_T$.
- **Algorithm Used:** **Viterbi Algorithm** (dynamic programming)
- **Bioinformatics Use:** "What is the most likely gene structure of this DNA sequence?"

### Problem 3 — The Learning Problem (Parameter Estimation)
> [!NOTE]
> **Given** an observation sequence $O$, **find** the model parameters $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ that maximize $P(O \mid \lambda)$.
- **Algorithm Used:** **Baum–Welch Algorithm** (a special case of Expectation-Maximization)
- **Bioinformatics Use:** "Train a model that best represents this family of protein sequences."

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Viterbi Algorithm (Decoding)

The Viterbi algorithm finds the *single best* state path. It uses dynamic programming.

1. **Initialization (t = 1):** Compute the probability of being in each state and generating the first observation:
$$\delta_1(i) = \pi_i \, b_i(O_1), \quad \psi_1(i) = 0$$
2. **Recursion (t = 2 to T):** For each state $j$ at time $t$, find the best preceding state:
$$\delta_t(j) = \max_{1 \le i \le N} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \, a_{ij} \right] \, b_j(O_t)$$
$$\psi_t(j) = \arg\max_{1 \le i \le N} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \, a_{ij} \right]$$
3. **Termination:** The best path probability is:
$$P^* = \max_{1 \le i \le N} \delta_T(i), \quad q_T^* = \arg\max_{1 \le i \le N} \delta_T(i)$$
4. **Path Backtracking:** Reconstruct $q_{T-1}^*, q_{T-2}^*, \dots, q_1^*$ using the $\psi$ pointers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Log-space trick:** Since probabilities underflow to zero for long sequences, all computations are performed in log-space:
> $$\log \delta_t(j) = \max_i \left[ \log \delta_{t-1}(i) + \log a_{ij} \right] + \log b_j(O_t)$$

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of the Forward–Backward Algorithm (Evaluation)

The Forward variable $\alpha_t(i)$ = probability of the partial observation sequence $O_1 \dots O_t$ *and* state $S_i$ at time $t$.

$$\alpha_1(i) = \pi_i \, b_i(O_1)$$
$$\alpha_t(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \, a_{ij} \right] b_j(O_t)$$

The Backward variable $\beta_t(i)$ = probability of the partial observation sequence $O_{t+1} \dots O_T$ given state $S_i$ at time $t$.

$$\beta_T(i) = 1$$
$$\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \, b_j(O_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(j)$$

The total probability is:
$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \beta_t(i)$$

---

## 2.4 Elements of Phylogenetic Substitution Models

### The Rate Matrix (Q)
A phylogenetic model is fully defined by a **4 × 4 rate matrix** $Q$ for DNA (or 20 × 20 for proteins). Each entry $q_{ij}$ gives the instantaneous rate of substitution from nucleotide $i$ to nucleotide $j$.

### Model 1 — Jukes–Cantor (JC69)
The simplest model: **all substitutions occur at equal rate $\mu$**, and the four nucleotides are equally frequent ($\pi_A = \pi_T = \pi_G = \pi_C = 0.25$).

$$Q_{JC} = \mu \begin{bmatrix} -3 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -3 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & -3 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

The probability of observing nucleotide $j$ after time $t$ starting from $i$:

$$P_{ij}(t) = \frac{1}{4} \left( 1 - e^{-4\mu t} \right) \quad \text{if } i \neq j$$
$$P_{ii}(t) = \frac{1}{4} \left( 1 + 3 e^{-4\mu t} \right)$$

### Model 2 — Kimura 2-Parameter (K80/K2P)
Distinguishes **transitions** (purine $\leftrightarrow$ purine: A↔G; pyrimidine ↔ pyrimidine: C↔T) from **transversions** (purine ↔ pyrimidine).

$$Q_{K2P} = \begin{bmatrix} -(\alpha+2\beta) & \beta & \alpha & \beta \\ \beta & -(\alpha+2\beta) & \beta & \alpha \\ \alpha & \beta & -(\alpha+2\beta) & \beta \\ \beta & \alpha & \beta & -(\alpha+2\beta) \end{bmatrix}$$

### Distance Correction Formulas
The **observed** fraction of differences $p$ underestimates the **true evolutionary distance** $d$ because multiple substitutions can be hidden.

| Model | Distance Formula $d$ |
|---|---|
| JC69 | $d_{JC} = -\frac{3}{4} \ln\left(1 - \frac{4}{3}p\right)$ |
| K2P | $d_{K2P} = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(1-2P-Q) - \frac{1}{4}\ln(1-2Q)$ |
| Raw (p-distance) | $d = p$ (no correction) |

where $P$ = fraction of transitions, $Q$ = fraction of transversions.

### Maximum Likelihood Estimation of Branch Length
The likelihood of observing sequence $j$ given ancestor $i$ after branch length $t$:

$$L(t) = P_{ij}(t)$$
$$\frac{dL}{dt} = 0 \implies t^* = \text{MLE branch length}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Markov property | $P(q_{t+1} \mid q_1 \dots q_t) = P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t)$ | Foundational assumption |
| 2 | Output independence | $P(O_t \mid \text{all history}) = P(O_t \mid q_t)$ | HMM second assumption |
| 3 | Forward recursion | $\alpha_t(j) = \left[\sum_i \alpha_{t-1}(i) a_{ij}\right] b_j(O_t)$ | For evaluation |
| 4 | Viterbi recursion | $\delta_t(j) = \max_i \left[\delta_{t-1}(i) a_{ij}\right] b_j(O_t)$ | For decoding |
| 5 | Baum–Welch update (emission) | $b_j(k)^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_{t: O_t=v_k} \gamma_t(j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} \gamma_t(j)}$ | $\gamma_t(j) = P(q_t=S_j \mid O, \lambda)$ |
| 6 | Baum–Welch update (transition) | $a_{ij}^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i,j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i)}$ | $\xi_t(i,j) = P(q_t=i, q_{t+1}=j \mid O, \lambda)$ |
| 7 | p-distance | $d = n_d / n$ | $n_d$ = differing sites, $n$ = aligned sites |
| 8 | JC69 distance | $d_{JC} = -\frac{3}{4} \ln\left(1 - \frac{4}{3}p\right)$ | Valid for $p < 0.75$ |
| 9 | K2P distance | $d_{K2P} = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(1-2P-Q) - \frac{1}{4}\ln(1-2Q)$ | $P$=transitions, $Q$=transversions |
| 10 | Time-resolved Q-matrix | $P(t) = e^{Qt}$ | Matrix exponential |
| 11 | Molecular clock | $d = 2rt$ | $r$ = rate, $t$ = divergence time |
| 12 | Viterbi termination | $P^* = \max_i \delta_T(i)$ | Log-probability of best path |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Biomedical Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** HMMs power the **UCSC Genome Browser gene predictions** and the **BLAST family of sequence search tools (via profile HMMs in HMMER)**. Phylogenetic models are deployed in **Nextstrain** (real-time SARS-CoV-2 lineage tracking), **COG database** (ortholog clustering), and **BEAST software** (Bayesian evolutionary analysis for HIV and influenza surveillance). Both tools are *first-line* in modern genomics pipelines, drug discovery, and pandemic preparedness.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Viterbi Recurrence

We want to derive the recurrence relation for $\delta_t(j)$, the probability of the most likely path ending in state $S_j$ at time $t$, having generated $O_1, O_2, \dots, O_t$.

**Step 1 — Definition.** Let $Q_{1:t} = (q_1, q_2, \dots, q_t)$ be a path. Then:
$$\delta_t(j) = \max_{Q_{1:t-1}} P\left(Q_{1:t-1}, q_t = S_j, O_1 O_2 \dots O_t \mid \lambda\right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Rule and the Markov assumption** to factorize:
$$P(Q_{1:t-1}, q_t = S_j, O_{1:t}) = P(Q_{1:t-1}, O_{1:t-1}) \cdot P(q_t = S_j \mid q_{t-1}) \cdot P(O_t \mid q_t = S_j)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the three multiplicative factors:**
- $P(Q_{1:t-1}, O_{1:t-1}) = \delta_{t-1}(i)$ when $q_{t-1} = S_i$ and the path is best
- $P(q_t = S_j \mid q_{t-1} = S_i) = a_{ij}$
- $P(O_t \mid q_t = S_j) = b_j(O_t)$

**Step 4 — Substitute into $\delta_t(j)$ and optimize** over the choice of $i$:
$$\delta_t(j) = \max_{i} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(O_t)$$

**Step 5 — Track the optimal predecessor** for backtracking:
$$\psi_t(j) = \arg\max_{i} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right]$$

**Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Full Derivation of the JC69 Distance Formula

We have a sequence pair of length $n$ nucleotides with $n_d$ observed differences. Let $p = n_d / n$. We want the *true* evolutionary distance $d$ such that the *expected* fraction of differences equals $p$, correcting for multiple hits.

**Step 1 — Define the substitution probability.** Under JC69, the probability that two nucleotides are *different* after divergence time $t$ (i.e., at least one substitution occurred) is:
$$P_{\text{different}}(t) = 1 - P_{ii}(t) = 1 - \frac{1}{4}\left(1 + 3e^{-4\mu t}\right) = \frac{3}{4}\left(1 - e^{-4\mu t}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the branch length.** By convention, the JC69 distance is $d = 2\mu t$ (since the two sequences diverged from a common ancestor, total path length is $2 \times \mu t$). Let $\lambda = 4\mu t = 2d$, so:
$$p = \frac{3}{4}(1 - e^{-2d})$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d$:**
$$\frac{4p}{3} = 1 - e^{-2d}$$
$$e^{-2d} = 1 - \frac{4p}{3}$$
$$-2d = \ln\left(1 - \frac{4p}{3}\right)$$
$$d = -\frac{1}{2}\ln\left(1 - \frac{4p}{3}\right) = -\frac{3}{4}\ln\left(1 - \frac{4p}{3}\right)$$

**Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Constraint:** This formula requires $p < 0.75$. When $p \ge 0.75$, the sequences are *saturated* and the distance becomes undefined (infinite) — the model cannot distinguish further divergence.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Viterbi on a Toy HMM

**Setup.** Consider a 2-state HMM for a DNA segment: states are **Exon (E)** and **Intron (I)**. Observations are **Purine (R)** or **Pyrimidine (Y)**.

**Given Parameters:**

$$\pi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} b_E(R)=0.8, \, b_E(Y)=0.2 \\ b_I(R)=0.3, \, b_I(Y)=0.7 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Observation sequence:** $O = R, Y, R$ (length $T = 3$).

**Step 1 — Initialization (t = 1, observe R):**
$$\delta_1(E) = \pi_E \cdot b_E(R) = 0.5 \times 0.8 = 0.40, \quad \psi_1(E) = 0$$
$$\delta_1(I) = \pi_I \cdot b_I(R) = 0.5 \times 0.3 = 0.15, \quad \psi_1(I) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Recursion at t = 2 (observe Y):**
$$\delta_2(E) = \max[\delta_1(E) \cdot a_{EE}, \, \delta_1(I) \cdot a_{IE}] \cdot b_E(Y)$$
$$= \max[0.40 \times 0.7, \, 0.15 \times 0.4] \times 0.2 = \max[0.28, 0.06] \times 0.2 = 0.28 \times 0.2 = 0.056$$
$$\psi_2(E) = E$$
$$\delta_2(I) = \max[\delta_1(E) \cdot a_{EI}, \, \delta_1(I) \cdot a_{II}] \cdot b_I(Y)$$
$$= \max[0.40 \times 0.3, \, 0.15 \times 0.6] \times 0.7 = \max[0.12, 0.09] \times 0.7 = 0.12 \times 0.7 = 0.084$$
$$\psi_2(I) = E$$

**Step 3 — Recursion at t = 3 (observe R):**
$$\delta_3(E) = \max[\delta_2(E) \cdot a_{EE}, \, \delta_2(I) \cdot a_{IE}] \cdot b_E(R)$$
$$= \max[0.056 \times 0.7, \, 0.084 \times 0.4] \times 0.8 = \max[0.0392, 0.0336] \times 0.8 = 0.0392 \times 0.8 = 0.03136$$
$$\psi_3(E) = E$$
$$\delta_3(I) = \max[\delta_2(E) \cdot a_{EI}, \, \delta_2(I) \cdot a_{II}] \cdot b_I(R)$$
$$= \max[0.056 \times 0.3, \, 0.084 \times 0.6] \times 0.3 = \max[0.0168, 0.0504] \times 0.3 = 0.0504 \times 0.3 = 0.01512$$
$$\psi_3(I) = I$$

**Step 4 — Termination:**
$$P^* = \max[\delta_3(E), \delta_3(I)] = \max[0.03136, 0.01512] = 0.03136$$
$$q_3^* = E$$

**Step 5 — Backtrack:** $\psi_3(E) = E \Rightarrow q_2^* = E$, $\psi_2(E) = E \Rightarrow q_1^* = E$.

**Final best path:** $Q^* = E, E, E$ (the entire observed sequence is most likely from an **Exon**).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HMM vs Phylogenetic Models

| Aspect | Hidden Markov Model | Phylogenetic Model |
|---|---|---|
| Primary unknown | Hidden state sequence | Tree topology, branch lengths, ancestral sequences |
| Observations | DNA/Protein sequence | Aligned columns of MSA |
| Core parameter matrix | $A$ (transitions) | $Q$ (substitution rates) |
| Time structure | Discrete, linear (left-to-right) | Continuous, tree-branching |
| Main algorithm | Viterbi, Forward, Baum–Welch | Maximum Likelihood, Neighbor-Joining, Bayesian MCMC |
| Real-world tool | HMMER, GENSCAN | PhyML, RAxML, BEAST, MEGA |
| Output | Annotated sequence | Phylogenetic tree |
| Assumption challenged | Output independence | Site independence (i.i.d. sites) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HMM Architecture (Markov Chain of Hidden States with Emission Probabilities)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Start((Start)) --> S1[State S1]
    S1 -- a11 --> S1
    S1 -- a12 --> S2
    S2 -- a21 --> S1
    S2 -- a22 --> S2
    S2 -- a23 --> S3
    S3 -- a32 --> S2
    S3 -- a33 --> S3
    S3 --> EndNode((End))

    S1 -. b1 k .-> O1[O1 v k]
    S2 -. b2 k .-> O2[O2 v k]
    S3 -. b3 k .-> O3[O3 v k]
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

> **Interpretation:** Solid arrows represent state transitions governed by matrix $A$; dashed arrows represent emissions governed by matrix $B$. The Markov chain on top is *hidden*, the observation sequence $O_1 O_2 O_3$ below is what we actually see.

---

## 4.2 Profile HMM Block Architecture (used in protein family modelling)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph MATCH_STATES
        M1[Match M1]
        M2[Match M2]
        M3[Match M3]
    end
    subgraph INSERT_STATES
        I1[Insert I1]
        I2[Insert I2]
    end
    subgraph DELETE_STATES
        D1[Delete D1]
        D2[Delete D2]
    end
    BEGIN((BEGIN)) --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> ENDNode((END))
    M1 --> I1
    I1 --> I1
    I1 --> M2
    M2 --> I2
    I2 --> I2
    I2 --> M3
    M1 --> D1
    D1 --> M2
    M2 --> D2
    D2 --> M3
```

> **Interpretation:** A profile HMM has three types of states — **Match (M)**, **Insert (I)**, **Delete (D)** — which together model a multiple sequence alignment as a probabilistic model. Used in Pfam and HMMER for sensitive homology detection.

---

## 4.3 Phylogenetic Tree with Branch Lengths and Substitution Model

```mermaid
graph TB
    ROOT((Root A)) -- t1 --> N1[Node X]
    N1 -- t2 --> N2[Node Y]
    N2 -- t3 --> L1((Leaf 1 G))
    N2 -- t4 --> L2((Leaf 2 T))
    N1 -- t5 --> N3[Node Z]
    N3 -- t6 --> L3((Leaf 3 A))
    N3 -- t7 --> L4((Leaf 4 C))

    style ROOT fill:#f9d77e
    style N1 fill:#a4c4ff
    style N2 fill:#a4c4ff
    style N3 fill:#a4c4ff
    style L1 fill:#caffbf
    style L2 fill:#caffbf
    style L3 fill:#caffbf
    style L4 fill:#caffbf
```

> **Interpretation:** Each branch has a length $t_i$ measured in *expected substitutions per site* under a chosen substitution model (JC69, K2P, GTR, etc.). The root represents the ancestral sequence; leaves represent extant (modern) sequences.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Workflow of Phylogenetic Analysis

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Step 1: Sequence Input FASTA] --> B[Step 2: Multiple Sequence Alignment]
    B --> C[Step 3: Choose Substitution Model Q matrix]
    C --> D[Step 4: Distance Computation p vs corrected d]
    D --> E[Step 5A: Distance Based NJ or UPGMA]
    D --> F[Step 5B: Character Based ML or Parsimony]
    D --> G[Step 5C: Bayesian MCMC]
    E --> H[Step 6: Tree Evaluation Bootstrap]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Step 7: Consensus Phylogenetic Tree]
```

> **Interpretation:** This is the standard KTU-style workflow diagram showing how a set of biological sequences is transformed into a phylogenetic tree. The substitution model $Q$ is the *engine*; tree-building method is the *chassis*; bootstrap is the *stress test*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Hidden Markov Model. List the five parameters used to define an HMM.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** is a doubly stochastic process in which a Markov chain with unobserved (hidden) states generates an observable output sequence, where each observation is a probabilistic function of the underlying hidden state.

The five parameters are:
1. **$S$** — set of $N$ hidden states: $S = \{S_1, S_2, \dots, S_N\}$
2. **$V$** — set of $M$ observation symbols (alphabet)
3. **$A = \{a_{ij}\}$** — state transition probability matrix
4. **$B = \{b_j(k)\}$** — observation emission probability matrix
5. **$\pi = \{\pi_i\}$** — initial state distribution

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a phylogenetic substitution model? Explain the Jukes–Cantor (JC69) model in brief.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **phylogenetic substitution model** is a probabilistic framework that quantifies the rate and pattern of nucleotide or amino acid replacements along the branches of an evolutionary tree. It is defined by a **rate matrix $Q$** and is used to convert observed sequence differences into corrected evolutionary distances.

The **Jukes–Cantor (JC69)** model assumes:
- All four nucleotides (A, T, G, C) occur in equal frequency $\pi = 0.25$
- All 12 possible nucleotide substitutions occur at the **same rate $\mu$**
- The off-diagonal entries of $Q$ are all $\mu$ and the diagonal entries are $-3\mu$.

The corrected distance formula is:
$$d_{JC} = -\frac{3}{4}\ln\left(1 - \frac{4}{3}p\right)$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Hidden Markov Model. List and briefly describe the three fundamental problems of HMMs. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **Viterbi algorithm** for the decoding problem. Apply it to find the most likely state path for a simple 2-state HMM with the following data:
- $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$, $\pi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$
- Observation: $O = v_1, v_2, v_1$ (length 3). **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Architecture:** A HMM has two layers — a hidden Markov chain (states $q_1, q_2, \dots, q_T$) and a parallel observation sequence ($O_1, O_2, \dots, O_T$). Transitions between hidden states are governed by $A$, and emissions to observations are governed by $B$. The initial state is drawn from $\pi$. (See Section 4.1 diagram for reference.)

**Three Fundamental Problems:**

| # | Problem | Given | Find | Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Evaluation | $\lambda$, $O$ | $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | Forward |
| 2 | Decoding | $\lambda$, $O$ | Best state sequence $Q^*$ | **Viterbi** |
| 3 | Learning | $O$ | $\lambda^*$ that maximizes $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | Baum–Welch |

#### Model Solution (b):

**Derivation:** As derived in Section 3.1 above, the Viterbi recurrence is:
$$\delta_t(j) = \max_i \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(O_t)$$

**Numerical application:**

**t = 1 (observe $v_1$):**
$$\delta_1(1) = 0.5 \times 0.9 = 0.45, \quad \delta_1(2) = 0.5 \times 0.2 = 0.10$$
$$\psi_1(1) = 0, \quad \psi_1(2) = 0$$

**t = 2 (observe $v_2$):**
$$\delta_2(1) = \max[0.45 \times 0.6, \, 0.10 \times 0.3] \times 0.1 = \max[0.27, 0.03] \times 0.1 = 0.027$$
$$\psi_2(1) = 1$$
$$\delta_2(2) = \max[0.45 \times 0.4, \, 0.10 \times 0.7] \times 0.8 = \max[0.18, 0.07] \times 0.8 = 0.144$$
$$\psi_2(2) = 1$$

**t = 3 (observe $v_1$):**
$$\delta_3(1) = \max[0.027 \times 0.6, \, 0.144 \times 0.3] \times 0.9 = \max[0.0162, 0.0432] \times 0.9 = 0.0389$$
$$\psi_3(1) = 2$$
$$\delta_3(2) = \max[0.027 \times 0.4, \, 0.144 \times 0.7] \times 0.2 = \max[0.0108, 0.1008] \times 0.2 = 0.0202$$
$$\psi_3(2) = 2$$

**Termination:**
$$P^* = \max[0.0389, 0.0202] = 0.0389, \quad q_3^* = 1$$

**Backtrack:** $\psi_3(1) = 2 \Rightarrow q_2^* = 2$, $\psi_2(2) = 1 \Rightarrow q_1^* = 1$.

**Final answer:** $Q^* = S_1, S_2, S_1$ with path probability $P^* = 0.0389$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Define a phylogenetic model. What are the essential elements of any phylogenetic model? With a suitable diagram, explain the difference between **distance-based** and **character-based** methods of tree building. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Two DNA sequences are 100 nucleotides long and differ at 12 sites, of which 8 are transitions and 4 are transversions. Compute:
- (i) The raw p-distance
- (ii) The Jukes–Cantor corrected distance
- (iii) The Kimura 2-parameter corrected distance **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Definition:** A phylogenetic model describes how biological sequences evolve over time by specifying substitution rates between characters (nucleotides/amino acids), branch lengths, and tree topology.

**Essential Elements:**
1. **Tree topology** $\tau$ — branching pattern
2. **Branch lengths** $t$ — evolutionary distance per branch
3. **Substitution matrix** $Q$ — rate of change between characters
4. **Base/amino acid frequencies** $\pi_i$ — equilibrium composition
5. **Site heterogeneity model** — Gamma distribution, invariant sites
6. **Multiple sequence alignment** $\mathcal{D}$ — input data

**Distance-based vs Character-based (using block diagram from Section 4.4):**
- **Distance-based** (NJ, UPGMA): converts alignment → pairwise distance matrix → tree. Fast but loses information.
- **Character-based** (Maximum Parsimony, Maximum Likelihood, Bayesian): uses each column of alignment individually. Slower but more accurate.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:** $n = 100$, $n_d = 12$, transitions $P = 8/100 = 0.08$, transversions $Q = 4/100 = 0.04$.

**(i) p-distance:**
$$p = \frac{n_d}{n} = \frac{12}{100} = 0.12$$

**(ii) JC69 corrected distance:**
Check validity: $4p/3 = 4 \times 0.12 / 3 = 0.16 < 1$ ✓
$$d_{JC} = -\frac{3}{4}\ln\left(1 - \frac{4}{3}(0.12)\right) = -\frac{3}{4}\ln(1 - 0.16) = -\frac{3}{4}\ln(0.84)$$
$$= -\frac{3}{4} \times (-0.17435) = 0.1308 \text{ substitutions/site}$$

**(iii) K2P corrected distance:**
Check validity: $1 - 2P - Q = 1 - 0.16 - 0.04 = 0.80 > 0$ ✓ and $1 - 2Q = 1 - 0.08 = 0.92 > 0$ ✓
$$d_{K2P} = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(1 - 2(0.08) - 0.04) - \frac{1}{4}\ln(1 - 2(0.04))$$
$$= -\frac{1}{2}\ln(0.80) - \frac{1}{4}\ln(0.92)$$
$$= -\frac{1}{2}(-0.2231) - \frac{1}{4}(-0.0834) = 0.1116 + 0.0208 = 0.1324 \text{ substitutions/site}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Distance Formulae:** Students frequently confuse the **JC69 formula** (uses $p$ only) with the **K2P formula** (uses $P$ and $Q$ separately). Loss of **1 mark** is common for: (a) forgetting to apply natural logarithm, (b) writing K2P formula as $-\frac{1}{2}\ln(1-2P-Q)$ *without* the second $-\frac{1}{4}\ln(1-2Q)$ term, (c) not verifying the validity constraint $p < 0.75$ before applying JC69. Also, do not confuse $p$-distance with evolutionary distance $d$ — $p$ underestimates $d$ for divergent sequences.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — HMM Questions:** Three high-frequency mark-loss areas in HMM problems:
> 1. **Mixing up Forward vs Viterbi:** Forward uses $\sum$ (sum over all paths); Viterbi uses $\max$ (best path). Conflating these loses **3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the emission term:** In the Viterbi/Forward recurrence, students often write only the transition $a_{ij}$ and forget to multiply by the emission probability $b_j(O_t)$. This loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping backtracking:** Stating the termination value $P^*$ without reconstructing the actual state path $Q^* = q_1^*, q_2^*, \dots, q_T^*$ using the $\psi$ pointers loses **2 marks** because the question usually asks for the "most likely path," not just its probability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):**

- [x] An HMM is fully specified by **5 parameters**: $S, V, A, B, \pi$.
- [x] The **two assumptions** of an HMM are: (1) Markov property, (2) Output independence.
- [x] The **three HMM problems** map to: **Forward** (Evaluation), **Viterbi** (Decoding), **Baum–Welch** (Learning).
- [x] **Viterbi uses $\max$, Forward uses $\sum$** — this is the single most-tested distinction.
- [x] Always perform Viterbi in **log-space** for long biological sequences to avoid underflow.
- [x] A **phylogenetic model** = Tree topology + Branch lengths + Substitution rate matrix $Q$.
- [x] **JC69**: all substitutions equal rate; $\pi = 0.25$; distance $d_{JC} = -\frac{3}{4}\ln(1 - \frac{4}{3}p)$.
- [x] **K2P**: distinguishes transitions ($\alpha$) from transversions ($\beta$); requires separate $P$ and $Q$ counts.
- [x] Raw $p$-distance **underestimates** true evolutionary distance for divergent sequences.
- [x] JC69 is invalid when $p \ge 0.75$ (sequence saturation).
- [x] **Distance-based** methods (NJ, UPGMA) are fast but lossy; **Character-based** methods (ML, Parsimony) are slow but accurate.
- [x] Bootstrap resampling is used to assess the **statistical confidence** of tree branches.
- [x] $\gamma_t(j)$ and $\xi_t(i,j)$ are the **posterior probabilities** used in Baum–Welch updates.
- [x] The **transition matrix $A$ row-sums** and **emission matrix $B$ row-sums** must each equal **1** (stochastic property).
- [x] HMMs power gene prediction; Phylogenetic models power evolutionary tree building — both are first-line tools in modern genomics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **phylogenetic bioinformatics**, *determining the substitution model* refers to the formal statistical procedure of selecting the most appropriate **nucleotide** or **amino acid substitution model** that best describes the evolutionary patterns observed in a given multiple sequence alignment (MSA), before the inference of a phylogenetic tree. The selection is performed by comparing candidate substitution models (which differ in parameters such as base frequencies, transition/transversion ratio, rate heterogeneity, and invariant sites) using **statistical criteria** such as the **Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT)**, the **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)**, the **Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC)**, and **Decision Theory (DT)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Context)**
> The *substitution model* is a probabilistic description of the rate at which one character (nucleotide or amino acid) is replaced by another character over evolutionary time along a phylogenetic tree. The *correct* substitution model is the one that best balances **goodness-of-fit** with **parsimony of parameters** to avoid over-parameterization.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **forensic linguist** trying to determine how an ancient manuscript was copied over centuries. You observe that certain letters (e.g., 'b' and 'd') get confused more often than others (e.g., 'b' and 'q'). The "**substitution model**" is essentially the rulebook that tells you *how often each letter gets mistaken for each other letter*. Some rulebooks are simple (e.g., "all letters are equally likely to be confused"), and some are very detailed (e.g., "vowels get confused twice as often, and confusion rates depend on their position in the word").

**Determining the substitution model** is like picking the rulebook that *most accurately describes the mistakes* in the manuscript you have in hand, without making the rulebook so complex that it describes noise rather than real patterns.

| Real-world Analogy | Bioinformatics Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Rulebook describing letter confusions | Nucleotide/amino acid substitution model |
| Different rulebooks (simple vs. complex) | JC69, K2P, HKY85, GTR … |
| Picking the best rulebook | Model selection (LRT, AIC, BIC) |
| A manuscript with many copies | Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the *correct* model important?**
> Using a *too-simple* model ignores real biological signal (e.g., transition bias). Using a *too-complex* model inflates variance by estimating too many parameters from limited data. Both lead to **systematically wrong trees** and incorrect branch-length estimates.

## 1.3 Substitution Models in the KTU 2024 Curriculum

The standard hierarchy taught in KTU Module 2 is:

1. **Jukes–Cantor (JC69 / JC)**: All substitutions equally likely, equal base frequencies.
2. **Felsenstein 81 (F81)**: Unequal base frequencies, equal substitution rates.
3. **K2P / Kimura 2-Parameter**: Distinguishes transitions from transversions, equal base frequencies.
4. **HKY85**: Distinguishes transitions from transversions, **unequal** base frequencies.
5. **SYM (Symmetric)**: All substitution rates different, equal base frequencies.
6. **GTR (General Time Reversible)**: All substitution rates different, **unequal** base frequencies — the most general reversible model.

> [!TIP]
> **GTR is the "umbrella" model** from which all the others are obtained by *constraining* parameters. This hierarchical nesting is what allows the **Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT)** to compare models.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Nesting of Models

The power of model selection in phylogenetics lies in the **mathematical hierarchy** of substitution models. Every model is a **restricted (constrained) version** of the more general model above it.

| Model | Free Parameters (nucleotide) | Base Frequencies | Substitution Rates | Rate Variation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **JC69** | 0 | All equal (= 0.25) | All equal (1) | None |
| **F81** | 3 | Free (π_A, π_C, π_G, π_T) | All equal | None |
| **K2P** | 1 | All equal | κ (ts/tv ratio) | None |
| **HKY85** | 4 | Free | κ (ts/tv ratio) | None |
| **SYM** | 5 | All equal | 6 distinct rates | None |
| **GTR** | 8 | Free | 6 distinct rates | None |
| **GTR+Γ+I** | 10 | Free | 6 distinct rates | Γ-shape (α), pinv |

> [!NOTE]
> **Parameters counted for nucleotide models:**
> - Base frequencies: 3 free (the 4th is constrained since they sum to 1).
> - Substitution rates: 6 free in GTR (constrained to sum to 1; the diagonal is fixed).
> - Plus 1 for κ (transition/transversion), and 1 each for the Gamma shape (α) and proportion of invariant sites (pinv).

## 2.2 The Workflow of Determining the Substitution Model

The KTU 2024 expected workflow (often implemented in tools like **jModelTest**, **ModelTest-NG**, **IQ-TREE**, and **PAUP\***) follows these steps:

1. **Input**: A multiple sequence alignment (MSA) and a candidate tree topology (or a set of topologies).
2. **Candidate model set**: Define the list of substitution models to be tested.
3. **Maximum Likelihood (ML) optimization**: For each candidate model, find the parameters (branch lengths, base frequencies, rate matrix entries) that **maximize the likelihood** of the data.
4. **Statistical comparison**:
   - **LRT** (for nested models only)
   - **AIC / BIC** (for nested and non-nested models)
   - **DT** (Decision Theory, using expected information loss)
5. **Selection**: Choose the model with the lowest AIC / BIC score, or the simplest model not rejected by LRT.
6. **Output**: The best-fit model and its estimated parameters.

## 2.3 The Maximum Likelihood (ML) Framework

The likelihood of observing the data $D$ (the alignment columns) given a model $M$ with parameters $\theta$ and a tree $T$ is:

$$
L(\theta, T \mid D) = P(D \mid \theta, T)
$$

Because alignment columns are assumed to be **independently evolved**, the total likelihood is the **product** over all $N$ sites:

$$
L = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(D_i \mid \theta, T)
$$

Computationally, we work with the **log-likelihood**:

$$
\ln L = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ln P(D_i \mid \theta, T)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The substitution model $M$ is what defines the **transition probability matrix** $P(t) = e^{Qt}$, where $Q$ is the instantaneous rate matrix and $t$ is branch length. Different models differ in how $Q$ is parameterized.

## 2.4 The Instantaneous Rate Matrix Q

For a nucleotide model, the general form of $Q$ is:

$$
Q = \begin{pmatrix}
- & \mu \pi_C & \mu \pi_G & \mu \pi_T \\
\mu \pi_A & - & \mu \pi_G & \mu \pi_T \\
\mu \pi_A & \mu \pi_C & - & \mu \pi_T \\
\mu \pi_A & \mu \pi_C & \mu \pi_G & -
\end{pmatrix}
$$

In **GTR**, the rates between the six ordered pairs $\{A \leftrightarrow C, A \leftrightarrow G, A \leftrightarrow T, C \leftrightarrow G, C \leftrightarrow T, G \leftrightarrow T\}$ are **distinct**: $a, b, c, d, e, f$. The off-diagonal entry $Q_{ij}$ for $i \neq j$ is $r_{ij} \pi_j$, and the diagonal is set to make each row sum to zero (so that the matrix is normalized).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table verbatim. It appears in **every KTU ESE question on model selection**.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Site likelihood (continuous-time Markov) | $P(t) = e^{Qt}$ | $Q$ is rate matrix, $t$ = branch length |
| 2 | Total log-likelihood | $\ln L = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ln P(D_i \mid \theta, T)$ | $N$ = alignment length |
| 3 | LRT statistic | $\delta = 2(\ln L_1 - \ln L_0)$ | $L_1$ = complex, $L_0$ = simple |
| 4 | LRT null distribution | $\delta \sim \chi^2_{df}$ | $df$ = difference in #parameters |
| 5 | AIC | $AIC = -2 \ln L + 2K$ | $K$ = #free parameters |
| 6 | Corrected AIC (small sample) | $AIC_c = AIC + \dfrac{2K(K+1)}{N-K-1}$ | Use when $N/K < 40$ |
| 7 | BIC | $BIC = -2 \ln L + K \ln N$ | $N$ = #sites, penalizes complexity more |
| 8 | DT (Decision Theory) | Minimize expected information loss | Equivalent to AIC in asymptote |
| 9 | Gamma rate heterogeneity | Site rates $r \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha, \alpha)$ | Mean rate = 1 |
| 10 | Pinv (invariant sites) | $P(\text{invariant}) = p_{inv}$ | Combined as $GTR + \Gamma + I$ |
| 11 | JC69 constraint | All $\pi_i = 0.25$, all $r_{ij} = 1$ | Simplest model |
| 12 | K2P constraint | All $\pi_i = 0.25$, $\kappa = ts/tv$ | One extra free parameter |
| 13 | HKY85 | Free $\pi_i$, $\kappa = ts/tv$ | 4 free parameters |
| 14 | GTR | Free $\pi_i$, 6 free $r_{ij}$ | 8 free parameters, most general reversible |
| 15 | Selection rule (AIC) | Pick model with minimum AIC | Lower is better |
| 16 | Selection rule (BIC) | Pick model with minimum BIC | Tends to choose simpler models |
| 17 | Δ-AIC (relative support) | $\Delta_i = AIC_i - AIC_{min}$ | $\Delta_i \le 2$ = substantial support |
| 18 | Akaike weight | $w_i = \dfrac{e^{-\Delta_i/2}}{\sum_j e^{-\Delta_j/2}}$ | Normalized model probabilities |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Board Exam:**
> "**AIC** asks: *How much information is lost?* (lower is better)
> **BIC** asks: *Is the extra complexity worth it given the data size?*
> **LRT** asks: *Is the more complex model significantly better?* (significance via χ²)."

## 2.6 Engineering / Real-world Utility

Although this topic appears in **bioinformatics**, the underlying mathematics (continuous-time Markov chains, AIC, BIC, LRT) is a **core statistical learning technique** used in:

- **Signal processing**: Hidden Markov Models for speech recognition.
- **Telecommunications**: Channel coding and error correction.
- **Civil/Structural engineering**: Model selection in finite-element fitting.
- **Financial engineering**: Selecting volatility models in time-series.
- **Machine learning**: Hyperparameter tuning and model selection (Lasso vs. Ridge).

Mastering this topic therefore provides a **transferable statistical skill set** that extends well beyond biology.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Substitution Rate Matrix Q to Transition Probability P(t)

This is the most important derivation for KTU Module 2, and it appears in Part B questions regularly.

### Step 1: Define the continuous-time Markov process

We assume that the evolution of a single nucleotide along a branch of length $t$ follows a **homogeneous, time-reversible, continuous-time Markov chain**. Let $P_{ij}(t)$ denote the probability of being in state $j$ at time $t$ given the chain was in state $i$ at time $0$.

### Step 2: Chapman–Kolmogorov equation

For any two times $s$ and $t$:

$$
P_{ij}(s+t) = \sum_{k} P_{ik}(s) P_{kj}(t)
$$

### Step 3: Differentiate and take the limit

Differentiating the Chapman–Kolmogorov equation with respect to $s$ and setting $s \to 0$:

$$
\frac{d P_{ij}(t)}{d t} = \sum_{k} Q_{ik} P_{kj}(t)
$$

In matrix form:

$$
\frac{d P(t)}{d t} = Q \cdot P(t)
$$

with the boundary condition $P(0) = I$ (identity matrix).

### Step 4: Solve the matrix ODE

This is a standard linear ODE whose solution is the **matrix exponential**:

$$
P(t) = e^{Q t}
$$

### Step 5: Evaluate $P(t)$ for the Jukes–Cantor model

For **JC69**, the rate matrix is:

$$
Q_{JC} = \mu \begin{pmatrix}
-1 & 1/3 & 1/3 & 1/3 \\
1/3 & -1 & 1/3 & 1/3 \\
1/3 & 1/3 & -1 & 1/3 \\
1/3 & 1/3 & 1/3 & -1
\end{pmatrix}
$$

Diagonalize $Q_{JC} = U \Lambda U^{-1}$, where the eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = 0$ and $\lambda_{2,3,4} = -\dfrac{4\mu}{3}$. Then:

$$
P_{JC}(t) = e^{Q_{JC} t} = U \, e^{\Lambda t} \, U^{-1}
$$

The resulting closed-form element (for $i \neq j$) is:

$$
P_{ij}(t) = \frac{1}{4} \left(1 - e^{-\frac{4\mu t}{3}}\right)
$$

And the diagonal element is:

$$
P_{ii}(t) = \frac{1}{4} \left(1 + 3 e^{-\frac{4\mu t}{3}}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The quantity $\mu t$ is interpreted as the **expected number of substitutions per site**. Setting $\mu = 1$ gives branch length in *substitutions per site* — the standard unit in modern phylogenetics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT) Statistic

This derivation is a 14-mark KTU favourite.

### Step 1: Two nested hypotheses

Let $H_0$ (null) and $H_1$ (alternative) be **nested** models: $H_0$ is a constrained special case of $H_1$. The null hypothesis is obtained by setting some parameters of $H_1$ to fixed values.

### Step 2: Maximum likelihood estimates

Let $\hat{\theta}_0$ and $\hat{\theta}_1$ be the **maximum likelihood estimates** (MLEs) of the parameters under $H_0$ and $H_1$, respectively. Let $L_0 = L(\hat{\theta}_0 \mid D)$ and $L_1 = L(\hat{\theta}_1 \mid D)$.

### Step 3: Form the likelihood ratio

By the nesting property, $L_0 \le L_1$ always. The likelihood ratio is:

$$
\Lambda = \frac{L_0}{L_1} \le 1
$$

### Step 4: Wilks' theorem (1938)

Wilks' theorem states that, under $H_0$ and certain regularity conditions, the statistic

$$
\delta = -2 \ln \Lambda = 2 (\ln L_1 - \ln L_0)
$$

converges in distribution to a **chi-square distribution** with $df = K_1 - K_0$ degrees of freedom, where $K_i$ is the number of free parameters in model $H_i$:

$$
\delta \sim \chi^2_{df}
$$

### Step 5: Hypothesis decision

- Compute $\delta_{obs}$ from the data.
- Compare with the critical value $\chi^2_{df, \alpha}$ at significance level $\alpha$ (usually 0.05).
- If $\delta_{obs} > \chi^2_{df, 0.05}$, **reject $H_0$** (the more complex model is justified).
- If $\delta_{obs} \le \chi^2_{df, 0.05}$, **fail to reject $H_0$** (use the simpler model — parsimony wins).

### Step 6: Worked numerical example

Suppose we compare **JC69** ($K_0 = 0$) versus **HKY85** ($K_1 = 4$) on an alignment of $N = 500$ sites.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Log-likelihood of JC69 on the data: } \ln L_0 &= -1842.30 \\
\text{Log-likelihood of HKY85 on the data: } \ln L_1 &= -1810.55 \\
\text{Degrees of freedom: } df &= K_1 - K_0 = 4 - 0 = 4 \\
\text{LRT statistic: } \delta_{obs} &= 2 \times (-1810.55 - (-1842.30)) \\
&= 2 \times 31.75 \\
&= 63.50
\end{aligned}
$$

Critical value from the chi-square table:

$$
\chi^2_{4, 0.05} = 9.488
$$

Decision: $63.50 \gg 9.488 \Rightarrow$ **Reject JC69** in favour of HKY85.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: AIC and BIC (Information-Theoretic Criteria)

### Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)

AIC is derived from **Kullback–Leibler divergence**. The expected information lost when model $M$ is used to approximate the true data-generating process is approximately:

$$
AIC = -2 \ln L + 2K
$$

The factor $2K$ is an **asymptotic bias correction** for the maximum likelihood estimator of the KL divergence.

### Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC)

BIC is derived from a **Bayesian** argument and approximates the marginal likelihood. The penalty term is $\dfrac{K \ln N}{2}$ on each side:

$$
BIC = -2 \ln L + K \ln N
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **BIC penalty vs. AIC penalty:**
> - For $N > 7$, $\ln N > 2$, so **BIC penalizes complexity more** than AIC.
> - For very large $N$, BIC tends to select the *true* model (consistency).
> - AIC tends to select the model that *minimizes prediction error* (efficiency).

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Example: Picking a Model with AIC

Given four candidate models and their $\ln L$ values for the same data ($N = 600$ sites):

| Model | $K$ | $\ln L$ | $AIC = -2 \ln L + 2K$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| JC69 | 0 | $-1820.4$ | $3640.8$ |
| F81 | 3 | $-1803.1$ | $3612.2$ |
| HKY85 | 4 | $-1798.7$ | $3605.4$ |
| GTR+Γ | 9 | $-1782.2$ | $3582.4$ |

**Step 1**: Identify the minimum AIC: $AIC_{min} = 3582.4$ (GTR+Γ).

**Step 2**: Compute $\Delta_i = AIC_i - AIC_{min}$:

| Model | $\Delta_i$ |
|---|---|
| JC69 | $58.4$ |
| F81 | $29.8$ |
| HKY85 | $23.0$ |
| GTR+Γ | $0$ |

**Step 3**: Akaike weights $w_i = e^{-\Delta_i / 2} / \sum_j e^{-\Delta_j / 2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{GTR+\Gamma} &= \frac{1}{1 + e^{-11.5} + e^{-14.9} + e^{-29.2}} \approx 1.000 \\
w_{HKY85} &\approx 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \\
w_{F81} &\approx 6.7 \times 10^{-7} \\
w_{JC69} &\approx 4.7 \times 10^{-13}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision**: GTR+Γ has virtually all the Akaike weight. **Select GTR+Γ.**

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Hierarchy of Nucleotide Substitution Models

The following **Mermaid block diagram** represents the nested hierarchy of common substitution models, with arrows indicating "is a constrained special case of".

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeGTR["GTR most general 6 rates free base frequencies"]
    nodeSYM["SYM 6 rates equal base frequencies"]
    nodeHKY["HKY85 kappa plus free base frequencies"]
    nodeK2P["K2P kappa plus equal base frequencies"]
    nodeF81["F81 free base frequencies equal rates"]
    nodeJC["JC69 simplest equal rates and frequencies"]

    nodeGTR --> nodeSYM
    nodeGTR --> nodeHKY
    nodeSYM --> nodeK2P
    nodeHKY --> nodeF81
    nodeK2P --> nodeJC
    nodeF81 --> nodeJC

    nodeGTR --> nodeGTRG["GTR plus Gamma rate heterogeneity"]
    nodeGTRG --> nodeGTRGI["GTR plus Gamma plus Invariant sites"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each downward arrow represents a **constraining** relationship. The model at the tail has *more* free parameters than the model at the head. The more general model is the parent; the simpler model is the child. This is the **mathematical nesting** that allows LRT to be used.

## 4.2 The Model-Selection Workflow (Block Diagram)

The following diagram captures the **complete decision flow** used by tools such as *jModelTest 2*, *ModelTest-NG*, and *IQ-TREE* ModelFinder.

```mermaid
    stepF{"Nested comparison"}
    stepG["Apply LRT chi square test"]
    stepH{"Non nested or model ranking"}

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC

```

A block-level view of the *internal components* of a typical phylogenetic model-selection engine (e.g., jModelTest):
