# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define the Jukes–Cantor (JC69) substitution model. State two of its main limitations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Jukes–Cantor (JC69) model**, proposed by Jukes and Cantor in 1969, is the simplest nucleotide substitution model. It assumes that:

1. All four nucleotides (A, C, G, T) occur with **equal frequency** ($\pi_A = \pi_C = \pi_G = \pi_T = 0.25$).
2. All substitutions between any two different nucleotides occur at the **same rate** $\mu$.

The off-diagonal elements of the rate matrix are all equal to $\dfrac{1}{3} \mu$ and the diagonal elements are $-\mu$.

**Limitations:**
- It does **not distinguish** between transitions and transversions, despite the fact that transitions are biologically more frequent.
- It assumes **equal base composition** across sequences, which is unrealistic for most real genomic data, especially organellar genomes and coding regions.
- It assumes **uniform rate** across sites, ignoring rate heterogeneity, which is widespread in real sequence data (some sites evolve much faster than others).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's note:** A complete answer must list the two constraints (equal frequencies, equal rates) and at least one biological limitation for full marks.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is meant by the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)? Mention its formula and the rule for model selection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)** is an information-theoretic measure used for **model selection** in phylogenetics (and in general statistics). It estimates the *expected Kullback–Leibler divergence* between the candidate model and the true data-generating process.

The formula is:

$$
AIC = -2 \ln L + 2K
$$

where $\ln L$ is the **maximum log-likelihood** of the data under the candidate model and $K$ is the **number of free parameters** estimated in the model.

**Rule for model selection:** Among a set of candidate models fitted to the same data, the model with the **lowest AIC value** is selected as the best model. The $2K$ term penalizes model complexity, thereby preventing overfitting.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: (a) Describe the hierarchical structure of common nucleotide substitution models (JC69, F81, K2P, HKY85, SYM, GTR). Draw a tree showing the nested relationships. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT) for comparing nested substitution models. Apply it to compare JC69 vs. HKY85 on a sample dataset where $\ln L_{JC} = -1842.30$ and $\ln L_{HKY} = -1810.55$ on an alignment of 500 sites at $\alpha = 0.05$. State the conclusion. (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Hierarchical Structure (7 marks):**

The nucleotide substitution models form a **nested hierarchy** in which more complex models are obtained from simpler ones by relaxing constraints. The hierarchy is:

- **JC69 (Jukes–Cantor, 1969)**: 0 free parameters. All base frequencies equal (0.25) and all substitution rates equal.
- **F81 (Felsenstein, 1981)**: 3 free parameters. **Unequal base frequencies** but equal substitution rates.
- **K2P (Kimura 2-Parameter, 1980)**: 1 free parameter ($\kappa$, the transition/transversion ratio). Equal base frequencies, but transitions and transversions are distinguished.
- **HKY85 (Hasegawa–Kishino–Yano, 1985)**: 4 free parameters ($\kappa$ + 3 free base frequencies). Both unequal base frequencies *and* distinct transition/transversion rates.
- **SYM (Symmetric model)**: 5 free parameters (6 distinct substitution rates). Equal base frequencies but all 6 substitution rates distinct.
- **GTR (General Time Reversible, Tavaré 1986)**: 8 free parameters (6 distinct rates + 3 free base frequencies). The **most general reversible** nucleotide model.

The nested tree (drawn in SECTION 4.1) shows the parent–child relationships: GTR → {SYM, HKY85}; HKY85 → F81; SYM → K2P; {F81, K2P} → JC69.

**Part (b) — LRT Derivation and Application (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Hypotheses**
$H_0$: JC69 is the true model (4 constrained parameters).
$H_1$: HKY85 is the true model (no constraint on κ and base frequencies).

**Step 2: Number of free parameters**
$K_0 = 0$ (JC69) and $K_1 = 4$ (HKY85), so $df = K_1 - K_0 = 4$.

**Step 3: Compute the LRT statistic**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{obs} &= 2 (\ln L_1 - \ln L_0) \\
&= 2 \times [(-1810.55) - (-1842.30)] \\
&= 2 \times 31.75 \\
&= 63.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Critical value**
From the chi-square table, $\chi^2_{4, 0.05} = 9.488$.

**Step 5: Decision**

$$
\delta_{obs} = 63.50 \gg 9.488 = \chi^2_{4, 0.05}
$$

Therefore, we **reject the null hypothesis $H_0$ (JC69)** and conclude that the more complex **HKY85 model** is significantly justified by the data.

**Conclusion:** HKY85 is preferred over JC69 for this alignment.

---

### **Question B1 — Choice B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) Explain the AIC and BIC criteria for substitution model selection. State the formulas and the rule for choosing the best model. Discuss when each is preferred. (7 marks)**

**(b) The following log-likelihoods are obtained for an alignment of 600 sites. Determine the best model using AIC and BIC. (7 marks)**

| Model | $K$ | $\ln L$ |
|---|---|---|
| JC69 | 0 | $-1820.4$ |
| F81 | 3 | $-1803.1$ |
| HKY85 | 4 | $-1798.7$ |
| GTR+Γ | 9 | $-1782.2$ |

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — AIC and BIC (7 marks):**

**AIC (Akaike Information Criterion)** estimates the *information loss* when the candidate model is used to approximate reality:

$$
AIC = -2 \ln L + 2K
$$

The penalty $2K$ discourages over-parameterization. The model with the **minimum AIC** is selected.

**BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion)** approximates the marginal likelihood and adds a stronger penalty for complexity, weighted by the log of sample size $N$:

$$
BIC = -2 \ln L + K \ln N
$$

The model with the **minimum BIC** is selected.

**Comparison:**
- For $N > 7$, $\ln N > 2$, so BIC penalizes more strongly than AIC.
- **BIC** is *consistent* — it selects the true model as $N \to \infty$ if the true model is in the candidate set.
- **AIC** is *efficient* — it minimizes prediction error, even if it overshoots by selecting a more complex model.
- In phylogenetics with **modest alignments** (hundreds to thousands of sites), AIC and BIC often agree. With **large alignments** (genome-scale), BIC tends to pick simpler models.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

For $N = 600$ sites, $\ln N = \ln 600 = 6.397$.

**AIC calculations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AIC_{JC69} &= -2(-1820.4) + 2(0) = 3640.8 \\
AIC_{F81} &= -2(-1803.1) + 2(3) = 3612.2 \\
AIC_{HKY85} &= -2(-1798.7) + 2(4) = 3605.4 \\
AIC_{GTR+\Gamma} &= -2(-1782.2) + 2(9) = 3582.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**BIC calculations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BIC_{JC69} &= -2(-1820.4) + 0 \cdot 6.397 = 3640.8 \\
BIC_{F81} &= -2(-1803.1) + 3 \cdot 6.397 = 3625.5 \\
BIC_{HKY85} &= -2(-1798.7) + 4 \cdot 6.397 = 3622.0 \\
BIC_{GTR+\Gamma} &= -2(-1782.2) + 9 \cdot 6.397 = 3622.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Recheck:** $9 \times 6.397 = 57.573$, so $BIC_{GTR+\Gamma} = 3564.4 + 57.573 = 3621.97 \approx 3622.0$.

**Result:** GTR+Γ has the **lowest AIC (3582.4)**, and HKY85 is essentially tied with GTR+Γ in BIC (~3622.0). The 2-point difference is negligible.

**Best-fit model by AIC:** **GTR+Γ** (decisive margin of 23 over the next best).
**Best-fit model by BIC:** **HKY85 or GTR+Γ** (tie within 2 units).

**Practical conclusion:** With $N=600$, the data is moderate, so **AIC is more reliable** here. We select **GTR+Γ** as the best substitution model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing LRT and AIC:** LRT can only be used on **nested** models. Do not apply LRT to compare JC69 with SYM (not nested in the same way). Use AIC/BIC for non-nested comparisons.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in AIC:** The penalty is $2K$, not $K$. Many students write $AIC = -\ln L + K$ — this is wrong and costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Degrees of freedom in LRT:** $df$ is the **difference** in free parameters, not the total of the larger model. $df = K_1 - K_0$.
> 4. **Misnaming SYM as GTR:** SYM has equal base frequencies; GTR has free base frequencies. They are *not* the same model.
> 5. **Conflating AIC and BIC interpretations:** AIC is about *prediction*; BIC is about *truth*. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist (read this the night before the exam):**

- ☐ The **substitution model** is the probabilistic rulebook for character change along a phylogenetic tree.
- ☐ Models differ in **base frequencies** ($\pi_i$) and **substitution rates** ($r_{ij}$).
- ☐ The hierarchy: **JC69 → F81 → HKY85 → GTR** (each level adds parameters).
- ☐ **K2P** is on a *separate branch* from F81 (it adds κ, not unequal π).
- ☐ **SYM** is a 5-parameter intermediate; **GTR** is the most general reversible model (8 parameters).
- ☐ Adding **+Γ** (Gamma-distributed rates across sites) and **+I** (proportion of invariant sites) further generalizes any base model.
- ☐ **LRT** uses the statistic $\delta = 2(\ln L_1 - \ln L_0) \sim \chi^2_{df}$.
- ☐ **AIC** = $-2 \ln L + 2K$; **BIC** = $-2 \ln L + K \ln N$.
- ☐ Lower **AIC** / **BIC** is better; use **Akaike weights** to quantify support.
- ☐ For non-nested models, use **AIC / BIC / DT** (Decision Theory), never LRT.
- ☐ The transition probability matrix is $P(t) = e^{Qt}$.
- ☐ For JC69, $P_{ij}(t) = \dfrac{1}{4}\left(1 - e^{-\frac{4\mu t}{3}}\right)$.
- ☐ Tools: **jModelTest 2**, **ModelTest-NG**, **IQ-TREE ModelFinder**, **PAUP\***, **PhyML**.
- ☐ The "correct" model is the one that **balances goodness-of-fit with parsimony of parameters**.
- ☐ An over-parameterized model inflates variance; an under-parameterized model inflates bias.
- ☐ Always state the model alongside any phylogenetic tree in publications — it is a **required methodological detail** in the KTU 2024 lab record and project viva.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluating Phylogenetic Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT604 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evaluating Phylogenetic Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Phylogenetic tree evaluation** is the systematic, statistical, and computational process of assessing the **reliability**, **accuracy**, and **robustness** of an inferred phylogenetic tree against the underlying biological data and evolutionary model. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PBBBT604 – Bioinformatics), tree evaluation encompasses three major pillars:

1. **Branch Support Assessment** – quantifying the statistical confidence placed on each internal branch (clade) of the tree using methods such as **non-parametric bootstrapping**, **posterior probabilities (Bayesian inference)**, and the **approximate likelihood ratio test (aLRT)**.
2. **Topological Congruence Testing** – comparing alternative tree topologies using distance metrics like the **Robinson–Foulds (RF) distance** and statistical tests such as the **Shimodaira–Hasegawa (SH) test** and the **Approximately Unbiased (AU) test**.
3. **Model Adequacy & Information-Theoretic Criteria** – evaluating whether the substitution model used to infer the tree is appropriate for the data, typically using the **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)** or the **Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Evaluation is a mandatory step that separates a *plausible* tree from a *biologically defensible* tree. Inference produces a tree; evaluation certifies it.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have asked 1,000 independent judges (each analyzing a different re-sampled survey of voters) to rank 8 political parties. The *best ranking* is the one that the *maximum number of judges agree upon*, even if no single judge saw the original survey. 

A **phylogenetic tree** is the "best ranking of evolutionary relationships." **Evaluation** is asking:

- *"How many times does this branch show up if we re-shuffle the data columns?"* → **Bootstrap**
- *"Which of two competing trees explains the data with a higher probability?"* → **Likelihood-based tests**
- *"Is my distance/likelihood model the simplest one that fits?"* → **AIC / BIC**

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Tree evaluation is the "quality-control" checkpoint. Inference gives you a hypothesis; evaluation tells you whether the hypothesis is sturdy enough to publish.

## 1.3 Why Evaluation is Non-Negotiable

A phylogenetic tree reconstructed by **Neighbor-Joining**, **Maximum Parsimony**, **Maximum Likelihood**, or **Bayesian Inference** is always a *point estimate* under a chosen model and alignment. Small changes in:

- alignment columns,
- substitution model parameters (e.g., transition/transversion ratio $\kappa$),
- taxon sampling,

can produce **drastically different topologies**. Hence, evaluation is mandatory before drawing biological conclusions.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Evaluation

| Metric | Purpose | Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bootstrap Proportion (BP)** | Branch support | **0 – 100 %** |
| **Posterior Probability (PP)** | Bayesian branch support | **0 – 1.0** |
| **Robinson–Foulds distance** | Tree–tree dissimilarity | **0 – $2(n-3)$** for binary trees |
| **AIC / BIC** | Model selection | Lower = better |
| **Likelihood score ($\ln L$)** | Model fit to data | Higher = better |
| **aLRT / SH-aLRT** | Branch support (likelihood-based) | **0 – 1.0** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A bootstrap value $\geq 70\%$ is conventionally interpreted as a *well-supported* clade. Values below 50 % usually indicate the branch is no better than random.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Tree Evaluation – Operational Logic

### Pillar I – Branch Support (How confident are we in each split?)

#### A. Non-Parametric Bootstrap (Felsenstein, 1985)

The bootstrap procedure generates a *pseudo-replicate* alignment by **sampling columns with replacement** from the original alignment, then re-infers the tree, and records how frequently each clade appears across replicates.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. Start with an alignment of $N$ sites (columns) and $S$ taxa.
2. For $b = 1, 2, \ldots, B$ (typically $B = 500$ to $1000$ for KTU/standard publications):
    - Draw a new alignment $D^{*(b)}$ of $N$ columns by sampling *with replacement* from the original $N$ columns.
    - Re-infer the tree $T^{*(b)}$ using the same inference method.
3. Map each $T^{*(b)}$ onto the reference clades of the original tree $T$.
4. The **bootstrap proportion** for a clade $C$ is:
$$BP(C) = \frac{\#\{b : C \in T^{*(b)}\}}{B}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The bootstrap proportion is **not** a strict confidence probability in the frequentist sense. It is a measure of the **signal-to-noise robustness** of the data. High BP means the clade is *reproducible* under column re-sampling.

#### B. Posterior Probability (Bayesian Inference – MrBayes / BEAST)

In Bayesian MCMC, the posterior probability of a clade $C$ is its marginal frequency in the converged posterior sample:

$$PP(C) = \frac{\#\{\text{post-burn-in samples containing } C\}}{\#\{\text{total post-burn-in samples}\}}$$

PP and BP often disagree: PP is generally **higher** than BP for the same data, because the Bayesian model explicitly averages over parameter space, while the bootstrap only averages over data resamples.

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical rule:** A PP of $\geq 0.95$ is considered strong support, but this is *not* equivalent to BP $\geq 95\%$. The two metrics are calibrated differently.

#### C. Approximate Likelihood Ratio Test (aLRT / SH-aLRT)

Implemented in **PhyML**, this approach computes, for each branch, the difference in log-likelihood between the ML tree and the best tree *constrained* to *not* contain that branch:

$$\text{SH-aLRT} \approx 1 - P\left[\chi^{2}_{1} \geq 2 \Delta \ln L\right]$$

It is fast, deterministic, and avoids the computational cost of bootstrapping.

---

### Pillar II – Topological Congruence (How different are two candidate trees?)

#### A. Robinson–Foulds (RF) Distance

For two unrooted binary trees $T_1$ and $T_2$ on the same leaf set:

$$RF(T_1, T_2) = \frac{\vert B(T_1) \triangle B(T_2) \vert}{2}$$

where $B(T)$ is the set of bipartitions (splits) induced by the internal branches of $T$, and $\triangle$ denotes the symmetric difference. For $n$ taxa, the **maximum possible RF distance** is $2(n-3)$.

- $RF = 0$: trees are identical.
- $RF = 2(n-3)$: trees are maximally different (the "caterpillar" vs. the "balanced" tree).

> [!TIP]
> **Normalization:** the *normalized* RF distance is:
> $$RF_{\text{norm}}(T_1, T_2) = \frac{RF(T_1, T_2)}{2(n-3)}$$
> which lies in $[0, 1]$, useful for comparing tree pairs with different leaf counts.

#### B. Shimodaira–Hasegawa (SH) Test

The SH test compares the log-likelihoods of $k$ candidate trees *on the same data*, accounting for the selection bias that occurs when the ML tree is one of the candidates:

$$p_i^{SH} = 1 - P\left[\max_{j}\left(\Delta_{ij}\right) \leq \Delta_{i,\text{obs}} \mid H_0\right]$$

A clade is rejected if its SH $p$-value is below the chosen significance level (typically $\alpha = 0.05$).

#### C. Approximately Unbiased (AU) Test

A multiscale bootstrap improvement of the SH test, less conservative and more powerful. Implemented in **CONSEL**.

---

### Pillar III – Model Selection (Is the model we used appropriate?)

#### A. Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)

For a model with $K$ free parameters fitted to data with maximized log-likelihood $\ln L$:

$$AIC = -2 \ln L + 2K$$

#### B. Corrected AIC (AICc) for small samples

$$AICc = AIC + \frac{2K(K+1)}{n - K - 1}$$

where $n$ is the number of observations (sites). Use when $n / K < 40$.

#### C. Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC)

$$BIC = -2 \ln L + K \ln n$$

BIC penalizes complexity more heavily than AIC and is **consistent** (selects the true model as $n \to \infty$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb:** Models with $\Delta AIC < 2$ are essentially equivalent; $4 < \Delta AIC < 7$ is moderate; $\Delta AIC > 10$ is decisively worse.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $BP(C) = \frac{N_C}{B}$ | Bootstrap proportion of clade $C$ | $[0,1]$ or $[0,100\%]$ |
| $PP(C) = \frac{M_C}{M}$ | Posterior probability from MCMC | $[0,1]$ |
| $RF(T_1, T_2) = \frac{\vert B_1 \triangle B_2 \vert}{2}$ | Robinson–Foulds distance | $[0, 2(n-3)]$ |
| $RF_{\text{norm}} = \frac{RF}{2(n-3)}$ | Normalized RF | $[0,1]$ |
| $AIC = -2 \ln L + 2K$ | Akaike Information Criterion | Lower is better |
| $AICc = AIC + \frac{2K(K+1)}{n-K-1}$ | Corrected AIC for small $n$ | Lower is better |
| $BIC = -2 \ln L + K \ln n$ | Bayesian Information Criterion | Lower is better |
| $\Delta AIC_i = AIC_i - AIC_{\min}$ | Relative AIC | $\Delta AIC < 2$ : equivalent |
| $w_i = \frac{\exp(-\Delta AIC_i / 2)}{\sum_r \exp(-\Delta AIC_r / 2)}$ | Akaike weight | $\sum_i w_i = 1$ |
| $\text{aLRT} \approx 1 - P[\chi^{2}_{1} \geq 2 \Delta \ln L]$ | Branch support (PhyML) | $[0,1]$ |
| $N_e \cdot t$ | Product of effective pop. size & divergence time (coalescent) | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on notation:** The vertical bar $\vert$ in the symmetric-difference notation is rendered as $\triangle$ here to comply with the markdown table constraint; the same set operation is mathematically standard.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Molecular epidemiology:** Bootstrap-supported clades of viral genomes (SARS-CoV-2 lineages) inform transmission tracking.
- **Conservation biology:** Robust phylogenies identify evolutionarily distinct lineages that warrant protection (Evolutionary Distinctiveness – ED score).
- **Drug design:** Phylogenetic evaluation confirms orthology/paralogy relationships critical for selecting model organisms.
- **Forensic bioinformatics:** Evaluating the strength of evolutionary signals in ancient DNA (aDNA) alignments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 – Bootstrap Proportion for a 4-Taxon Tree

**Setup.** Suppose we have an alignment of 4 taxa (A, B, C, D) with $N = 6$ sites. We run $B = 5$ bootstrap replicates, each time re-sampling 6 columns with replacement. The reference ML tree $T$ groups $\{A,B\}$ and $\{C,D\}$.

**Step 1 – Original alignment (hypothetical):**

Sites: $s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5, s_6$.

**Step 2 – Generate replicate $D^{*(1)}$:**

Pick 6 sites with replacement. Suppose the draw is: $s_3, s_1, s_3, s_5, s_6, s_2$.

**Step 3 – Re-infer tree from $D^{*(1)}$:**

Suppose the resulting tree also groups $\{A,B\}$ and $\{C,D\}$. Record: clade $\{A,B\}$ gets a "+1".

**Step 4 – Repeat for $b = 2, 3, 4, 5$:**

Suppose the counts across 5 replicates for the clade $\{A,B\}$ are: 4 out of 5 trees contain it.

**Step 5 – Compute BP:**

$$BP(\{A,B\}) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80 = 80\%$$

Since $BP \geq 70\%$, the clade $\{A,B\}$ is considered **well supported**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Remark:** The number of replicates $B$ influences the *precision* of the BP estimate. The standard error of BP is approximately:
> $$SE(BP) \approx \sqrt{\frac{BP \cdot (1-BP)}{B}}$$
> For $B = 500, BP = 0.70$, we get $SE \approx 0.020$, i.e. a 95 % CI of roughly $[0.66, 0.74]$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 – Robinson–Foulds Distance Between Two Trees

**Setup.** Consider 4 taxa $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and two unrooted binary trees:

- $T_1$: $((1,2), (3,4))$ — the "balanced" topology.
- $T_2$: $((1,3), (2,4))$ — an alternative topology.

**Step 1 – Extract bipartitions from $T_1$:**

Each internal branch of an unrooted binary tree on $n$ taxa defines a bipartition. $T_1$ has 2 internal branches, giving:

$$B(T_1) = \{\{1,2\} \mid \{3,4\},\; \{1,2,3,4\} \mid \emptyset\}$$

The second is the trivial "trifurcation-into-all" split induced by removing any pendant edge; we typically ignore trivial bipartitions. So the non-trivial set is:

$$B_{\text{nt}}(T_1) = \{\{1,2\} \mid \{3,4\}\}$$

**Step 2 – Extract bipartitions from $T_2$:**

$$B_{\text{nt}}(T_2) = \{\{1,3\} \mid \{2,4\}\}$$

**Step 3 – Symmetric difference:**

$$B_{\text{nt}}(T_1) \triangle B_{\text{nt}}(T_2) = \{\{1,2\} \mid \{3,4\},\; \{1,3\} \mid \{2,4\}\}$$

The cardinality is 2.

**Step 4 – Compute RF:**

$$RF(T_1, T_2) = \frac{2}{2} = 1$$

**Step 5 – Normalize:**

For $n = 4$, the maximum RF is $2(n-3) = 2(1) = 2$. Hence:

$$RF_{\text{norm}}(T_1, T_2) = \frac{1}{2} = 0.50$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** $T_1$ and $T_2$ differ in 50 % of their internal splits; they are *moderately* dissimilar.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 – AIC-based Model Selection

**Setup.** We fit 3 substitution models to the same alignment of 600 sites:

- Model M1 (JC69): $K_1 = 0$ free parameters beyond branch lengths (since JC69 is parameter-free; let $K_1 = 1$ for branch length parameter); $\ln L_1 = -1230.5$.
- Model M2 (K2P): $K_2 = 2$ (transition rate $\alpha$, transversion rate $\beta$); $\ln L_2 = -1198.7$.
- Model M3 (GTR + $\Gamma$): $K_3 = 9$; $\ln L_3 = -1180.1$.

**Step 1 – Compute AIC for each model:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AIC_{M1} &= -2(-1230.5) + 2(1) = 2461.0 + 2 = 2463.0 \\
AIC_{M2} &= -2(-1198.7) + 2(2) = 2397.4 + 4 = 2401.4 \\
AIC_{M3} &= -2(-1180.1) + 2(9) = 2360.2 + 18 = 2378.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Identify the minimum-AIC model:**

$AIC_{\min} = AIC_{M3} = 2378.2$.

**Step 3 – Compute $\Delta AIC$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta AIC_{M1} &= 2463.0 - 2378.2 = 84.8 \\
\Delta AIC_{M2} &= 2401.4 - 2378.2 = 23.2 \\
\Delta AIC_{M3} &= 0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Compute Akaike weights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{M1} &= \frac{e^{-84.8/2}}{e^{-84.8/2} + e^{-23.2/2} + e^{0}} = \frac{e^{-42.4}}{e^{-11.6} + 1} \approx 0 \\
w_{M2} &= \frac{e^{-11.6}}{e^{-11.6} + 1} \approx \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0000090} \approx 9.0 \times 10^{-6} \\
w_{M3} &= \frac{1}{1.0000090} \approx 0.99999
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Conclude:**

The **GTR + $\Gamma$** model has essentially all the Akaike weight ($\approx 1.0$). M1 is decisively rejected ($\Delta AIC > 10$); M2 has only marginal weight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Caveat:** $K$ above counts the free substitution parameters; in practice one also includes $K_{\text{branches}} = 2n - 3$ (for $n$ taxa) in $K$ when comparing *trees* under different models. Always keep $K$ definitionally consistent.

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 4 – Standard Error of Bootstrap Proportion

**Setup.** From $B = 1000$ replicates, a clade appears in $k = 740$ of them.

**Step 1 – Compute the observed proportion:**

$$\widehat{BP} = \frac{740}{1000} = 0.74$$

**Step 2 – Compute the standard error (binomial approximation):**

$$SE(\widehat{BP}) = \sqrt{\frac{\widehat{BP}(1 - \widehat{BP})}{B}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.74 \times 0.26}{1000}} = \sqrt{0.0001924} \approx 0.0139$$

**Step 3 – Approximate 95 % CI (normal approximation):**

$$CI_{95\%} \approx 0.74 \pm 1.96 \times 0.0139 = [0.713,\; 0.767]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Alternative:** use the Wilson score interval for better small-sample behavior:
> $$CI_{95\%} = \frac{\widehat{p} + \frac{z^2}{2B} \pm z \sqrt{\frac{\widehat{p}(1-\widehat{p})}{B} + \frac{z^2}{4B^2}}}{1 + \frac{z^2}{B}}$$
> with $z = 1.96$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Bootstrap | Posterior Probability | aLRT | AIC / BIC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Computational cost | High (×B) | Moderate (MCMC) | Low | Low |
| Statistical basis | Resampling | Bayes theorem | Likelihood ratio | Information theory |
| Output range | $[0, 100]\%$ | $[0, 1]$ | $[0, 1]$ | Real number |
| Calibration strictness | Lenient | Often overconfident | Conservative | Model-level, not branch-level |
| Best use case | General ML / MP | Large trees, complex models | Quick branch screening | Choosing substitution model |
| Tool examples | RAxML, IQ-TREE | MrBayes, BEAST | PhyML | jModelTest, ModelTest-NG |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bootstrap Procedure – Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Original Alignment D of N sites and S taxa"] --> B["Infer Reference ML Tree T_ref"]
    B --> C["Initialize bootstrap counter b = 1"]
    C --> D["Resample N sites with replacement to form D_star_b"]
    D --> E["Infer tree T_star_b using same method"]
    E --> F["Map bipartitions of T_star_b onto T_ref clades"]
    F --> G["Increment support counter for matching clades"]
    G --> H{"b less than B ?"}
    H -- Yes --> C
    H -- No --> I["Compute Bootstrap Proportion BP_C for each clade C"]
    I --> J["Annotate T_ref with BP values"]
    J --> K["Output: Evaluated Tree with Branch Supports"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The loop on the right (`H` decision) is the heart of bootstrapping. Every iteration re-samples data and re-runs the entire inference pipeline, which is why bootstrap is computationally expensive — it is, in effect, $B$ independent tree searches.

---

## 4.2 Phylogenetic Tree Evaluation – End-to-End Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        I1["Multiple Sequence Alignment"]
        I2["Candidate Tree T from ML / MP / NJ / Bayesian"]
    end

    subgraph Pillar1["PILLAR I — BRANCH SUPPORT"]
        P1A["Non-parametric Bootstrap"]
        P1B["Posterior Probability via MCMC"]
        P1C["aLRT / SH-aLRT"]
    end

    subgraph Pillar2["PILLAR II — TOPOLOGY COMPARISON"]
        P2A["Robinson-Foulds Distance RF"]
        P2B["SH Test"]
        P2C["AU Test"]
    end

    subgraph Pillar3["PILLAR III — MODEL SELECTION"]
        P3A["AIC"]
        P3B["AICc"]
        P3C["BIC"]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1["Annotated Tree with Support Values"]
        O2["Model Adequacy Report"]
        O3["Topology Test Report"]
    end

    I1 --> P1A
    I1 --> P1B
    I1 --> P1C
    I1 --> P3A
    I1 --> P3B
    I1 --> P3C

    I2 --> P1A
    I2 --> P1B
    I2 --> P1C
    I2 --> P2A
    I2 --> P2B
    I2 --> P2C

    P1A --> O1
    P1B --> O1
    P1C --> O1

    P2A --> O3
    P2B --> O3
    P2C --> O3

    P3A --> O2
    P3B --> O2
    P3C --> O2
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Logic – How to Choose a Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["What is the question?"] --> Q2A["Is this single branch reliable?"]
    Q1 --> Q2B["Are two trees equally good?"]
    Q1 --> Q2C["Is the substitution model right?"]

    Q2A --> A1["Compute Bootstrap on ML tree"]
    Q2A --> A2["Or compute Posterior Probability via MrBayes"]
    Q2A --> A3["Or compute SH-aLRT via PhyML"]

    Q2B --> B1["Run SH test or AU test in IQ-TREE / CONSEL"]
    Q2B --> B2["Or compute RF distance for global topology comparison"]

    Q2C --> C1["Fit candidate models JC69, HKY, GTR, plus Gamma and Pinvar"]
    Q2C --> C2["Compute AIC, AICc, BIC"]
    Q2C --> C3["Select minimum-criterion model"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three branches?** In KTU 2024 exams, examiners often pose questions like *"Justify which evaluation method is appropriate when..."* — this decision tree is the canonical answer skeleton.

---

## 4.4 RF Distance Computation — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["Input: Two trees T1 and T2 on n taxa"] --> R2["Enumerate internal branches of T1 and T2"]
    R2 --> R3["Represent each internal branch as a bipartition split"]
    R3 --> R4["Form sets B_T1 and B_T2"]
    R4 --> R5["Compute symmetric difference B_T1 triangle B_T2"]
    R5 --> R6["Count cardinality and divide by 2 to get RF distance"]
    R6 --> R7["Optional: divide by 2 times n minus 3 for normalized RF"]
    R7 --> R8["Output: RF distance and normalized value"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the bootstrap proportion in phylogenetic tree evaluation, and what is the conventional threshold for declaring a clade as "well supported"?**

**Model Answer:**

The **bootstrap proportion (BP)** is a non-parametric resampling measure of branch support. From an alignment of $N$ sites, $B$ pseudo-replicate alignments are generated by sampling $N$ sites *with replacement*. A tree is inferred from each replicate, and the fraction of replicate trees that contain a given clade $C$ is:

$$BP(C) = \frac{\#\{b : C \in T^{*(b)}\}}{B}$$

A BP of **$\geq 70\%$** is the conventional threshold for declaring a clade well supported; values below $50\%$ indicate the branch is no better than random grouping. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for the formula, 1 for the threshold and interpretation.)

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) and the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). Which is more conservative in penalizing model complexity?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | AIC | BIC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Formula | $-2 \ln L + 2K$ | $-2 \ln L + K \ln n$ |
| Penalty for complexity | $2K$ (linear in $K$) | $K \ln n$ (logarithmic, but larger for typical $n$) |
| Asymptotic property | Efficient (not consistent) | Consistent (selects true model as $n \to \infty$) |
| Use case | Prediction-focused | Truth-focused |

For typical alignments with $n > 7$, $\ln n > 2$, so **BIC imposes a heavier penalty on model complexity** than AIC and is therefore more **conservative**. (3 marks: 1 for AIC formula, 1 for BIC formula, 1 for the comparison/conclusion.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **non-parametric bootstrap procedure** of Felsenstein (1985) for assessing branch support in a maximum likelihood phylogeny. Describe the algorithmic steps clearly and write the formula for computing the bootstrap proportion of a clade.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a dataset of $n = 6$ taxa, two ML trees $T_1$ and $T_2$ are obtained with $\ln L_1 = -2150.3$ and $\ln L_2 = -2156.8$. Using the Shimodaira–Hasegawa (SH) test framework, comment on whether $T_2$ can be rejected. Mention the conceptual basis of the SH test and how it differs from a naive log-likelihood ratio test.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 – Define the inference setting.** We start with a multiple sequence alignment $D$ of $N$ columns and $S$ taxa, and an ML tree $T_{\text{ML}}$ obtained by optimizing $\ln P(D \mid T, \theta)$ over topologies $T$ and parameters $\theta$.

**Step 2 – Initialize.** Set bootstrap replicate counter $b = 1$ and total replicates $B$ (typically 500 – 1000).

**Step 3 – Resample (with replacement).** For each $b$, generate a pseudo-alignment $D^{*(b)}$ of $N$ columns by drawing columns uniformly at random *with replacement* from the original $N$ columns. Some columns appear multiple times; others are omitted.

**Step 4 – Re-infer.** Apply the *same* ML inference procedure to $D^{*(b)}$ to obtain a bootstrap tree $T^{*(b)}$.

**Step 5 – Map clades.** Identify which clades of $T_{\text{ML}}$ are also present in $T^{*(b)}$. Increment a counter for each matching clade.

**Step 6 – Loop.** Repeat steps 3–5 for $b = 1, \ldots, B$.

**Step 7 – Compute BP.** For any clade $C$:

$$BP(C) = \frac{\text{Number of bootstrap trees containing } C}{B}$$

**Step 8 – Interpret.** $BP \geq 70\%$ → well supported; $50\% \leq BP < 70\%$ → weakly supported; $BP < 50\%$ → unresolved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual caveat (often asked in KTU):** Bootstrap is *not* the probability that the clade is true under a frequentist framework. It is the probability that the clade would be recovered under the procedure applied to data drawn from the *best-fit model*.

(Marks distribution: 1 mark each for steps 1–6, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the interpretation.)

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Conceptual basis of SH test:**

The SH test compares the log-likelihoods of $k$ a-priori selected trees on the *same* alignment, while correcting for the bias that arises from the fact that the ML tree is *one of the candidates*. The null hypothesis $H_0$ is: *all $k$ trees fit the data equally well*.

**Step 1 – Compute observed log-likelihoods.**

$\ln L_1 = -2150.3$ and $\ln L_2 = -2156.8$.

**Step 2 – Compute observed differences.**

For each tree $i$, define the site log-likelihood differences $d_{ij}^{(s)} = \ln L_i^{(s)} - \ln L_j^{(s)}$ at site $s$. The observed difference in total log-likelihoods is:

$$\Delta_{12,\text{obs}} = \ln L_1 - \ln L_2 = -2150.3 - (-2156.8) = 6.5$$

**Step 3 – Generate the null distribution via RELL bootstrapping.**

Resample sites with replacement $R$ times (e.g., $R = 10000$). For each resample $r$, recompute $\Delta_{12}^{(r)}$. The SH $p$-value for tree $i$ is:

$$p_i^{SH} = 1 - P\left[\max_{j}\left(\Delta_{ij}^{(r)} \leq \Delta_{i,\text{obs}}^{(r)}\right) \text{ for all } r\right]$$

**Step 4 – Decision.** If $p_2^{SH} < 0.05$, $T_2$ is rejected. With $\Delta_{12} = 6.5$ log-likelihood units, the difference is substantial (often $\Delta \ln L > 2$ is considered meaningful), suggesting $T_2$ would be **rejected** as a significantly worse fit than $T_1$.

**Difference from naive LRT:**

A naive log-likelihood ratio test assumes a *nested* model hierarchy and a $\chi^2$ distribution, which is invalid for comparing two *non-nested* phylogenetic topologies. The SH test uses a resampling-based null distribution that **does not require nesting** and corrects for the ML tree's selection bias.

(Marks distribution: 1 mark conceptual basis, 1 mark formula, 1 mark observed $\Delta$, 1 mark resampling procedure, 1 mark decision rule, 2 marks for the contrast with LRT.)

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the **Robinson–Foulds (RF) distance** between two phylogenetic trees. For two unrooted binary trees $T_1$ and $T_2$ on $n = 5$ taxa, derive the RF distance and the normalized RF distance when the non-trivial bipartition sets are:
$$B_{\text{nt}}(T_1) = \{\{1,2\} \mid \{3,4,5\},\; \{1,2,3\} \mid \{4,5\}\}$$
$$B_{\text{nt}}(T_2) = \{\{1,2\} \mid \{3,4,5\},\; \{1,3\} \mid \{2,4,5\}\}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)** and the **Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC)** for substitution model selection in phylogenetics. For a dataset of $n = 500$ sites, three models yield:

| Model | $K$ | $\ln L$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| JC69 | 1 | -1820.4 |
| HKY + $\Gamma$ | 5 | -1750.2 |
| GTR + $\Gamma$ + I | 11 | -1738.9 |

Compute AIC, AICc, and BIC for each model and identify the best-fitting model.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition of RF distance:**

For two unrooted trees $T_1$ and $T_2$ on the same leaf set, the **Robinson–Foulds distance** is half the cardinality of the symmetric difference of their bipartition sets:

$$RF(T_1, T_2) = \frac{\vert B(T_1) \triangle B(T_2) \vert}{2}$$

**Step 1 – Identify common bipartitions.**

$B_{\text{nt}}(T_1) \cap B_{\text{nt}}(T_2) = \{\{1,2\} \mid \{3,4,5\}\}$.

**Step 2 – Identify the symmetric difference.**

$$B_{\text{nt}}(T_1) \triangle B_{\text{nt}}(T_2) = \{\{1,2,3\} \mid \{4,5\},\; \{1,3\} \mid \{2,4,5\}\}$$

**Step 3 – Compute RF.**

$$RF(T_1, T_2) = \frac{2}{2} = 1$$

**Step 4 – Compute normalized RF.**

For $n = 5$ taxa, $2(n-3) = 4$. Hence:

$$RF_{\text{norm}}(T_1, T_2) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The two trees differ in 25% of their non-trivial splits — moderately similar.

(Marks distribution: 1 mark definition, 2 marks for set operations, 2 marks for the RF computation, 2 marks for normalization.)

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Theoretical discussion:**

The **AIC** estimates the Kullback–Leibler divergence between the fitted model and the true data-generating process. It is computed as $-2 \ln L + 2K$ and is *efficient*: in the asymptotic limit it selects the model that minimizes the expected loss. The **BIC** approximates the marginal likelihood under a flat prior and is *consistent*: with enough data it will select the true model if it is among the candidates. AIC is more lenient with complexity, BIC is more conservative.

**Formulas used:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AIC &= -2 \ln L + 2K \\
AICc &= AIC + \frac{2K(K+1)}{n - K - 1} \\
BIC &= -2 \ln L + K \ln n
\end{aligned}
$$

with $n = 500$.

**Step 1 – Compute AIC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AIC_{\text{JC69}} &= -2(-1820.4) + 2(1) = 3640.8 + 2 = 3642.8 \\
AIC_{\text{HKY}+\Gamma} &= -2(-1750.2) + 2(5) = 3500.4 + 10 = 3510.4 \\
AIC_{\text{GTR}+\Gamma+I} &= -2(-1738.9) + 2(11) = 3477.8 + 22 = 3499.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Compute AICc:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AICc_{\text{JC69}} &= 3642.8 + \frac{2(1)(2)}{500 - 1 - 1} = 3642.8 + \frac{4}{498} \approx 3642.81 \\
AICc_{\text{HKY}+\Gamma} &= 3510.4 + \frac{2(5)(6)}{500 - 5 - 1} = 3510.4 + \frac{60}{494} \approx 3510.52 \\
AICc_{\text{GTR}+\Gamma+I} &= 3499.8 + \frac{2(11)(12)}{500 - 11 - 1} = 3499.8 + \frac{264}{488} \approx 3500.34
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Compute BIC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BIC_{\text{JC69}} &= 3640.8 + 1 \cdot \ln(500) = 3640.8 + 6.215 = 3647.02 \\
BIC_{\text{HKY}+\Gamma} &= 3500.4 + 5 \cdot \ln(500) = 3500.4 + 31.073 = 3531.47 \\
BIC_{\text{GTR}+\Gamma+I} &= 3477.8 + 11 \cdot \ln(500) = 3477.8 + 68.361 = 3546.16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Compare:**

| Model | AIC | AICc | BIC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| JC69 | 3642.8 | 3642.81 | 3647.02 |
| HKY + $\Gamma$ | **3510.4** | **3510.52** | **3531.47** |
| GTR + $\Gamma$ + I | 3499.8 | 3500.34 | 3546.16 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (a key KTU-style trap):** 
> - **AIC / AICc** select the **GTR + $\Gamma$ + I** model (lowest AIC = 3499.8).
> - **BIC** selects the **HKY + $\Gamma$** model (lowest BIC = 3531.47), because BIC's heavier penalty on $K$ disfavors the parameter-rich GTR model.
> 
> This is the classic AIC–BIC disagreement, and it is **a perfectly defensible answer**: report both, justify the chosen model, and note the trade-off between fit and parsimony.

(Marks distribution: 1 mark theory, 1 mark each for AIC, AICc, BIC of the three models = 3 marks, 1 mark for comparison table, 1 mark for final conclusion with interpretation.)

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Hotspots in 'Evaluating Phylogenetic Trees' Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating Bootstrap and Posterior Probability.** Students often claim "BP = 0.95 means 95% confidence the clade is true." Wrong. Both are clade-recovery frequencies under *different* statistical philosophies. Lose 1–2 marks if you do not state this.
> 2. **Using AICc without verifying $n > K$.** If $n \leq K$, AICc is undefined (division by zero or negative denominator). Always state the validity condition.
> 3. **Forgetting to ignore trivial bipartitions in RF.** Including the leaf-to-all split inflates RF by 1. Subtract it.
> 4. **Reporting RF without normalization.** For $n = 50$, an RF of 5 is tiny; for $n = 5$, RF of 5 is maximal. Always normalize: $RF_{\text{norm}} = \frac{RF}{2(n-3)}$.
> 5. **Confusing SH test with the Kishino–Hasegawa (KH) test.** The KH test is more conservative because it does not correct for the selection bias of the ML tree; SH is the corrected version. Examiners reward the distinction.
> 6. **Choosing between AIC and BIC without justification.** Always state your criterion for selection (e.g., "BIC used because the true model is among candidates and we have moderate $n$").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Phylogenetic tree evaluation quantifies the reliability of an inferred tree through branch support, model adequacy, and topology comparison.
- **Bootstrap (Felsenstein, 1985):** Resample columns with replacement; $BP(C) = N_C / B$. Conventional threshold: **$\geq 70\%$** for support.
- **Posterior Probability:** From Bayesian MCMC, $PP(C) = M_C / M$. Threshold: **$\geq 0.95$**. Generally higher than equivalent BP — *not* interchangeable.
- **aLRT / SH-aLRT:** Fast, deterministic branch support from PhyML using a $\chi^2_1$ approximation. Computed from $\Delta \ln L$ between ML tree and best tree constrained to lack the branch.
- **Robinson–Foulds distance:** $RF = \frac{\vert B_1 \triangle B_2 \vert}{2}$; range $[0, 2(n-3)]$. Normalized by $2(n-3)$ to give $[0,1]$.
- **SH test:** Compares log-likelihoods of multiple trees on the same data, correcting for ML selection bias. Uses RELL bootstrapping.
- **AU test:** Multiscale bootstrap improvement of SH; less conservative; implemented in CONSEL.
- **AIC:** $-2 \ln L + 2K$ — efficient, prediction-focused.
- **AICc:** $AIC + \frac{2K(K+1)}{n - K - 1}$ — small-sample correction.
- **BIC:** $-2 \ln L + K \ln n$ — consistent, truth-focused, more conservative.
- **Akaike weights:** $w_i = \frac{\exp(-\Delta AIC_i / 2)}{\sum_r \exp(-\Delta AIC_r / 2)}$; sum to 1; interpreted as the relative probability that model $i$ is the best Kullback–Leibler model.
- **Bootstrap standard error:** $SE(BP) = \sqrt{BP(1-BP)/B}$.
- **Three-pillar framework:** Always assess (i) branch support, (ii) topology congruence, (iii) model adequacy — never just one.
- **Tool checklist for KTU practicals:** RAxML / IQ-TREE (bootstrap), MrBayes / BEAST (posterior probabilities), PhyML (aLRT), jModelTest / ModelTest-NG (AIC/BIC), CONSEL (SH/AU), dendropy / ete3 (RF distance).
- **Exam mantra:** *"Inference gives you a tree; evaluation gives you the right to believe it."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure Prediction and Analysis: Predictive Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT604 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Structure Prediction and Analysis: Predictive Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — STRUCTURE PREDICTION AND ANALYSIS: PREDICTIVE METHODS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Predictive Methods (Definition):**
> *Predictive methods in computational bioinformatics are a class of in-silico algorithms, statistical models, and machine-learning frameworks that infer the three-dimensional conformation, secondary-structural topology, functional motifs, and physico-chemical behaviour of a biological macromolecule (protein, RNA, or DNA) directly from its primary sequence, when no experimental structure (X-ray, NMR, Cryo-EM) is available in the Protein Data Bank (PDB).*

These methods bridge the **"sequence–structure gap"** — i.e., the bottleneck that exists because the number of known sequences (≈ 250+ million in UniProt, 2024) far outstrips the number of experimentally solved structures (≈ 220,000 in PDB).

The KTU 2024 scheme classifies these predictive methods under **Module 3** because they form the analytical core of structural bioinformatics, integrating:
- Information theory (GOR method),
- Empirical propensity statistics (Chou–Fasman),
- Energy minimization (ab initio folding),
- Evolutionary conservation (homology & threading),
- Pattern recognition (machine-learning: SVMs, neural networks, Random Forests).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Blueprint vs. The Building Analogy:**
> Imagine an architect who is given only the *shopping list of materials* (cement, steel, glass, wood — like an amino acid sequence) for a building, but no architectural drawing. The architect must **predict** the building's blueprint (3D structure) using:
> 1. *Material properties* — concrete is rigid, glass is brittle (amino acid propensities).
> 2. *Past experience* — buildings with similar lists of materials follow similar designs (homology).
> 3. *Physical laws* — gravity, wind, structural balance (energy minimization).
> 4. *Recognisable patterns* — windows always appear in certain arrangements (motifs).
>
> **Predictive methods are exactly this — using sequence "hints" to forecast 3D shape.**

### 1.3 The Hierarchy of Structural Prediction (KTU High-Yield Layered View)

The prediction is conventionally performed in a **hierarchical cascade** because each level feeds the next:

| Layer | Level of Structure | Typical Prediction Accuracy (CASP Average) | Representative Methods |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1° → 2° | Secondary structure (α-helix, β-strand, coil) | **~80–88 %** (Q3 score) | Chou–Fasman, GOR, PSIPRED, JPred |
| 2° → 3° | Tertiary fold / topology | **~70–90 %** (GDT_TS, template-dependent) | Homology modelling, Threading |
| 3° → 3° | Ab initio (no template) | **~40–60 %** (small proteins < 120 aa) | Rosetta, QUARK, I-TASSER (hybrid) |
| Specialised | TM helices, signal peptides, disorder | **>90 %** for specific classes | TMHMM, SignalP, IUPred, DISOPRED |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** Examiners frequently ask the student to *contrast the prediction accuracy of secondary vs. tertiary structure prediction*. Memorise the **~80 %** Q3 ceiling for 3-state secondary structure prediction as the canonical answer.

### 1.4 The Three Cardinal Axioms of Predictive Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **Axiom 1 — Anfinsen's Thermodynamic Hypothesis (1972, Nobel Prize 1972):**
> The native structure of a small globular protein is determined by *only* its amino acid sequence, under physiological conditions. This is the philosophical foundation of all predictive methods.
>
> **Axiom 2 — Evolutionary Conservation:**
> Structure is more conserved than sequence. Therefore, a homologous protein of known structure can serve as a template.
>
> **Axiom 3 — Local Sequence → Local Structure:**
> Short windows of residues (typically 13–17 residues) statistically determine local secondary structure, justifying sliding-window algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Predictive Methods

Predictive methods are partitioned into **four master families**, each driven by a different mathematical philosophy:

### Family A — Empirical / Statistical Propensity Methods
Operate by computing the **statistical tendency** of each amino acid to occur in a specific secondary-structure element.

- **Chou–Fasman Algorithm (1974)** — single-residue propensity based on 29 solved proteins.
- **Garnier–Osguthorpe–Robson (GOR I, II, III, IV)** — information-theoretic, window-based.
- **Lim's method** — combines physical and statistical parameters.

### Family B — Homology (Comparative) Modelling
> Builds a 3D model for a *target* sequence using an experimentally solved *template* whose sequence is evolutionarily related (sequence identity ≥ 25–30 %).
>
> **Pipeline:** Template identification (BLAST/HHblits) → Sequence alignment → Model building (Modeller, SWISS-MODEL, AlphaFold2 with MSA) → Loop modelling → Refinement → Validation (Ramachandran, ProCheck).

### Family C — Threading (Fold Recognition)
> Used when no clear sequence homologue exists (the "**twilight zone**" of < 25 % identity).
>
> The target sequence is *threaded* through a library of known folds; a **scoring function** evaluates the fitness of each (sequence, fold) pair. Tools: **THREADER, 3D-PSS, FUGUE, GenTHREADER, MUSTER**.

### Family D — Ab Initio (De Novo / Free Modelling)
> Predicts 3D structure from first principles using physics-based energy functions and conformational search (Monte Carlo, Molecular Dynamics, fragment assembly).
>
> Tools: **Rosetta, CHARMM, AMBER, QUARK, I-TASSER (hybrid)**.

## 2.2 Secondary Structure Prediction — Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 Chou–Fasman Algorithm (Operational Steps)

**Step 1 — Compute Propensities:**
For each amino acid $a$ and structural state $s \in \{\text{Helix (H), Strand (E), Coil (C)}\}$:

$$P(a, s) = \frac{f(a \mid s)}{f(a)}$$

where $f(a \mid s)$ is the frequency of residue $a$ within structural state $s$, and $f(a)$ is the overall frequency of $a$ in the dataset.

**Step 2 — Initiate a Helix / Strand:**
A nucleation region of **4 consecutive residues** with $P(a, H) > 1.0$ initiates helix; **3 consecutive** with $P(a, E) > 1.0$ initiates a strand.

**Step 3 — Extend / Terminate:**
Helix is extended until four consecutive residues with $\langle P(a, H) \rangle < 1.0$ are encountered (the helix *breakers*: Pro, Gly, Ser are common terminators).

**Step 4 — Resolve Conflicts:**
Overlapping predictions are resolved by comparing helix and strand *average propensities* over the disputed region.

### 2.2.2 GOR Method (Information-Theoretic)

Unlike Chou–Fasman (single-residue), GOR uses a **sliding window** of 17 residues and computes the **information content** $I$ contributed by each residue to the structural state:

$$I(S_{j}; a_{j-k}, \ldots, a_{j}, \ldots, a_{j+k}) = \log \frac{P(S_{j} \mid a_{j-k}, \ldots, a_{j+k})}{P(S_{j})}$$

This captures **long-range pairwise correlations** between residue $j$ and its neighbours, raising accuracy from ~50 % (Chou–Fasman) to ~65 % (GOR III) and ~73 % (GOR V with neural networks).

## 2.3 Transmembrane Helix Prediction

Membrane-embedded helices have a characteristic **hydrophobic profile**. Predictive tools use hydrophobicity scales such as:

**Kyte–Doolittle Hydropathy Index (most commonly tested):**

$$H_{index}(j) = \frac{1}{w} \sum_{i=j-w/2}^{j+w/2} \Delta G_{transfer}(a_{i})$$

A window of $w = 19$ residues is slid across the sequence. A region with $H_{index} > 1.6$ for ≥ 20 consecutive residues strongly indicates a transmembrane helix.

> [!NOTE]
> **Positive-inside rule:** Positively charged residues (Lys, Arg) are statistically enriched on the cytoplasmic side, assisting in topology assignment.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Meaning / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Chou–Fasman Propensity | $P(a,s) = f(a \mid s) / f(a)$ | Dimensionless ratio; $P > 1$ favours state $s$ |
| 2 | Helix nucleation rule (CF) | 4 residues with $P(a,H) > 1.0$ | Threshold for helix initiation |
| 3 | Strand nucleation rule (CF) | 3 residues with $P(a,E) > 1.0$ | Threshold for strand initiation |
| 4 | GOR Information Score | $I = \log \dfrac{P(S_{j} \mid a_{j-k \ldots j+k})}{P(S_{j})}$ | Information in bits; window $k = 8$ (17 residues) |
| 5 | Kyte–Doolittle Hydropathy | $H(j) = \dfrac{1}{w} \sum \Delta G_{transfer}(a_{i})$ | Window $w = 19$; threshold $> 1.6$ |
| 6 | Q3 Accuracy Metric | $Q_{3} = \dfrac{\text{Correctly predicted residues}}{\text{Total residues}}$ | Three states: H, E, C; typically $0.80$–$0.88$ |
| 7 | SOV (Segment Overlap) | $SOV = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum \min(\text{ovl}_{i}) \cdot \dfrac{\text{len}(s_{i})}{\text{len}(s_{i}) + \delta}$ | Better than Q3 for segment continuity |
| 8 | Identity Threshold for Homology | $\% \text{ identity} \ge 25 \text{–} 30\,\%$ | Below this → "twilight zone" |
| 9 | RMSD (Validation) | $RMSD = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert \mathbf{r}_{i}^{model} - \mathbf{r}_{i}^{native} \vert^{2}}$ | In Å; < 2 Å = high quality |
| 10 | Ramachandran Allowed Region | $\phi, \psi \in$ favoured core | $> 90\%$ residues in core is target |
| 11 | Boltzmann Energy | $E = -k_{B} T \ln Z$ | Conformational probability $\propto e^{-E/k_{B}T}$ |
| 12 | Amino Acid Identity Matrix | BLOSUM62, PAM250 | Substitution scoring for alignment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall — Use of Pipe Symbols:**
> Never write $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table row (the $\vert$ breaks the table renderer). Always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$.

## 2.5 Engineering & Real-World Utility

Predictive methods have direct applications in:

1. **Drug Discovery (CADD):** In silico screening of ligand-binding pockets predicted by homology.
2. **Vaccine Design:** Predicting B-cell / T-cell epitopes on modelled surface proteins (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 spike during the COVID-19 pandemic).
3. **Enzyme Engineering:** Predicting stability mutants (Rosetta-ddG).
4. **Disease Variant Interpretation:** Predicting whether a point mutation (SNP) disrupts structure (e.g., BRCA1, p53 mutations).
5. **Synthetic Biology:** De novo design of novel protein folds (David Baker's group, I-TASSER, AlphaFold2).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Chou–Fasman Propensity From First Principles

**Premise:** We have a dataset of $N$ solved proteins containing a total of $N_{tot}$ residues. Of these, $N_H$ are in helices, $N_E$ in strands, $N_C$ in coils.

**Step A — Compute frequency of residue $a$ in state $s$:**

$$f(a \mid s) = \frac{n_{a,s}}{N_s}$$

where $n_{a,s}$ is the count of residue $a$ observed in state $s$.

**Step B — Compute the overall frequency of residue $a$ in the dataset:**

$$f(a) = \frac{n_{a,total}}{N_{tot}}$$

**Step C — Define the propensity as the ratio of conditional to marginal frequency:**

$$\boxed{P(a, s) = \frac{f(a \mid s)}{f(a)}}$$

> **Interpretation:** If $P(a, s) > 1$, residue $a$ is *over-represented* in state $s$ relative to its background frequency, hence *favours* state $s$. If $P(a, s) < 1$, it is *under-represented* (i.e., disfavours state $s$).

**Step D — Worked Numerical Example (Standard Textbook Values for Alanine in α-Helix):**

Suppose in a dataset of 1000 residues, alanine appears 80 times in helices ($n_{\text{Ala,H}} = 80$), there are $N_H = 250$ helical residues in total, and total alanine occurrences = 100 in the full dataset of $N_{tot} = 1000$.

$$f(\text{Ala} \mid H) = \frac{80}{250} = 0.32$$

$$f(\text{Ala}) = \frac{100}{1000} = 0.10$$

$$P(\text{Ala}, H) = \frac{0.32}{0.10} = 3.2$$

> **Interpretation:** Alanine is **3.2× more likely** than random to occur in a helix. This makes Ala the *strongest helix former* — a fact students must remember for KTU viva questions.

**Step E — Helix Breakers:**

Proline and Glycine typically yield:

$$P(\text{Pro}, H) \approx 0.39, \quad P(\text{Gly}, H) \approx 0.53$$

Both are far below 1.0, hence they are *helix breakers*. They terminate any nascent helix by disrupting the backbone H-bond pattern (Pro lacks an amide H; Gly is too flexible).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — GOR Information Score (Bayesian Foundation)

The GOR method formulates secondary-structure prediction as a **maximum a posteriori (MAP)** decision:

**Step A — We seek the state $S_{j}$ at position $j$ that maximises the posterior probability:**

$$S_{j}^{*} = \arg\max_{S_{j}} \, P(S_{j} \mid a_{j-k}, \ldots, a_{j}, \ldots, a_{j+k})$$

**Step B — Apply Bayes' theorem (with constant prior so it cancels in the log-ratio):**

$$P(S_{j} \mid \text{neighbours}) = \frac{P(\text{neighbours} \mid S_{j}) \, P(S_{j})}{P(\text{neighbours})}$$

**Step C — Take the log to convert products into sums (information bits):**

$$I(S_{j}; \text{window}) = \log \frac{P(S_{j} \mid \text{window})}{P(S_{j})} = \log \frac{P(\text{window} \mid S_{j})}{P(\text{window})}$$

Expanding the joint into single + pairwise terms:

$$I(S_{j}; \text{window}) = \sum_{m=-k}^{+k} \log \frac{P(a_{j+m} \mid S_{j})}{P(a_{j+m})} + \sum_{m \neq n} \log \frac{P(a_{j+m}, a_{j+n} \mid S_{j})}{P(a_{j+m}) \, P(a_{j+n})}$$

**Step D — Numerical Interpretation:**

If $P(a_{j+m}, a_{j+n} \mid H) > P(a_{j+m}) \, P(a_{j+n})$, then the pair is *cooperatively* helical. The pairwise term is the *information gain* over single-residue Chou–Fasman.

> This is precisely why **GOR IV outperforms Chou–Fasman**: it captures correlated residue effects within the 17-residue window.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Threading Scoring Function

For threading, the energy of placing a target residue $a$ at a folded position with environment $\varepsilon$ is approximated as:

$$E_{threading} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha(a_{i}, \varepsilon_{i}) + \sum_{i < j} \beta(a_{i}, a_{j}, d_{ij})$$

where $\alpha$ is the **singleton term** (residue–environment fitness) and $\beta$ is the **pairwise term** (residue–residue contact, dependent on distance $d_{ij}$).

> This functional form is the same form used in **AlphaFold2's structure module** (Evoformer + IPA), making the lineage of predictive methods transparent.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Q3 Accuracy Metric

$$Q_{3} = \frac{N_{HH} + N_{EE} + N_{CC}}{N_{total}}$$

where $N_{HH}$ is the count of residues correctly predicted helix, etc. Q3 is bounded above by $\sim 88$–$90\,\%$ for any single-sequence method; reaching higher requires **multiple sequence alignments (PSSMs)** or **deep learning**.

## 3.5 Comparative Performance Matrix

| Method | Year | Q3 Accuracy | Sequence Identity Required | Computational Cost |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Chou–Fasman | 1974 | ~50 % | None (single sequence) | Trivial |
| GOR I | 1978 | ~57 % | None | Low |
| GOR III | 1987 | ~65 % | None | Low |
| PHD / NN | 1992 | ~72 % | MSA required | Moderate |
| PSIPRED | 1999 | ~80 % | PSSM | Moderate |
| JPred 4 | 2015 | ~82 % | MSA | Moderate |
| SPOT-1D | 2017 | ~84 % | Deep NN | High |
| AlphaFold2 (3-state via DSSP) | 2021 | ~90 % | MSA + templates | Very High (GPU) |
| OmegaFold | 2022 | ~87 % | MSA-free | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Workflow of Predictive Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Sequence FASTA] --> B{Experimental structure in PDB?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Experimental Structure]
    B -- No --> D[Sequence Database Search BLAST or HHblits]
    D --> E{Homologue Found Identity >= 30 percent?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Homology Modelling Pipeline]
    E -- No Identity 25 to 30 percent --> G[Threading Fold Recognition]
    E -- No Identity less than 25 percent --> H[Ab Initio De Novo Modelling]
    F --> I[Model Refinement and Energy Minimisation]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Validation: Ramachandran, ProCheck, Verify3D]
    J --> K[Final Predicted 3D Structure]
    C --> K
```

## 4.2 Chou–Fasman Algorithm Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Sequence] --> B[Compute Propensity P a s for each residue]
    B --> C[Sliding Window Scan]
    C --> D{4 Consecutive P a H greater than 1.0?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Mark Helix Nucleation]
    D -- No --> F{3 Consecutive P a E greater than 1.0?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Mark Strand Nucleation]
    F -- No --> H[Mark as Coil Candidate]
    E --> I[Extend Helix until Breakers]
    G --> J[Extend Strand until Breakers]
    I --> K[Conflict Resolution Compare Avg Propensity]
    J --> K
    H --> K
    K --> L[Final Secondary Structure: H E C]
```

## 4.3 Homology Modelling Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Target Sequence] --> B[Template Search: BLAST, PSI-BLAST, HHblits]
    B --> C[Pairwise or Multiple Alignment]
    C --> D[Backbone Construction from Template]
    D --> E[Loop Modelling via ab initio or database]
    E --> F[Side Chain Placement: Rotamer Library SCWRL]
    F --> G[Energy Minimisation: GROMACS, AMBER]
    G --> H[Validation Suite: PROCHECK, WHAT IF, Verify3D]
    H --> I[Final Homology Model PDB File]
```

## 4.4 GOR Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sequence Window 17 residues] --> B[Single Residue Log Odds Term]
    A --> C[Pairwise Coupling Term]
    B --> D[Sum Information Content]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Choose State with Maximum Information}
    E -- Helix wins --> F[Assign H]
    E -- Strand wins --> G[Assign E]
    E -- Coil wins --> H[Assign C]
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree: Which Predictive Method To Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Predictive Method] --> B{Is homolog known 30 percent or more?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use AlphaFold2 or SWISS MODEL]
    B -- No --> D{Is fold library available?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Threading: GenTHREADER or I-TASSER]
    D -- No --> F{Sequence length less than 120 aa?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Ab Initio: QUARK or Rosetta]
    F -- No --> H[Use Hybrid: I-TASSER]
    H --> I[Final Model]
    C --> I
    E --> I
    G --> I
```

## 4.6 Functional Block Diagram: Modern AlphaFold2 Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input MSA Multiple Sequence Alignment] --> B[Evoformer Block]
    C[Input Pair Representation] --> B
    B --> D[Structure Module IPA]
    D --> E[3D Atomic Coordinates Output]
    F[Template Information Optional] --> B
```
