## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define molecular docking. List any four commonly used docking software.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Molecular docking is a computational technique that predicts the preferred binding orientation and conformation of a small molecule (ligand) when bound to a macromolecular target (receptor, usually a protein). It estimates the binding affinity through a scoring function and is widely used in structure-based drug design (SBDD) to identify hit compounds from chemical libraries.

**Four docking software (1 mark):**
1. AutoDock / AutoDock Vina
2. Glide (Schrödinger)
3. GOLD (Genetic Optimization for Ligand Docking)
4. FlexX or CDOCKER or DOCK

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Explain Lipinski's Rule of Five with its significance in drug discovery.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Rule (2 marks):** Lipinski's Rule of Five (proposed by Christopher Lipinski, 1997) is a heuristic to evaluate the **drug-likeness** and **oral bioavailability** of a chemical compound. A compound is likely to be orally bioavailable if it satisfies:
- **Molecular weight (MW) ≤ 500 Da**
- **LogP (octanol-water partition coefficient) ≤ 5**
- **Hydrogen bond donors (HBD) ≤ 5**
- **Hydrogen bond acceptors (HBA) ≤ 10**

**Significance (1 mark):** It is used in early-stage drug discovery to filter out compounds with poor pharmacokinetic properties, reducing experimental cost and time. More than two violations are associated with poor oral absorption.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option A)

> **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`
> **(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **structure-based drug design (SBDD)** pipeline. Describe the role of each step. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **Lennard-Jones potential** and show that the equilibrium distance is $r_{min} = 2^{1/6}\sigma$ with minimum energy $-\epsilon$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

The **SBDD pipeline** involves the following sequential steps:

**Step 1: Target Identification and Structure Retrieval (1 mark)**
- Identify a disease-relevant protein (e.g., enzyme, receptor).
- Retrieve the 3D structure from the **Protein Data Bank (PDB)** or predict using **AlphaFold2** if no experimental structure is available.

**Step 2: Structure Preparation (1 mark)**
- Add missing hydrogen atoms.
- Assign proper protonation states (considering **pH 7.4**).
- Remove crystallographic artifacts (e.g., water molecules not in the binding site).
- Assign partial charges and atom types per the chosen **force field** (e.g., AMBER, CHARMM).

**Step 3: Active Site Identification (1 mark)**
- Identify the binding pocket via co-crystallized ligand location or using computational tools like **fpocket**, **SiteMap**, or **CASTp**.

**Step 4: Ligand Library Preparation (1 mark)**
- Build or download a virtual library of small molecules (e.g., from **ZINC**, **PubChem**, **ChEMBL**).
- Generate 3D conformers, optimize geometry, and assign charges.

**Step 5: Molecular Docking (1.5 marks)**
- Use a docking program (AutoDock Vina, Glide) to place each ligand in the binding site.
- Generate multiple poses per ligand and rank by **scoring function**.

**Step 6: Post-Docking Analysis (1 mark)**
- Visually inspect top-ranked poses for chemical feasibility.
- Re-score using more rigorous methods.
- Filter by ADMET properties.

**Step 7: MD Simulation and Free Energy Calculation (0.5 mark)**
- Refine the top complex via MD simulation.
- Compute $\Delta G_{bind}$ using **MM-PBSA** or **MM-GBSA**.

> Refer to **Diagram 4.2** (SBDD Pipeline) for the flowchart.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Derivation of Lennard-Jones Potential (3 marks):**

The Lennard-Jones (LJ) potential describes the **van der Waals interaction** between two non-bonded atoms $i$ and $j$ as the sum of:
- A short-range **repulsion** term (Pauli exclusion) decaying as $r^{-12}$.
- A long-range **attraction** term (London dispersion) decaying as $r^{-6}$.

$$
E_{LJ}(r) = 4\epsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]
$$

Where:
- $r$ = interatomic distance
- $\sigma$ = distance at which $E_{LJ} = 0$ (collision diameter)
- $\epsilon$ = depth of the potential well (interaction strength)

**Differentiation to find minimum (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{dE_{LJ}}{dr} = 4\epsilon \left[ -12 \frac{\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} + 6 \frac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}} \right] = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
12 \frac{\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} = 6 \frac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}} \Rightarrow r^6 = 2\sigma^6
$$

$$
\boxed{r_{min} = 2^{1/6} \sigma \approx 1.122 \sigma}
$$

**Substituting to find minimum energy (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{LJ}(r_{min}) &= 4\epsilon \left[ \left( \frac{1}{2^{1/6}} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{1}{2^{1/6}} \right)^{6} \right] \\
&= 4\epsilon \left[ 2^{-2} - 2^{-1} \right] \\
&= 4\epsilon \left[ \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{2} \right] \\
&= 4\epsilon \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{4} \right) \\
&= -\epsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{LJ}^{min} = -\epsilon}
$$

**Physical interpretation**: The well depth $\epsilon$ quantifies the strength of the attractive van der Waals interaction at the equilibrium distance $r_{min}$.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option B)

> **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`
> **(a)** Describe the steps involved in **molecular dynamics (MD) simulation**. Explain the role of force fields and integration algorithms. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The inhibition constant $K_i$ of a drug candidate is 50 nM at 25 °C. Calculate the standard free energy of binding. If the ligand has 25 heavy atoms, compute its ligand efficiency. Comment on its drug-likeness. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (1 mark):** Molecular dynamics simulation is a computational method that solves **Newton's equations of motion** for every atom in a molecular system over time, generating a trajectory of conformational states at a defined temperature and pressure.

**Steps of MD Simulation (5 marks):**

**Step 1: System Preparation**
- Obtain starting 3D coordinates (from PDB or homology model).
- Solvate in a **water box** (TIP3P, SPC, SPC/E models) with explicit ions (Na⁺, Cl⁻) at physiological concentration (~0.15 M).
- Apply **periodic boundary conditions (PBC)** to mimic infinite system.

**Step 2: Energy Minimization**
- Run steepest descent and conjugate gradient minimization to remove steric clashes and bad contacts.
- Monitor energy convergence.

**Step 3: Equilibration (Thermalization + Pressurization)**
- **NVT** (constant Number, Volume, Temperature) ensemble: heat the system from 0 K to **300 K** using a **thermostat** (Berendsen, Nosé-Hoover, Langevin).
- **NPT** (constant Number, Pressure, Temperature) ensemble: equilibrate pressure to **1 atm** using a **barostat** (Parrinello-Rahman, Berendsen).

**Step 4: Production Run**
- Integrate Newton's equations for the desired simulation time (e.g., **100 ns**).
- Use **Verlet** or **Velocity Verlet** integrator with timestep $\Delta t = 2$ fs.
- Constrain bond lengths involving hydrogens using **SHAKE** or **LINCS** algorithms.
- Save trajectory every 1–10 ps for analysis.

**Step 5: Trajectory Analysis**
- **RMSD**: structural stability.
- **RMSF**: per-residue flexibility.
- **Radius of Gyration ($R_g$)**: compactness.
- **Hydrogen bond analysis**: interaction persistence.
- **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**: major motions.

**Role of Force Fields (0.5 mark):**
Force fields (AMBER, CHARMM, OPLS, GROMOS) provide the **mathematical parameters** ($k_b$, $k_\theta$, $k_\phi$, $\epsilon$, $\sigma$, $q$) required to compute the potential energy of every configuration. Without them, MD cannot proceed.

**Role of Integration Algorithms (0.5 mark):**
Integrators (Verlet, Velocity Verlet, Leapfrog) solve Newton's second law $F = ma$ to update atomic positions over discrete timesteps, ensuring energy conservation and numerical stability.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Identify given values (1 mark)**
- $K_i = 50$ nM $= 50 \times 10^{-9}$ M $= 5.0 \times 10^{-8}$ M
- $T = 25°\text{C} = 298.15$ K
- $R = 1.987 \times 10^{-3}$ kcal/(mol·K)
- $N_{HA} = 25$ heavy atoms

**Step 2: Compute $RT$ (1 mark)**

$$
RT = 1.987 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kcal/(mol·K)} \times 298.15 \text{ K} = 0.5925 \text{ kcal/mol}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $\ln K_i$ (1 mark)**

$$
\ln(5.0 \times 10^{-8}) = \ln(5.0) + \ln(10^{-8}) = 1.6094 - 18.4207 = -16.8113
$$

**Step 4: Compute $\Delta G$ (2 marks)**

$$
\Delta G^\circ = RT \ln K_i = 0.5925 \times (-16.8113) = -9.96 \text{ kcal/mol}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta G^\circ \approx -9.96 \text{ kcal/mol}}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Ligand Efficiency (1 mark)**

$$
LE = \frac{\Delta G}{N_{HA}} = \frac{-9.96}{25} = -0.398 \text{ kcal/mol·atom}
$$

$$
\boxed{LE \approx -0.40 \text{ kcal/mol·atom}}
$$

**Step 6: Drug-likeness comment (1 mark)**

The standard threshold for acceptable ligand efficiency is $LE \leq -0.30$ kcal/mol·atom. Since $-0.40 \leq -0.30$, the compound passes the LE criterion. However, $LE \geq -0.50$ is also desirable (highly efficient). The compound is moderately efficient. Additionally, with $\Delta G = -9.96$ kcal/mol corresponding to $K_i = 50$ nM, the compound demonstrates **nanomolar affinity**, making it a strong drug candidate. Further ADMET profiling and selectivity assays are recommended.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3:**
> 1. **Confusing SBDD with LBDD**: SBDD requires a 3D target structure; LBDD only requires known active ligands. Mixing them up costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $4\epsilon$ prefactor in LJ potential**: The complete form is $4\epsilon[(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^6]$, not just $(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^6$. This is a **favourite 2-mark trap**.
> 3. **Wrong signs in $\Delta G = RT \ln K_i$**: Students often write $\Delta G = -RT \ln K_i$ (which is the binding affinity relation). For the inhibition constant, the correct form depends on convention. Memorize the convention.
> 4. **Neglecting units**: Always specify $\Delta G$ in **kcal/mol**, RMSD in **Å**, $K_i$ in **M (or nM/μM with conversion)**.
> 5. **MD vs. Docking confusion**: Docking gives a single static pose; MD provides a dynamic ensemble. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 6. **Skipping the differentiation step** in LJ derivation: showing $r_{min} = 2^{1/6}\sigma$ without derivation loses **2 marks**.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Bioinformatics Approaches for Molecular Modeling in Drug Discovery**

- **CADD** = Computer-Aided Drug Design; integrates SBDD + LBDD.
- **SBDD prerequisites**: 3D target structure (PDB/AlphaFold).
- **LBDD prerequisite**: Known active ligands and their bioactivity data.
- **Docking output**: Binding pose (geometry) + score (affinity estimate).
- **MD output**: Time-resolved trajectory of all atoms.
- **Force fields**: AMBER, CHARMM, OPLS, GROMOS.
- **LJ potential**: $E_{LJ} = 4\epsilon[(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^6]$.
- **LJ minimum**: $r_{min} = 2^{1/6}\sigma$; $E_{min} = -\epsilon$.
- **Coulomb's law**: $E_{elec} = q_i q_j / (4\pi\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r r_{ij})$.
- **MD integrator**: Verlet / Velocity Verlet; timestep **2 fs**.
- **MD ensembles**: NVE (microcanonical), NVT (canonical), NPT (isothermal-isobaric).
- **Thermostats**: Berendsen, Nosé-Hoover, Langevin.
- **Barostats**: Berendsen, Parrinello-Rahman.
- **Bond constraints**: SHAKE, LINCS.
- **Periodic boundary conditions**: PBC; cell shape (cubic, truncated octahedron).
- **Solvent models**: TIP3P, SPC, SPC/E (explicit); Generalized Born (implicit).
- **Trajectory analysis metrics**: RMSD, RMSF, $R_g$, hydrogen bond occupancy, SASA.
- **Free energy methods**: MM-PBSA, MM-GBSA, FEP, TI (Thermodynamic Integration).
- **QSAR**: Hansch equation, Free-Wilson analysis, 3D-QSAR (CoMFA, CoMSIA).
- **Pharmacophore**: 3D arrangement of H-bond donor/acceptor, hydrophobic, aromatic, ionic features.
- **ADMET**: Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion, Toxicity.
- **Lipinski's Rule of Five**: MW $\leq 500$, LogP $\leq 5$, HBD $\leq 5$, HBA $\leq 10$.
- **Lead-Like Rule**: MW $\leq 350$, LogP $\leq 3$ (Veber's rules complement).
- **Ligand Efficiency (LE)**: $\Delta G / N_{HA}$; threshold $\leq -0.30$ kcal/mol·atom.
- **Binding affinity relation**: $\Delta G = -RT \ln K_a = RT \ln K_i$.
- **$R = 1.987$ cal/(mol·K) = 1.987 × 10⁻³ kcal/(mol·K)**.
- **Standard physiological conditions**: $T = 300$ K, $P = 1$ atm, pH 7.4.
- **Boltzmann constant in mol units**: $R = N_A k_B$.
- **AlphaFold2**: DeepMind's protein structure prediction tool (2021, Nobel 2024).
- **PDB**: Protein Data Bank; > 200,000 entries (2024).
- **Common docking software**: AutoDock Vina, Glide, GOLD, FlexX, DOCK 6.
- **MD software**: GROMACS, AMBER, NAMD, CHARMM, Desmond, OpenMM.
- **Visualization software**: PyMOL, ChimeraX, VMD.
- **Free energy conventions**: Negative $\Delta G$ = favorable binding.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protein-Protein Interactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT604 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Protein-Protein Interactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Protein-Protein Interactions (PPIs)** refer to the physical contacts established between two or more protein molecules, mediated by **electrostatic forces, hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and van der Waals forces**, which result in a defined biochemical consequence such as signal transduction, transport, gene regulation, or immune response.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PBBBT604, Module 3), PPI studies form the **foundation of systems biology**, where proteins are not viewed in isolation but as nodes in a complex, dynamic interaction network. The **interactome** is defined as the complete set of molecular interactions in a cell, organism, or biological system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PPI is studied at two levels:
> 1. **Pairwise (binary)** interactions – one-to-one
> 2. **Co-complex** interactions – many proteins in a stable assembly
>
> The classical interaction metric is the **dissociation constant $K_d$**, measured in **Molar (M)**, typically ranging from **$10^{-3}$ M to $10^{-12}$ M**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cellular Switchboard"

Think of a cell as a **massive corporate office building**. The employees are proteins. They don't work in isolation; they constantly pass files, give instructions, and collaborate on projects. 

- A **transient interaction** is like two employees briefly meeting at the water cooler to share an idea — short, fast, dynamic.
- A **permanent (obligate) interaction** is like a married couple — they always exist as a unit (e.g., a heterodimer).
- A **signaling cascade** is like a chain of phone calls where each manager calls the next to relay a critical message.
- The **interactome** is the entire org chart of the company.

Just as you would need to map who-talks-to-whom in a company to understand its workflow, bioinformaticians map PPIs to understand cellular behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics in PPI Studies:**
> - **Affinity ($K_d$)**: Strength of binding (lower = stronger)
> - **Stoichiometry**: Number of subunits in a complex
> - **Coverage**: Fraction of the interactome mapped
> - **Precision/Recall**: Accuracy of computational predictions

## 1.3 Course Outcome Alignment

| Course Outcome | Cognitive Level | PPI Topic Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| CO3 | Understand | Classify experimental & computational PPI methods |
| CO4 | Apply | Predict PPIs from sequence/structure features |
| CO5 | Analyze | Analyze PPI networks using graph theory |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Protein-Protein Interactions

### 2.1.1 By Stability & Lifetime

- **Obligate interactions**: Subunits are unstable in isolation; exist only as a complex (e.g., DNA polymerase III holoenzyme).
- **Non-obligate interactions**: Each partner is stable on its own (e.g., trypsin–bovine pancreatic trypsin inhibitor).
- **Transient interactions**: Form and break rapidly, central to signaling (e.g., kinase–substrate, GPCR–G-protein).
- **Permanent interactions**: Effectively irreversible under physiological conditions.

### 2.1.2 By Structural Topology

- **Heterodimer / Homodimer**: Two subunits (different or identical).
- **Cyclically symmetric oligomers**: e.g., ring-shaped helicases.
- **Heterogeneous complexes**: Multiple distinct subunits (e.g., ribosome, proteasome).

### 2.1.3 By Interface Properties

- **Domain-domain interactions** (e.g., SH3–proline-rich motif).
- **Domain-peptide interactions** (e.g., PDZ domain binding C-terminal peptides).
- **Wrapped interactions** (extended interface, large flat surfaces).
- **Surface-surface interactions** (large buried surface area).

## 2.2 Experimental Methods — Logic Steps

### 2.2.1 Yeast Two-Hybrid (Y2H) System

**The Why**: Tests binary interactions *in vivo* using a transcription-based reporter.
**The How**:
1. **Bait protein** fused to **DNA-Binding Domain (DBD)** of GAL4.
2. **Prey protein** fused to **Activation Domain (AD)** of GAL4.
3. Co-transformation into yeast reporter strain.
4. If bait and prey interact → DBD + AD reconstitute → activate **reporter gene (HIS3, LacZ, ADE2)**.
5. Positive colonies grow on selective media.

> [!NOTE]
> **Variants:** Y3H, Mammalian Two-Hybrid, Bacterial Two-Hybrid.

### 2.2.2 Co-Immunoprecipitation (Co-IP)

- Uses an antibody against a target ("bait") protein to pull it down along with physically associated partners from a cell lysate.
- Detected via **SDS-PAGE + Western Blot** or **mass spectrometry**.

### 2.2.3 Tandem Affinity Purification (TAP)

- Two-step purification with two different tags (e.g., Protein A + Calmodulin-Binding Peptide).
- Higher specificity, lower background than single-step Co-IP.

### 2.2.4 BiFC (Bimolecular Fluorescence Complementation)

- Two non-fluorescent fragments of a fluorescent protein (e.g., YFP) are fused to candidate proteins.
- Fluorescence is reconstituted **only** upon interaction.

### 2.2.5 FRET (Förster Resonance Energy Transfer)

- Energy transfer from donor fluorophore to acceptor occurs only when they are within **1–10 nm**.
- Used to measure **real-time interaction kinetics** in living cells.

### 2.2.6 Cryo-EM & X-ray Crystallography

- Provide **atomic-resolution 3D structures** of complexes.
- Gold standard for validating predicted interactions and mapping interface residues.

## 2.3 Computational Methods — Logic Steps

### 2.3.1 Sequence-Based Methods

| Method | Core Idea |
|---|---|
| **Interolog mapping** | Transfer interaction from ortholog in another species |
| **Gene fusion (Rosetta Stone)** | If two proteins are fused in one organism, they likely interact |
| **Phylogenetic profile** | Proteins with correlated presence/absence across genomes are functionally linked |
| **Gene neighborhood** | Operon-based co-localization in prokaryotes |
| **Mirror tree** | Co-evolution of phylogenetic trees = interaction |
| **Mutual information (MI)** | Co-evolution of sequence positions |

### 2.3.2 Structure-Based Methods

- **Protein docking** (e.g., HADDOCK, ZDOCK, ClusPro): Predict binding mode from 3D structures.
- **Template-based docking**: Use known complex structures as templates.
- **Interface prediction**: Hot-spot residue identification using energetic scoring.

### 2.3.3 Machine Learning–Based Prediction

Features used: amino acid composition, PSSM (Position-Specific Scoring Matrix), hydrophobicity, secondary structure, solvent accessibility.
Classifiers: **SVM, Random Forest, Deep Neural Networks, Graph Neural Networks**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** for KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Bioinformatics.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_d = \dfrac{[A][B]}{[AB]}$ | Dissociation constant | Molar (M) |
| 2 | $\Delta G = -RT \ln K_a$ | Free energy of binding | $K_a = 1/K_d$ |
| 3 | $K_a = \dfrac{1}{K_d}$ | Association constant | M$^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $\theta = \dfrac{r}{R_0}^6 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + (r/R_0)^6}$ | FRET efficiency; $R_0$ = Förster distance (~5 nm) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_{\text{interaction}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{true positives}}}{N_{\text{true positives}} + N_{\text{false positives}}}$ | Precision in PPI prediction | 0 to 1 |
| 6 | $R_{\text{recall}} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Recall / Sensitivity | 0 to 1 |
| 7 | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision & recall | 0 to 1 |
| 8 | $S_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{20} (p_i^k - p_j^k) \ln \dfrac{p_i^k}{p_j^k}$ | Mutual information between sequence positions $i$ and $j$ | Bits |
| 9 | $C(v) = \deg(v)$ | Degree centrality in PPI network | Integer |
| 10 | $BC(v) = \sum_{s \neq v \neq t} \dfrac{\sigma_{st}(v)}{\sigma_{st}}$ | Betweenness centrality | Sum of fractions |
| 11 | $C_C = \dfrac{2E}{N(N-1)}$ | Clustering coefficient (undirected) | 0 to 1 |
| 12 | $\lambda_1$ | Algebraic connectivity of PPI graph | $\geq 0$ |

> **Physical constants used in PPI thermodynamics:**
> - $R = 8.314$ **J mol$^{-1}$ K$^{-1}$** (gas constant)
> - $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ **mol$^{-1}$** (Avogadro's number)
> - $T = 310$ **K** (physiological, 37 °C)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

PPI studies are the cornerstone of:
- **Drug discovery** — designing interface inhibitors (e.g., venetoclax targets BCL-2/BAX interaction).
- **Disease biomarker discovery** — corrupted PPIs underpin cancer, neurodegeneration.
- **Synthetic biology** — engineering synthetic cellular circuits and logic gates.
- **Vaccine design** — mapping viral surface protein interactions.
- **Agricultural biotechnology** — engineering stress-resistant crops by modulating PPIs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Free Energy of PPI Binding

We start from the thermodynamic relationship between equilibrium constant and Gibbs free energy.

For a binding reaction $A + B \rightleftharpoons AB$ at equilibrium:

$$K_a = \dfrac{[AB]}{[A][B]}$$

The fundamental thermodynamic relation is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ and $Q = K_a$ (the reaction quotient equals the equilibrium constant). Therefore:

$$0 = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln K_a$$

Solving for $\Delta G^{\circ}$:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_a$$

Since $K_a = \dfrac{1}{K_d}$, we substitute:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln \left( \dfrac{1}{K_d} \right) = -RT \cdot (-\ln K_d) = RT \ln K_d$$

Thus the final compact forms are:

$$\boxed{\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_a = RT \ln K_d}$$

**Conversion logic summary:**

1. Started with general thermodynamic identity $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$.
2. Applied equilibrium condition $\Delta G = 0$ and $Q \to K_a$.
3. Algebraically isolated $\Delta G^{\circ}$.
4. Expressed result in terms of $K_d$ (the experimentally measured quantity).

### 3.1.1 Numerical Worked Example

A protein–protein interaction has $K_d = 10^{-9}$ M at $T = 298$ K.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^{\circ} &= RT \ln K_d \\
&= (8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1})(298 \text{ K}) \cdot \ln(10^{-9}) \\
&= 2477.572 \text{ J/mol} \cdot (-20.723) \\
&= -51{,}340 \text{ J/mol} \\
&\approx -51.3 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: The strongly negative $\Delta G^{\circ}$ confirms a thermodynamically stable, spontaneous binding event.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mutual Information Score for Co-evolution

The mutual information between two sequence positions $i$ and $j$ in a multiple sequence alignment (MSA) of homologs is:

$$S_{ij} = \sum_{a=1}^{20} \sum_{b=1}^{20} p(a_i, b_j) \cdot \log_2 \left( \dfrac{p(a_i, b_j)}{p(a_i)\, p(b_j)} \right)$$

where:
- $p(a_i, b_j)$ = joint probability of residue $a$ at position $i$ AND residue $b$ at position $j$.
- $p(a_i)$ = marginal probability of residue $a$ at position $i$.
- $p(b_j)$ = marginal probability of residue $b$ at position $j$.

### 3.2.1 Numerical Worked Example (Toy MSA)

Suppose we have the following aligned fragment from 4 sequences, focusing on positions $i$ and $j$:

| Seq | Pos $i$ | Pos $j$ |
|---|---|---|
| S1 | A | L |
| S2 | A | L |
| S3 | V | P |
| S4 | V | P |

Counts:
- (A, L) = 2
- (V, P) = 2
- All other combinations = 0
- Total = 4

Marginals: $p(A)=2/4=0.5$, $p(V)=0.5$, $p(L)=0.5$, $p(P)=0.5$.

Joint: $p(A,L) = 0.5$, $p(V,P) = 0.5$, others $= 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{ij} &= p(A,L) \log_2 \dfrac{p(A,L)}{p(A)\,p(L)} + p(V,P) \log_2 \dfrac{p(V,P)}{p(V)\,p(P)} \\
&= 0.5 \log_2 \dfrac{0.5}{0.25} + 0.5 \log_2 \dfrac{0.5}{0.25} \\
&= 0.5 \log_2(2) + 0.5 \log_2(2) \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1 + 0.5 \cdot 1 \\
&= 1.0 \text{ bit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: A mutual information of **1.0 bit** is the **maximum possible** (since there are 2 distinct joint states), indicating **perfect co-evolution** at these two positions — strong evidence of a physical or functional coupling, possibly a contact in a protein complex.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of PPI Detection Methods

| Method | Throughput | False Positives | False Negatives | Interaction Type | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Y2H | High | High | High | Binary | Low |
| Co-IP/MS | Low | Low | High | Co-complex | Medium |
| TAP-MS | Low | Low | Medium | Co-complex | Medium |
| BiFC | Medium | Low | High | Binary in vivo | Medium |
| FRET | Low | Low | Low | Live cell binary | High |
| Cryo-EM | Very Low | Very Low | Low | Structural | Very High |
| Phylogenetic profile | Very High | High | Medium | Functional linkage | Very Low |
| Sequence co-evolution | High | Medium | High | Direct contact | Low |
| Structure docking | Low | Medium | High | Binary structural | Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
